AI.tldr: A closed-loop dual-agent framework that unifies perception, social reasoning (via Theory of Mind), and multimodal expression to create socially intelligent digital humans that understand mental states and generate bidirectional, emotion-controllable speaker-listener interactions.
AI.motivation: Current approaches treat dialogue and embodied expression as separate tasks: LLMs lack physical expression, while diffusion-based talking head models lack social cognition. No existing framework integrates cognitive reasoning with multimodal generation for truly socially intelligent digital humans.
AI.method: A closed-loop dual-agent framework with three modules: (1) perception module analyzing multimodal partner behaviors from video, (2) social reasoning module using Theory of Mind to infer hidden mental states with an ensemble selection mechanism, and (3) expression module generating emotion-controllable dual-agent videos (both speaker and listener). A hierarchical Persona-Scenario dataset with psychologically grounded personas and private goals supports evaluation under information asymmetry.
AI.result: Competitive or superior performance on both dialogue quality and video generation metrics. Notably surpasses full-information Script mode on key dialogue dimensions, suggesting mental state inference under uncertainty produces more thoughtful dialogue than unrestricted information access.
AI.conclusion: Explicit mental state inference under information asymmetry can elicit more thoughtful dialogue than full-information settings, validating the value of Theory of Mind reasoning for socially intelligent digital humans.

title: When Tools Fail: Benchmarking Dynamic Replanning and Anomaly Recovery in LLM Agents
summary: Existing benchmarks evaluate Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) in LLMs on idealized ''happy paths'', largely overlooking real-world tool failures. We introduce ToolMaze, a benchmark for dynamic path discovery and error recovery in TIR agents. To separate systematic replanning from blind trial-and-error, ToolMaze adopts a two-dimensional design: DAG-based topological complexity and a $2 \times 2$ taxonomy of tool perturbations (explicit/implicit, transient/permanent). Evaluations show that perturbations degrade performance across nearly all models, with the sharpest drops under implicit semantic failures. Driven by systemic over-trust in corrupted outputs, Perturbation Recovery Rate (PRR) plummets by around 37\% in these scenarios, while complex topologies trap agents in futile trial-and-error loops. Crucially, agentic fault-tolerance improves with model scale $3.66\times$ slower than basic task execution, highlighting dynamic replanning as a distinct bottleneck unaddressed by model scaling or prompting. Data and code are available at https://github.com/Zhudongsheng75/ToolMaze.

AI.tldr: ToolMaze is a new benchmark evaluating how LLMs handle real-world tool failures (not just happy paths) in Tool-Integrated Reasoning. It uses DAG-based topologies and a 2x2 taxonomy of tool perturbations. Key findings: implicit semantic failures cause the steepest performance drops (~37% lower recovery), complex topologies lead to futile trial-and-error loops, and agentic fault-tolerance scales 3.66x slower than basic task execution—revealing dynamic replanning as a distinct bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Existing TIR benchmarks only test idealized happy paths ignoring real-world tool failures, so the authors created ToolMaze to systematically evaluate error recovery and dynamic replanning capabilities.
AI.method: ToolMaze uses a two-dimensional design: (1) DAG-based topological complexity for path discovery, and (2) a 2x2 taxonomy of tool perturbations (explicit/implicit × transient/permanent). Evaluated multiple LLMs on these scenarios.
AI.result: Perturbations degrade performance across nearly all models; implicit semantic failures cause the most severe drops (~37% PRR reduction); complex topologies trap agents in trial-and-error loops; fault-tolerance improvement scales 3.66× slower than basic task execution with model scale.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic replanning for tool error recovery is a distinct bottleneck that cannot be effectively addressed by simply scaling models or improving prompting strategies.

title: Benchmarking Counterfactual Prediction in Epidemic Time Series with Time-Varying Interventions
summary: Deep learning has enabled significant advances in time-series causal inference, yet progress remains constrained by the lack of realistic benchmarks with observable counterfactual outcomes. Existing datasets either rely on real-world observations without ground-truth counterfactuals or on simplified simulations that fail to capture complex causal dynamics. To address this gap, we develop a large-scale benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series under dynamic interventions. Unlike existing benchmarks, it supports static and time-varying treatments, as well as both single-policy and multi-policy intervention settings, enabling evaluation of causal inference methods across a broad range of causal inference scenarios. Leveraging a calibrated agent-based model grounded in real-world demographic, mobility, epidemiological, and policy data, we generate realistic counterfactual trajectories across more than 150 U.S. counties. Using this benchmark, we evaluate widely used and state-of-the-art causal inference methods, revealing substantial performance differences and highlighting the challenges of realistic time-series causal reasoning.

AI.tldr: A large-scale benchmark for counterfactual prediction in epidemic time series under dynamic interventions, supporting static/time-varying treatments and single/multi-policy settings, generated from a calibrated agent-based model using real-world data across 150+ U.S. counties.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for time-series causal inference lack either ground-truth counterfactuals (real-world data) or realistic complexity (simplified simulations). There is a need for realistic benchmarks with observable counterfactuals to advance causal inference methods.
AI.method: The authors build a calibrated agent-based model grounded in real-world demographic, mobility, epidemiological, and policy data from over 150 U.S. counties to generate realistic counterfactual epidemic trajectories under dynamic interventions.
AI.result: Evaluation of widely used and state-of-the-art causal inference methods on this benchmark reveals substantial performance differences and highlights challenges in realistic time-series causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed benchmark fills a critical gap in time-series causal inference evaluation, and the results underscore the difficulty of accurate counterfactual prediction in realistic epidemic settings.

title: Self-Learning Expression Deformations for Data-Efficient Gaussian Avatars
summary: Modeling dynamic facial expressions using 3D Gaussian representations remains challenging due to their unstructured nature. Conventional Gaussian avatar pipelines require extensive multiview and sequential expression data, limiting scalability and accessibility. In this work, we introduce Self-Adaptive Gaussian Expression (SAGE), a framework for self-learning expression-induced Gaussian deformations that enables high-fidelity, animatable avatars from minimal input data. Our method jointly optimizes 2D Gaussian surfels and a Signed Distance Field (SDF) to enforce compact, surface-aligned Gaussian distributions, while a self-supervised expression learning phase replaces long training sequences with geometric and appearance consistency constraints. This design allows flexible deployment across multiple reconstruction regimes: in the multiview setting, only a single frame (timestep) is required instead of thousands; in the monocular setting, only head rotations are needed without expression sequences; and in the one-shot setting, no pretraining or priors are necessary. Experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves reconstruction and animation quality comparable to state-of-the-art methods, while reducing data requirements by several orders of magnitude. Our results highlight the potential of self-supervised Gaussian deformation learning as a step toward accessible, data-efficient avatar creation.

AI.tldr: SAGE is a self-supervised framework that learns expression-induced Gaussian deformations from minimal input (single frame, monocular, or one-shot) to create high-fidelity, animatable 3D avatars, reducing data requirements by orders of magnitude while achieving state-of-the-art quality.
AI.motivation: Existing Gaussian avatar methods struggle with modeling dynamic facial expressions due to unstructured 3D Gaussian representations and require extensive multiview and sequential expression data, limiting scalability and accessibility.
AI.method: Jointly optimizes 2D Gaussian surfels with a Signed Distance Field (SDF) for compact surface alignment, plus a self-supervised expression learning phase using geometric and appearance consistency constraints instead of long training sequences.
AI.result: Achieves reconstruction and animation quality comparable to state-of-the-art methods while reducing data requirements by several orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised Gaussian deformation learning is a promising step toward more accessible and data-efficient avatar creation.

title: Statistical Priors for Implicit Preferences: Decoupling Skill Selection as a Local Harness in Personal Agents
summary: As Large Language Model (LLM) capabilities advance, locally deployed personal agents relying on API-based remote models and external skills have emerged as a novel paradigm. With the rapid expansion of available skills, enabling personal agents to learn and adapt to implicit user preferences becomes a critical challenge. However, local deployment constraints preclude complex centralized selection algorithms, creating an urgent need for a lightweight local preference harness. This paper explores the implementation of such a harness through a novel architecture that strictly decouples statistical preference learning from semantic intent parsing. Specifically, we leverage localized statistical results to influence and modulate the selection decisions of the remote LLM. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our decoupled approach achieves the lowest cumulative regret and highest test accuracy, significantly outperforming traditional memory-augmented agents.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a lightweight, locally-deployed preference learning architecture that decouples statistical preference tracking from semantic intent parsing, enabling personal agents to adapt to implicit user preferences without complex centralized algorithms, achieving superior accuracy and lower regret than traditional memory-augmented agents.
AI.motivation: As LLM-powered personal agents increasingly rely on API-based remote models and a growing number of external skills, learning implicit user preferences becomes critical. However, local deployment constraints prevent using complex centralized selection algorithms, creating an urgent need for a lightweight local preference harness.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel architecture that strictly decouples statistical preference learning from semantic intent parsing, using localized statistical results to influence and modulate the remote LLM's selection decisions.
AI.result: The decoupled approach achieves the lowest cumulative regret and highest test accuracy, significantly outperforming traditional memory-augmented agents.
AI.conclusion: A lightweight, decoupled architecture for local preference learning can effectively adapt to implicit user preferences under local deployment constraints, outperforming more complex memory-based approaches.

title: MolE-RAG: Molecular Structure-Enhanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Chemistry
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise for molecular property prediction, but their ability to reason over chemical structures remains limited, as molecular representations such as SMILES differ substantially from the natural language on which LLMs are primarily trained. To bridge this semantic and chemical knowledge gap, we propose MolE-RAG, a training-free, molecule-centric retrieval-augmented generation framework for LLM-based molecular property prediction. MolE-RAG augments each prediction with three complementary sources of inference-time context: retrieved chemistry literature, molecule-specific information including compound synonyms, identifiers, functional group annotations, and physicochemical descriptors, and structurally similar molecules retrieved from the training set. We evaluate MolE-RAG across nine molecular property prediction tasks using proprietary, chemistry-specialized, and open-source LLMs. Across general-purpose LLMs, MolE-RAG improves ROC-AUC by up to 28 percentage points on classification tasks and reduces regression RMSE by up to 67% relative to a SMILES-only baseline. We further find that the utility of each context source varies across models and tasks, with different models benefiting most from textual retrieval, molecular context, or structural retrieval. These results suggest that molecule-centric retrieval can improve LLM-based molecular property prediction without model fine-tuning while providing a flexible framework for integrating heterogeneous chemical knowledge at inference time.

AI.tldr: MolE-RAG is a training-free retrieval-augmented generation framework that improves LLM-based molecular property prediction by augmenting SMILES inputs with chemistry literature, molecular descriptors, and structurally similar molecules, achieving up to 28% ROC-AUC improvement and 67% RMSE reduction.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with molecular property prediction because molecular representations like SMILES differ substantially from natural language. Existing methods lack chemical reasoning capabilities and often require fine-tuning.
AI.method: MolE-RAG augments inference with three complementary context sources: (1) retrieved chemistry literature, (2) molecule-specific information (synonyms, identifiers, functional groups, physicochemical descriptors), and (3) structurally similar molecules from the training set. It is a training-free framework compatible with any LLM.
AI.result: Across nine molecular property prediction tasks, MolE-RAG improves ROC-AUC by up to 28 percentage points on classification and reduces RMSE by up to 67% on regression. Different context sources benefit different models and tasks variably.
AI.conclusion: Molecule-centric retrieval augmentation can significantly improve LLM-based molecular property prediction without fine-tuning, offering a flexible framework for integrating heterogeneous chemical knowledge at inference time.

title: T-FunS3D: Task-Driven Hierarchical Open-Vocabulary 3D Functionality Segmentation
summary: Open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation enables robots to localize functional object components in 3D scenes. It is a challenging task that requires spatial understanding and task interpretation. Current open-vocabulary 3D segmentation methods primarily focus on object-level recognition, while scene-wide part segmentation methods attempt to segment the entire scene exhaustively, making them highly resource-intensive and time consuming. Balancing segmentation performance in terms of granularity, accuracy, and speed remains a challenge. As one step towards alleviating this, we introduce T-FunS3D, a task-driven hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation method that provides actionable perception for robotic applications. Our method takes as input the 3D point cloud and posed RGB-D images of an indoor scene. We construct an open-vocabulary scene graph by extracting instances and their visual embeddings in the environment. Given a task description, T-FunS3D identifies the most relevant instances in the scene graph and locates their functional components leveraging a vision-language model. Experiments on the SceneFun3D dataset demonstrate that T-FunS3D is comparable to state-of-the-art in open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation, while achieving faster runtime and reduced memory usage.

AI.tldr: T-FunS3D is a task-driven, hierarchical open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation method that achieves fast and efficient localization of functional object components by building an open-vocabulary scene graph and leveraging a vision-language model, balancing granularity, accuracy, and speed.
AI.motivation: Current open-vocabulary 3D segmentation methods focus on object-level recognition or exhaustive scene-wide segmentation, which are resource-intensive and time-consuming. There is a need for a method that balances granularity, accuracy, and speed for robotic applications requiring actionable perception.
AI.method: T-FunS3D takes 3D point clouds and posed RGB-D images as input, constructs an open-vocabulary scene graph by extracting instances and their visual embeddings, then uses a task description to identify relevant instances and locate functional components via a vision-language model.
AI.result: Experiments on the SceneFun3D dataset show T-FunS3D achieves comparable performance to state-of-the-art methods while achieving faster runtime and reduced memory usage.
AI.conclusion: T-FunS3D provides an efficient, task-driven solution for open-vocabulary 3D functionality segmentation, making it suitable for robotic applications where speed and resource efficiency are critical.

title: CamFlow+: Hybrid Motion Bases for 2D Camera Motion Estimation with Stabilization Applications
summary: Estimating 2D camera motion is fundamental to computer vision and computational photography. Existing homography-based methods work well for planar scenes or pure rotation, but struggle with camera translation, depth variation, and local parallax; local homography and mesh-based models improve flexibility but still rely on piecewise planar assumptions. We introduce CamFlow+, a hybrid-basis framework that represents 2D camera motion directly in dense-flow space. CamFlow+ combines homography-derived physical bases, stochastic bases sampled from homography flows, and depth-translational bases derived from depth and camera intrinsics, relaxing the single-plane constraint while preserving camera-motion regularity. A depth-aware smoothness term further regularizes translation-induced parallax in continuous-depth regions while preserving motion changes near depth boundaries. We evaluate CamFlow+ on GHOF-Cam, a camera-motion benchmark that masks out dynamic objects and ill-posed occlusion regions in an optical-flow benchmark to isolate camera-induced motion. Experiments show that CamFlow+ improves sparse and dense camera-motion estimation. In digital video stabilization, CamFlow+ also improves global and local stability, achieving the best top-1 preference rate in a blind user study. Code and datasets will be available on the project page: https://lhaippp.github.io/CamFlow+.

AI.tldr: CamFlow+ introduces a hybrid-basis framework for 2D camera motion estimation that combines homography bases, stochastic bases, and depth-translational bases to handle translation, depth variation, and parallax better than traditional homography methods, achieving improved results on camera motion estimation and video stabilization.
AI.motivation: Existing homography-based methods work for planar scenes or pure rotation but fail under camera translation, depth variation, and local parallax; local homography and mesh models still rely on piecewise planar assumptions.
AI.method: A hybrid-basis framework (CamFlow+) combining homography-derived physical bases, stochastic bases from homography flows, and depth-translational bases with a depth-aware smoothness term for regularization.
AI.result: Improves sparse/dense camera-motion estimation on GHOF-Cam benchmark; achieves best top-1 preference rate in blind user study for video stabilization.
AI.conclusion: CamFlow+ effectively relaxes single-plane constraints while preserving camera-motion regularity, offering a more flexible and accurate approach for camera motion estimation and downstream tasks like video stabilization.

title: Agentic Molecular Recovery via Molecule-Aware Exploration
summary: Text-guided molecular generation with LLMs often yields invalid SMILES. We argue that invalid drafts should be addressed through a shift from validity-oriented repair to identity-preserving molecular recovery: the objective is not only to restore chemical validity, but also to preserve target-relevant structural cues and recover the molecular identity implied by the description. This perspective reveals the limitations of existing correction strategies. Post-hoc repair can recover validity while distorting key structures, LLM-only correction can introduce unintended global drift, and generic agentic correction remains constrained by greedy single-candidate trajectories even when equipped with executable RDKit edit tools. To address these limitations, we propose AMREC, which couples molecule-aware mismatch tracking with expanded candidate exploration and trajectory-level selection. On invalid ChEBI-20 drafts from three backbone models, AMREC achieves the strongest overall recovery profile across structural, exact-match, and string-level metrics.

AI.tldr: AMREC is a molecular correction method that recovers valid SMILES from invalid LLM-generated drafts by preserving structural identity, using mismatch tracking and trajectory-level selection, outperforming existing repair strategies.
AI.motivation: Existing correction strategies (post-hoc repair, LLM-only correction, generic agentic correction) either distort key structures, introduce global drift, or are limited by greedy single-candidate trajectories, failing to preserve molecular identity.
AI.method: AMREC couples molecule-aware mismatch tracking with expanded candidate exploration and trajectory-level selection to recover valid, identity-preserving molecules from invalid SMILES drafts.
AI.result: On invalid ChEBI-20 drafts from three backbone models, AMREC achieves the strongest overall recovery profile across structural, exact-match, and string-level metrics.
AI.conclusion: A shift from validity-oriented repair to identity-preserving molecular recovery is necessary, and AMREC offers an effective solution.

title: Revisiting Prototype Rehearsal for Exemplar-Free Continual Learning: Manifold-Aware Boundary Sampling with Adaptive Class-Balanced Loss
comment: Published in CVPR 2026 Findings. 10 pages, 6 figures. CVF version: https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2026F/html/Xu_Revisiting_Prototype_Rehearsal_for_Exemplar-Free_Continual_Learning_Manifold-Aware_Boundary_Sampling_CVPRF_2026_paper.html. Code: https://github.com/HXuSz11/ACB_CEOS_CVPR2026_Findings
summary: Exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL) aims to acquire new classes over time without storing raw data. Historically, prototype rehearsal, which samples around stored class prototypes and mixes them with current-task data, has been a popular strategy to reduce catastrophic forgetting. However, recent drift-compensation methods that explicitly realign prototypes in the evolving feature space consistently outperform prototype-based rehearsal, raising the question of whether rehearsal itself is fundamentally limited. We argue that the performance gap stems not from the idea of prototype rehearsal per se, but from how it is typically instantiated: existing approaches treat prototypes as isolated class summaries that ignore information from nearby enemy classes, and fail to correct the emerging class imbalance between a handful of synthetic old-class samples and hundreds of real instances from newly introduced classes. Building on this hypothesis, we revisit prototype rehearsal and propose a manifold-aware variant that restores its competitiveness in EFCIL. First, we introduce Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling, which interpolates each old-class prototype toward its nearest enemy features from new classes, generating boundary-aware rehearsal samples that better follow the underlying data manifold while preserving inter-class separation. Second, we design an Adaptive Class-Balanced loss that performs time-based class weighting, amplifying gradients from older prototypes when they are most informative and gradually annealing their influence as richer supervision from later tasks accumulates. Together, these components turn prototype rehearsal into a drift-resilient, imbalance-aware mechanism that closes, and often reverses, the gap to recent drift-compensation methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple EFCIL benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper revisits prototype rehearsal for exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL), proposing manifold-aware modifications—Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling and Adaptive Class-Balanced loss—that close the performance gap with drift-compensation methods and achieve state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: To understand why prototype rehearsal underperforms compared to drift-compensation methods in EFCIL, and to determine whether this gap is inherent to rehearsal or due to poor implementation choices.
AI.method: (1) Constrained Expansive Over-Sampling: interpolates old-class prototypes toward nearest enemy features from new classes to generate boundary-aware, manifold-following rehearsal samples. (2) Adaptive Class-Balanced loss: time-based class weighting that amplifies old-prototype gradients early and anneals them later to mitigate class imbalance.
AI.result: The proposed method closes and often reverses the performance gap to recent drift-compensation methods, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple EFCIL benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Prototype rehearsal is not fundamentally limited; its prior poor performance stems from treating prototypes as isolated summaries and failing to address class imbalance. The proposed manifold-aware, imbalance-correcting variant restores competitiveness in EFCIL.

title: PLAN-S: Bridging Planning with Latent Style Dynamics for Autonomous Driving World Models
summary: Latent world models (LWMs) have strengthened end-to-end autonomous driving by forecasting compact scene dynamics for downstream planning. However, existing LWM-based planners usually generate trajectories directly from entangled latent representations. This compact latent-to-planner pathway lacks explicit modeling of risk, drivability, and diverse style preferences, making driving-style dynamics difficult to supervise, inspect, or modulate before a final trajectory is selected. We propose PLAN-S (PLANning with latent Style dynamics), a planner-facing bridge that addresses this compactness-controllability dilemma by decoding a style-conditioned, four-channel semantic cost map from the latent representation. The cost map is conditioned on ego state and driving style and is consumed up-stream of the planning decision through two host-side interfaces: attention-level fusion for regression planners and reward-level fusion for anchor-score planners. We validate PLAN-S on two architecturally distinct hosts, ResWorld on nuScenes and WoTE on NAVSIM, while keeping the host backbones frozen to isolate the contribution of the proposed bridge. On nuScenes, PLAN-S reduces L2 at every horizon over the baseline, with 0.55 m average L2 and a 42% relative reduction in the 3 s collision rate. On NAVSIM, the rule-cost variant reaches 89.4 Predictive Driver Model Score (PDMS), while the learned cost variant provides complementary gains on baseline-challenging scenes. Ablations show that the cost pathway contributes most directly to safer trajectory selection. Qualitative results further show that PLAN-S can produce diverse cost maps, with spatially consistent variations aligned to different driving styles.

AI.tldr: PLAN-S bridges the compactness-controllability dilemma in latent world model-based autonomous driving by decoding a style-conditioned semantic cost map from latent representations, enabling risk-aware and style-modulated trajectory selection without modifying host planners.
AI.motivation: Existing LWM-based planners generate trajectories directly from entangled latent representations, lacking explicit modeling of risk, drivability, and driving style preferences, making it difficult to supervise, inspect, or modulate driving dynamics before trajectory selection.
AI.method: PLAN-S decodes a style-conditioned four-channel semantic cost map from latent representation, conditioned on ego state and driving style. It integrates via attention-level fusion (regression planners) and reward-level fusion (anchor-score planners), keeping host backbones frozen.
AI.result: 0.55m avg L2 with 42% relative reduction in 3s collision rate on nuScenes (ResWorld). 89.4 PDMS on NAVSIM (WoTE). Cost pathway contributes most directly to safer trajectory selection. Diverse, style-consistent cost maps produced.
AI.conclusion: PLAN-S effectively adds interpretability and controllability to LWM planners without modifying host architectures, improving safety and enabling driving style modulation.

title: Unveiling the Unknown: Open Vocabulary Object Detection with Scene Graphs
summary: Open-vocabulary object detection seeks to identify novel object categories that were not part of the training data. Many knowledge distillation-based approaches have shown promising performance by transferring knowledge from pre-trained vision-language models to object detection. However, these methods often overlook structured, image-specific relationships between objects, such as interactions and spatial arrangements. This oversight can significantly restrict the effectiveness of detecting novel categories. To address this issue, we propose a Scene-guided Relational Modeling detection framework. This framework utilizes scene graphs to capture structured semantic and spatial relationships between candidate regions and their contextual objects. It explicitly models interactions among neighboring regions and incorporates a Relation Attention Module to implicitly amplify the key relational cues extracted from the scene graph. Furthermore, we present a scene-based textual alignment branch that distills category knowledge from captions to guide relational alignment. This approach facilitates a seamless integration of visual relations with semantic information for enhanced detection performance. Comprehensive experiments show that our model achieves superior performance compared to other OVOD methods, improving the AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Scene-guided Relational Modeling framework (SRM) for open-vocabulary object detection that uses scene graphs to capture structured semantic and spatial relationships between objects, improving novel category detection on COCO and LVIS.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge distillation-based OVOD methods overlook structured, image-specific relationships (interactions and spatial arrangements) between objects, limiting detection of novel categories.
AI.method: (1) Scene graph to capture structured semantic & spatial relationships; (2) Relation Attention Module to amplify relational cues; (3) Scene-based textual alignment branch that distills category knowledge from captions.
AI.result: Superior performance compared to other OVOD methods, improving AP for novel categories on COCO and LVIS datasets.
AI.conclusion: Modeling explicit relational information through scene graphs and relation attention significantly enhances open-vocabulary object detection performance.

title: Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents: A Lightweight Framework for Measuring Behavioral Patterns
summary: AI agents are commonly evaluated using task success, reward, latency, and cost. These metrics are useful, but they often miss important aspects of agent behavior: whether an agent explores too much, repeats itself too rigidly, uses tools effectively, reduces uncertainty over time, or remains robust across repeated runs. This paper proposes Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents (EEA), a lightweight framework for measuring agent behavior through entropy. Rather than treating intelligence as only final task completion, EEA studies the structure of the agents decision process. The framework introduces action entropy, trajectory entropy, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, and robustness entropy. These metrics are intended to complement, not replace, traditional evaluation methods. We also present a practical Python implementation designed to integrate with agent frameworks such as LangChain, Google ADK, custom agent loops, and stored observability traces.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Entropy-Based Evaluation of AI Agents (EEA), a lightweight framework that uses entropy metrics (action entropy, trajectory entropy, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, robustness entropy) to measure agent behavior beyond traditional task success and cost metrics, with a practical Python implementation for integration with major agent frameworks.
AI.motivation: Traditional evaluation metrics (task success, reward, latency, cost) miss important behavioral aspects of AI agents such as over-exploration, rigid repetition, tool usage effectiveness, uncertainty reduction, and cross-run robustness.
AI.method: The paper introduces EEA, a framework with six entropy-based metrics: action entropy, trajectory entropy, tool entropy, information gain, exploration efficiency, and robustness entropy, along with a Python implementation for integration with LangChain, Google ADK, custom agent loops, and observability traces.
AI.result: The framework provides measurable insights into agent decision process structure rather than just final task completion outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-based metrics complement traditional evaluation by capturing behavioral aspects of agent performance, offering a more holistic view of agent intelligence.

title: Intercomparison of Machine Learning Algorithms for Remote Sensing-based In-season Crop Mapping
summary: In-season crop type mapping is critical for food security in the face of increasingly extreme climate-related threats to crops. Currently, the USDA Cropland Data Layer provides crop type labels at 30m resolution and is available the February after harvest, but no product exists that maps crop types before harvest with satisfactory accuracy that would allow emergency managers to respond to crop threats in near real time. Furthermore, the relative advantages of a wide range of algorithms have not been evaluated in a way that accounts for interannual variability, until this study. Here, Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel surface reflectance imagery time series and crop rotation history information are combined to map corn in Iowa and almonds in California at 30m resolution accurately by early June in unseen years, with robust quantification of uncertainty due to phenology and crop distribution. Thousands of model configurations across ten machine learning algorithms were compared using a year-wise cross-validation and a suite of metrics. Hyperparameter search revealed Support Vector Machines to be the most successful algorithm overall, with a mean F1 score of 0.74 (0.59) across five unseen validation years for almonds by early June in California (corn by early June in Iowa). Interannual variation was a large source of uncertainty, but patterns showed the potential to further improve performance with ensemble approaches or ancillary data. Future work may extend these methods to include multiclass maps of all crop types, CONUS-wide maps, and in-season crop yield forecasting.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that combining HLS satellite imagery with crop rotation history enables accurate 30m-resolution mapping of corn in Iowa and almonds in California by early June, with SVM as the best-performing algorithm (F1=0.74 for almonds, 0.59 for corn) across unseen years.
AI.motivation: No existing product maps crop types before harvest with satisfactory accuracy for near-real-time emergency response to climate-related threats, and previous algorithm evaluations failed to account for interannual variability.
AI.method: Combined Harmonized Landsat-Sentinel (HLS) surface reflectance time series with crop rotation history; compared thousands of model configurations across ten ML algorithms using year-wise cross-validation.
AI.result: SVM achieved mean F1 of 0.74 for almonds in California and 0.59 for corn in Iowa by early June across five unseen validation years. Interannual variation was a major uncertainty source.
AI.conclusion: In-season crop type mapping at 30m is feasible by early June; ensemble methods or ancillary data could further improve performance. Future work includes multiclass maps, CONUS-wide coverage, and yield forecasting.

title: Synthetic Data Generation and Vision-based Wrinkle and Keypoint Detection for Bimanual Cloth Manipulation
summary: Robotic manipulation of textiles remains challenging because continuous deformation and self-occlusions hinder the robust visual perception required to estimate the cloth's state. To address the lack of annotated real-world data, we developed a Blender-based synthetic pipeline exporting auto-annotated keypoints, and combined manually labeled renders with real-world data to train a wrinkle detector. We present a perception framework integrating a CNN for permutation-invariant keypoint detection and a YOLOv8-OpenCV pipeline to extract grasping points from structural wrinkles. A proposed bimanual algorithm uses this system to stretch fully folded garments via wrinkles, transitioning to keypoint-based ironing once corners emerge. The keypoint model achieves a Mean Position Error (MPE) of 1.7615 pixels. The perception system transfers to physical fabrics without fine-tuning, outperforming baselines that fail in high-occlusion states or yield false positives on severe folds.

AI.tldr: A Blender-based synthetic data pipeline trains a perception system for robotic textile manipulation, combining CNN-based keypoint detection and YOLOv8-OpenCV wrinkle grasping, enabling a bimanual robot to stretch and iron folded garments.
AI.motivation: Robotic manipulation of textiles is difficult due to continuous fabric deformation and self-occlusions that hinder robust visual state estimation, compounded by a lack of annotated real-world data.
AI.method: Developed a Blender-based synthetic pipeline for auto-annotated keypoints; trained a CNN for permutation-invariant keypoint detection and a YOLOv8-OpenCV pipeline for extracting grasping points from structural wrinkles; designed a bimanual algorithm that stretches folded garments via wrinkles then transitions to keypoint-based ironing.
AI.result: Keypoint model achieves 1.7615 pixels Mean Position Error; perception system transfers to real fabrics without fine-tuning, outperforming baselines that fail under high occlusion or produce false positives on severe folds.
AI.conclusion: The synthetic-to-real transfer approach successfully enables robust cloth state perception and bimanual manipulation, suggesting that wrinkle-based grasping combined with keypoint detection is effective for automated textile handling.

title: MemoryCard: Topic-Aware Multi-Modal Clue Compression for Long-Video Question Answering
summary: Long-video question answering remains challenging for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), as answer-relevant evidence is often sparse, transient, and temporally dispersed across lengthy video contexts. Existing frame-centric approaches improve efficiency through uniform sampling, query-aware frame selection, visual-token compression, and adaptive resolution strategies. However, they still rely on isolated and fragmented frames as the fundamental evidence units, limiting VLMs' ability to effectively capture coherent event-level semantics. To address this limitation, we propose MemoryCard, a video-memory-based augmentation framework that organizes long videos into self-contained Memory Cards. Specifically, MemoryCard first performs a self-reading process over videos and aligned utterances to segment the video into semantically coherent units, each corresponding to a distinct topic or event. For each unit, it generates an event-level video gist and selects representative visual moments, which are then rendered into unified Memory Cards for retrieval and question answering. Experimental results demonstrate that MemoryCard consistently improves long-video QA performance under comparable visual-token budgets, achieving up to a 21.8% relative improvement in accuracy. All code is available at https://github.com/NEUIR/MemoryCard.

AI.tldr: MemoryCard is a video-memory-based augmentation framework that organizes long videos into self-contained Memory Cards (event-level units with gist and visual moments), improving long-video QA accuracy by up to 21.8% over frame-centric approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing frame-centric approaches rely on isolated, fragmented frames as evidence units, limiting VLMs' ability to capture coherent event-level semantics in long videos.
AI.method: MemoryCard performs self-reading over videos and aligned utterances to segment the video into semantically coherent units (topics/events), generates event-level video gists and selects representative visual moments for each unit, then renders them into unified Memory Cards for retrieval and QA.
AI.result: Consistently improves long-video QA performance under comparable visual-token budgets, achieving up to 21.8% relative improvement in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Organizing long videos into structured, event-level Memory Cards is more effective than fragmented frame-centric methods for long-video question answering.

title: QCFuse: Query-Aware Cache Fusion via Compressed View for Efficient RAG Serving
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves large language model (LLM) answer quality by grounding generation in external evidence, but processing retrieved contexts makes the prefill stage a dominant serving cost. RAG cache fusion reduces this cost by reusing precomputed key-value (KV) caches for retrieved chunks and selectively recomputing tokens under the current prompt. Existing selectors, however, face a dilemma between quality and efficiency: fast query-agnostic or final-layer query-to-context selectors can miss request-relevant evidence, whereas full-view query-aware selectors require broad context and layer visibility before recomputation and therefore stall the layer-wise cache-fusion pipeline. We present QCFuse, a compressed-view query-aware selector for RAG cache fusion. QCFuse uses chunk-anchor query probing to condition user-query states on compact per-chunk anchors and critical-layer profiling to identify recomputation tokens without all-layer inspection. We implement QCFuse in SGLang and evaluate it on four open-weight LLMs across six datasets. QCFuse reaches full-prefill-level quality. At matched quality, QCFuse achieves an average prefill-time speedup of 1.7x over full prefill and 1.5x over ProphetKV, the strongest quality-preserving baseline.

AI.tldr: QCFuse is a compressed-view query-aware selector for RAG cache fusion that uses chunk-anchor query probing and critical-layer profiling to achieve high-quality token selection for KV cache reuse, delivering 1.7x speedup over full prefill and 1.5x over ProphetKV at matched quality.
AI.motivation: Existing selectors for RAG cache fusion face a dilemma between quality and efficiency: fast query-agnostic selectors miss relevant evidence, while full-view query-aware selectors require broad context inspection before recomputation, stalling the pipeline.
AI.method: QCFuse introduces two key techniques: (1) chunk-anchor query probing, which conditions user-query states on compact per-chunk anchors, and (2) critical-layer profiling, which identifies recomputation tokens without inspecting all layers.
AI.result: Implemented in SGLang and evaluated on 4 open-weight LLMs across 6 datasets. QCFuse achieves full-prefill-level quality with 1.7x average prefill-time speedup over full prefill and 1.5x over ProphetKV.
AI.conclusion: QCFuse effectively resolves the quality-efficiency trade-off in RAG cache fusion, delivering both high-quality retrieval and significant speed improvements during the prefill stage.

title: Zero-Copy Semantic Contagion: An In-Memory Streaming Architecture for Evolving Attention Graphs
comment: Accepted to the 2026 ACM SIGMOD Workshop on Data Management for the Modern Financial Systems (FinDS). 10 pages, 4 figures
summary: Per-ticker forecasting models dominate financial time-series work yet remain blind to cross-company propagation: a foundry disruption in Taiwan does not register in a single-asset model until Apple's own price has already moved. To address this limitation, we introduce a heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture that maps cross-company attention as a continuous-time graph driven directly from text. We show that on the ingestion side, a zero-copy Rust edge parses news records in $\sim$100 ns and scans the target equity universe in $\sim$1.2 $μ$s. On the inference end, a multivariate Neural Hawkes Process featuring per-node continuous-time LSTM states and a bilinear latent projection propagates directed excitation, while an adaptive pruning rule bounds the computational cost of dynamic neighborhood updates. Combining these stages, we demonstrate an end-to-end processing latency of $\sim$13 ms per incoming news record on a single commodity CPU. Evaluated on a one-month temporal holdout of the FNSPID corpus (638 articles across 47 tickers), the system delivers a $1.70\times$ precision lift over random at the 90th-percentile next-day return threshold, and $3.36\times$ over a same-sector baseline. Crucially, removing the graph topology collapses precision to zero, confirming that the dynamic attention network is the sole driver of cross-company signal in this architecture.

AI.tldr: A heterogeneous Rust-Python streaming architecture uses continuous-time graph attention driven by news text to capture cross-company stock return propagation, achieving ~13ms latency and significant precision gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: Per-ticker forecasting models ignore cross-company propagation (e.g., a foundry disruption affecting Apple), limiting predictive power. The paper aims to address this blind spot.
AI.method: A Rust-Python streaming system with: (1) zero-copy Rust edge parsing news (~100ns) and scanning equities (~1.2μs); (2) multivariate Neural Hawkes Process with per-node continuous-time LSTM and bilinear latent projection for directed excitation; (3) adaptive pruning for dynamic neighborhood updates.
AI.result: ~13ms end-to-end latency per news record on a single CPU. On FNSPID holdout (638 articles, 47 tickers): 1.70× precision lift over random at 90th-percentile next-day return threshold, 3.36× over same-sector baseline. Removing graph topology collapses precision to zero.
AI.conclusion: The dynamic attention network is the sole driver of cross-company signal; cross-company propagation is essential and cannot be captured by single-asset models.

title: Faithful, Enriched, and Precise: Benchmarking Natural-Science Illustration Generation by T2I models
summary: Scientific illustrations are essential tools for communicating research findings, especially in natural science, where they visualize complex concepts and processes. As Text-to-Image (T2I) models become increasingly capable, researchers have started to use them for scientific illustration generation. However, existing benchmarks often assess outputs at a holistic level, overlooking fine-grained elements, while scientific reasoning ability and output conciseness remain under-quantified. We introduce FEPBench, a benchmark built from carefully selected high-quality scientific illustrations across multiple disciplines and layout types. With the assistance of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and human experts, we provide fine-grained atom set annotations and systematically evaluate T2I models along three dimensions: instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, and semantic precision. Our evaluation further decomposes model performance across visual, textual, relation, and layout elements. Results show that even state-of-the-art (SOTA) closed-source models, such as GPT Image 2 and Nano Banana Pro, still suffer from text-rendering bottlenecks, limited reasoning enrichment, and difficulty balancing generation richness with precision. These findings provide practical guidance for improving and deploying T2I models in scientific illustration generation. Benchmark data, atom set annotations, and evaluation code will be released by us.

AI.tldr: FEPBench is a new benchmark for evaluating Text-to-Image models on scientific illustration generation, providing fine-grained atom set annotations and assessing models across instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, and semantic precision. Results show even SOTA models struggle with text rendering, reasoning, and balancing richness with precision.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks assess T2I outputs at a holistic level, overlooking fine-grained elements, while scientific reasoning ability and output conciseness remain under-quantified. There is a need for a more detailed evaluation framework for scientific illustration generation.
AI.method: The authors built FEPBench from high-quality scientific illustrations across multiple disciplines and layout types, using MLLMs and human experts to provide fine-grained atom set annotations. They evaluate T2I models along three dimensions: instruction faithfulness, reasoning enrichment, and semantic precision, with further decomposition across visual, textual, relation, and layout elements.
AI.result: Even SOTA closed-source models (GPT Image 2, Nano Banana Pro) suffer from text-rendering bottlenecks, limited reasoning enrichment, and difficulty balancing generation richness with precision.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide practical guidance for improving and deploying T2I models in scientific illustration generation. Benchmark data and code will be released.

title: Retry Policy Gradients in Continuous Action Spaces
summary: Retry-based objectives such as pass@K and max@K optimize the best return obtained from multiple sampled trajectories, and recent work has shown that they can promote exploration without explicit exploration bonuses. In discrete action spaces, ReMax was shown to do so by adapting to return uncertainty. In this work, we introduce pathwise derivative estimators for retry objectives and use them to extend ReMax to continuous action spaces. We study the resulting learning dynamics and show that, even with deterministic rewards, ReMax can encourage stochastic exploration by reshaping the policy-gradient landscape. In particular, it alters gradients both in direction, biasing updates toward higher policy entropy, and in magnitude, damping gradients and slowing convergence. We further show that Adam's adaptive normalization can mitigate this damping, depending on its numerical stabilization parameter. Empirically, we instantiate this objective as ReMax Actor-Critic (ReMAC), an off-policy actor--critic algorithm that optimizes the ReMax objective using a pathwise derivative estimator. Our experiments show that ReMAC can promote higher policy entropy without entropy regularization and achieves performance comparable to SAC.

AI.tldr: This paper extends ReMax (a retry-based objective) to continuous action spaces using pathwise derivative estimators, studies how it reshapes policy-gradient landscapes to encourage exploration, and proposes ReMAC (an off-policy actor-critic algorithm) that achieves performance comparable to SAC without explicit entropy regularization.
AI.motivation: Retry-based objectives like pass@K and max@K can promote exploration without explicit bonuses, but existing work (ReMax) only handles discrete action spaces. The authors aim to extend this to continuous action spaces and understand the underlying learning dynamics.
AI.method: The paper introduces pathwise derivative estimators for retry objectives to extend ReMax to continuous action spaces. It analyzes how ReMax reshapes policy gradients (biasing toward higher entropy, damping magnitudes) and proposes ReMAC, an off-policy actor-critic algorithm using this estimator.
AI.result: ReMax encourages stochastic exploration in continuous action spaces even with deterministic rewards. Adam's adaptive normalization can mitigate gradient damping depending on its stabilization parameter. Empirically, ReMAC achieves higher policy entropy without entropy regularization and performs comparably to SAC.
AI.conclusion: Retry-based objectives are effective for exploration in both discrete and continuous action spaces, and ReMAC provides a practical algorithm that matches state-of-the-art off-policy methods without requiring explicit entropy bonuses.

title: Retrospective Harness Optimization: Improving LLM Agents via Self-Preference over Trajectory Rollouts
summary: AI agents rely on a harness of skills, tools, and workflows to solve complex problems. Continually improving this harness is essential for adapting to new tasks. However, existing optimization methods typically require ground-truth validation sets, yet such labeled data is difficult to acquire in practical deployment settings. To address this problem, we introduce Retrospective Harness Optimization (RHO), a self-supervised method that optimizes the agent harness using only past trajectories. Specifically, RHO selects a diverse coreset of challenging tasks from past trajectories and re-solves them in parallel. The agent analyzes these rollouts using self-validation and self-consistency, then generates candidate harness updates and selects the most effective one by its own pairwise self-preference. We evaluate RHO across three diverse domains, spanning software engineering, technical work, and knowledge work. Notably, a single optimization round improves the pass rate on SWE-Bench Pro from 59% to 78% without any external grading. Furthermore, our analysis demonstrates that RHO effectively targets prior failure modes. As a result, the optimized harness alters the agent's behavior patterns and sustains higher accuracy during long-horizon sessions.

AI.tldr: RHO is a self-supervised method that optimizes AI agent harnesses (skills, tools, workflows) using only past trajectories—without ground-truth labels. It selects challenging tasks, re-solves them in parallel, analyzes rollouts via self-validation/self-consistency, and selects harness updates via self-preference. It improves SWE-Bench Pro pass rate from 59% to 78% without external grading.
AI.motivation: Existing agent harness optimization methods require ground-truth validation sets, which are difficult to acquire in practical deployment settings. The authors aim to enable self-supervised optimization without labeled data.
AI.method: RHO (1) selects a diverse coreset of challenging tasks from past trajectories, (2) re-solves them in parallel, (3) analyzes rollouts using self-validation and self-consistency, (4) generates candidate harness updates, and (5) selects the most effective update via pairwise self-preference.
AI.result: On SWE-Bench Pro, a single RHO optimization round improves pass rate from 59% to 78% without external grading. The method also effectively targets prior failure modes and sustains higher accuracy during long-horizon sessions across three diverse domains (software engineering, technical work, knowledge work).
AI.conclusion: RHO demonstrates that self-supervised harness optimization is feasible and effective, enabling agents to improve autonomously by learning from their own past trajectories, thus reducing reliance on costly labeled validation data.

title: Domain-Adapted Small Language Models with Hybrid Post-Processing: Achieving Cost-Efficient, Low-Latency Multi-Label Structured Prediction via LoRA Fine-Tuning on Scarce Data
summary:
Deploying frontier large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific structured evaluation tasks often incurs substantial latency, cost, and data privacy overhead. We present a hybrid framework that combines a fine-tuned small language model (LLaMA 3.1 8B, with only 2.05% trainable parameters via LoRA) and a deterministic rule-based post-processing layer. Trained on just 219 curated examples, the system is applied to multi-label compliance evaluation of conversational transcripts spanning 18 heterogeneous output fields. In blind evaluation on 53 previously unseen production transcripts, it achieves 100% JSON structural validity, 83.0% human-validated overall accuracy, and 100% accuracy on the most critical classification field. The proposed approach formalizes a hybrid neural-symbolic decomposition and introduces targeted hard-negative augmentation to improve performance on critical decision boundaries. Running on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, inference completes in approximately 2 seconds, which is 2-5x faster than frontier-model APIs. The system costs only $0.013 per evaluation compared with $0.025-$0.055 for proprietary alternatives, resulting in 46-76% cost savings. These results demonstrate that domain-adapted small language models, when combined with deterministic post-processing, can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while substantially reducing operational cost, latency, and privacy risk.
  Keywords: small language models, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, LoRA, domain adaptation, hybrid inference, compliance evaluation, structured output.

AI.tldr: A hybrid framework combining a fine-tuned small LLM (LLaMA 3.1 8B with LoRA) and rule-based post-processing achieves 83% accuracy, 100% JSON validity, 46-76% cost savings, and 2-5x faster inference compared to frontier LLMs for structured compliance evaluation on conversational transcripts.
AI.motivation: To reduce the substantial latency, cost, and data privacy overhead associated with deploying frontier large language models for domain-specific structured evaluation tasks.
AI.method: A hybrid neural-symbolic framework combining LoRA fine-tuned LLaMA 3.1 8B (2.05% trainable parameters) with deterministic rule-based post-processing, trained on 219 curated examples with targeted hard-negative augmentation.
AI.result: 100% JSON structural validity, 83.0% overall accuracy, 100% accuracy on the most critical classification field, ~2 seconds inference on single A100 GPU, $0.013 per evaluation (46-76% cost savings).
AI.conclusion: Domain-adapted small language models combined with deterministic post-processing can match frontier-model accuracy for structured compliance evaluation while substantially reducing cost, latency, and privacy risk.

title: Video-Rate Streaming Stylization on a Vision-Aware MLLM-Conditioned Edit Diffusion: Asymmetric Batched Inference on a Distilled UNet + MLLM Text Encoder
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures, 12 tables. Under review at IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology. Code, evaluation harness, and the released v3 Temporal LLLite adapter weights are at https://github.com/otanl/dreamlite-stream (also mirrored to Hugging Face and Zenodo)
summary: Aggressive distillation of the diffusion U-Net inverts the per-frame bottleneck of real-time text-to-image pipelines: once the denoiser is a 4-step or 1-step distilled student, the text encoder becomes the critical path. This inversion is most acute in vision-aware edit diffusion, where the encoder is a multimodal large language model (MLLM). We study the case of a 0.39B distilled edit U-Net paired with a 2.13B MLLM text encoder (Qwen3-VL) and present a streaming pipeline targeted at this regime built around three engineering mechanisms: asymmetric side-stream / main-stream CUDA pipelining with batched text-encoder amortisation (and optional static-prompt caching), a compile-friendly ControlNet-LLLite reformulation that folds the entire U-Net + adapter stack into a single fused graph, and a periodic conditioning-refresh schedule with a hook subset that amortises the per-frame conditioning cost. On a single consumer RTX 3090 Ti at 512x512 the pipeline sustains 27.4 fps over a 480-frame run at batch size B=8 and 29.6 fps at B=16, with end-to-end p50 latency of approximately 0.5 and 1.0 seconds respectively; the same operating point measures 54.9 fps on RTX 4090 and 74.1 fps on RTX 5090. We report video-rate streaming throughput rather than interactive low latency, and locate our numbers against same-stack StreamDiffusion re-runs as systems context, not as a benchmark superiority claim. For the trained oil-painting style, the released temporal adapter generalises within in-clip noise to 19 unused DAVIS-2017 sequences and 15 non-DAVIS clips from seven sources; prompt-level generalisation to unseen style families is bounded and reported separately.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a streaming pipeline for real-time text-to-image editing with a distilled U-Net (0.39B) and a large MLLM text encoder (2.13B), achieving 27.4-74.1 fps on consumer GPUs via asymmetric CUDA pipelining, fused ControlNet-LLLite, and periodic conditioning-refresh scheduling.
AI.motivation: The distillation of diffusion U-Nets inverts the bottleneck — the text encoder (especially MLLMs) becomes the critical path in real-time pipelines. The authors aim to address this inversion for video-rate streaming text-to-image editing.
AI.method: Three engineering mechanisms: (1) asymmetric side-stream/main-stream CUDA pipelining with batched text-encoder amortization and optional static-prompt caching; (2) compile-friendly ControlNet-LLLite reformulation fusing U-Net + adapter into one graph; (3) periodic conditioning-refresh schedule with hook subset to amortize per-frame conditioning cost.
AI.result: On RTX 3090 Ti: 27.4 fps (B=8) and 29.6 fps (B=16) at 512x512 over 480 frames; 54.9 fps on RTX 4090, 74.1 fps on RTX 5090. Generalizes to unseen DAVIS-2017 sequences and non-DAVIS clips for the trained style.
AI.conclusion: The proposed streaming pipeline effectively addresses the MLLM text-encoder bottleneck in distilled diffusion pipelines, achieving video-rate throughput on consumer hardware. Prompt-level generalization to unseen style families remains bounded.

title: Towards World Models in Biomedical Research
summary: A central goal of biomedicine is to understand, predict and ultimately control the dynamic mechanisms by which biological systems respond to perturbations, disease progression and therapeutic intervention. Although foundation models and large language models have accelerated biomedical data interpretation, most current systems remain focused on static pattern recognition rather than prospective simulation of biological futures. Here we propose biomedical world models as a paradigm for AI-driven discovery. These models learn latent representations of molecular, cellular, tissue and clinical states, together with intervention-conditioned dynamics that allow future trajectories to be simulated before actions are taken. We discuss how biomedical world models could function as data engines, environment simulators and scientific planning substrates across applications including virtual cells, organoids, virtual patients and surgical simulation. We outline the data infrastructure, evaluation benchmarks, safety constraints and governance frameworks required. Biomedical world models may provide a foundation for simulation-guided, closed-loop and experimentally actionable biomedical discovery.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes "biomedical world models" as a new AI paradigm that can simulate future biological trajectories across molecular to clinical levels, enabling predictive, simulation-guided biomedical discovery.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems in biomedicine are limited to static pattern recognition and cannot prospectively simulate biological futures, hindering prediction and control of disease progression and treatment responses.
AI.method: Biomedical world models learn latent representations of multi-scale biological states (molecular, cellular, tissue, clinical) along with intervention-conditioned dynamics to simulate future trajectories before actions are taken.
AI.result: The framework is positioned to function as data engines, environment simulators, and scientific planning substrates for applications such as virtual cells, organoids, virtual patients, and surgical simulation.
AI.conclusion: Biomedical world models could provide a foundation for closed-loop, simulation-guided, and experimentally actionable biomedical discovery, though they require appropriate data infrastructure, benchmarks, safety, and governance.

title: Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks for Counterfactual Outcome Prediction
summary: Longitudinal treatment decisions require predicting potential outcomes under future treatment sequences in the presence of time-varying confounding, heterogeneous patient dynamics, and limited domain-specific data. Existing longitudinal causal estimators typically train a new model for each cohort or simulator. We introduce Causal Longitudinal Prior-Fitted Networks (CausalLongPFN), a prior-fitted in-context predictor for longitudinal causal prediction. The model is pretrained entirely on synthetic episodes sampled from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, exposing it to treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear state evolution, delayed effects, and cumulative treatment responses. At test time, CausalLongPFN is frozen: it conditions on support trajectories, a query history, and a proposed future treatment sequence, and returns a predictive distribution over future outcomes without gradient updates or propensity-model fitting. Multi-step predictions are obtained by recursively applying the one-step predictor under the specified treatment sequence. We evaluate on branchable cancer, HIV, and warfarin benchmarks with ground-truth counterfactual labels, and on factual-only rolling-origin prediction in MIMIC-III ICU trajectories. CausalLongPFN is competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines on counterfactual benchmarks and performs strongly on factual MIMIC-III prediction, suggesting that broad synthetic causal pretraining can provide a useful frozen alternative when repeated domain-specific training is costly or impractical.

AI.tldr: CausalLongPFN is a frozen, pre-trained model for longitudinal causal prediction that uses synthetic pretraining over temporal causal models to predict outcomes under treatment sequences without retraining, performing competitively with domain-specific baselines on counterfactual benchmarks and factual MIMIC-III ICU prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing longitudinal causal estimators require training a new model for each cohort or simulator, which is costly and impractical when domain-specific data is limited or repeated training is needed.
AI.method: CausalLongPFN is a prior-fitted in-context predictor pretrained on synthetic episodes from a broad prior over temporal structural causal models, covering treatment-confounder feedback, latent heterogeneity, nonlinear dynamics, delayed effects, and cumulative responses. At test time, the model is frozen and recursively applies one-step predictions conditioned on support trajectories, query history, and proposed future treatments.
AI.result: Competitive with domain-trained longitudinal baselines on counterfactual benchmarks (cancer, HIV, warfarin) and performs strongly on factual MIMIC-III ICU prediction.
AI.conclusion: Broad synthetic causal pretraining can provide a useful frozen alternative when repeated domain-specific training is costly or impractical.

title: Multimodal Sexism Identification and Characterization using Large Language Models and Gradient Boosting
summary: We present the AILS-NTUA submission to the EXIST 2026 Lab at CLEF, addressing multimodal sexism identification and characterization in memes (Task 2) and short-form videos (Task 3). Our system follows a feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline built around gradient-boosted regression models and hierarchical post-processing. For memes, we combine visual, textual, demographic, biometric, and LLM-derived semantic indicators designed to capture high-level cues such as stereotyping, objectification, irony, and misogyny. For videos, we investigate the effect of feature selection, frame-based visual representations, OCR-based textual features, acoustic descriptors, and sensor-derived metadata. Development results show that focused LLM-derived semantic cues improve meme sexism identification, while video performance is highly sensitive to feature dimensionality and cross-modal noise. For videos, development results favor compact feature selection, but official test results show that this conclusion does not fully transfer to unseen data, where the unfiltered representation generalizes better. Overall, our findings highlight the usefulness of targeted semantic feature engineering for static memes and the need for more robust temporal modeling in noisy short-form video settings.

AI.tldr: AILS-NTUA presents a multimodal sexism detection system for memes and short-form videos, using feature-engineered late fusion with gradient boosting, finding that LLM semantic cues help memes while video performance depends on feature selection and generalization trade-offs.
AI.motivation: To address multimodal sexism identification and characterization in memes (Task 2) and short-form videos (Task 3) for the EXIST 2026 Lab at CLEF.
AI.method: Feature-engineered late-fusion pipeline using gradient-boosted regression models and hierarchical post-processing. For memes: visual, textual, demographic, biometric, and LLM-derived semantic indicators. For videos: frame-based visual features, OCR text, acoustic descriptors, and sensor metadata with feature selection analysis.
AI.result: LLM-derived semantic cues improve meme sexism identification. Video performance is sensitive to feature dimensionality; compact selection works on dev set but unfiltered representation generalizes better on test data.
AI.conclusion: Targeted semantic feature engineering is useful for static memes; short-form videos require more robust temporal modeling to handle cross-modal noise and generalization challenges.

title: A Pre-Registered Causal Partition of Self-Consistency Elicitation and Reward Design in RLVR
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves reasoning even when the reward signal is spurious -- assigning credit to the group-plurality answer rather than a ground-truth verifier. Practitioners commonly interpret naive = acc(TRUE) - acc(RANDOM) as the reward-design effect. We prove this estimand is systematically biased: it conflates self-consistency elicitation (sharpening the policy toward its modal answer via majority pseudo-reward) with genuine reward-design signal. Using a controlled tabular-GRPO simulator we derive an exact telescoping decomposition total = null + elicit + rd and measure each term across five prior-strength levels. The reward-design fraction of the naive estimator ranges from 0.139 at weak prior (ps=0.20) to 0.05 at strong prior (ps=0.80), with the elicitation term flipping sign at the self-consistency crossover. A pre-registered 2x2x2 factorial confirms non-additivity (interaction ratio 0.385; AxC effect -0.089). A points-vs-bounds pilot gate shows strong-prior regimes are point-identified while near-crossover regimes are only bounded. Re-audits of two named published results yield ELICITATION DOMINATED (elicitation share 0.98) and REWARD DESIGN DOMINATED (rd share 1.18) verdicts respectively, demonstrating the diagnostic value of the partition. We pre-commit to submit regardless of flip outcome; a non-flip is a finding of equal standing. We release a reusable one-command harness for any alignment paper to run the same audit.

AI.tldr: RLVR's naive reward estimator is biased because it conflates self-consistency elicitation with genuine reward-design signal. The authors derive an exact decomposition (total = null + elicit + rd), measure it across prior strengths, and show that most apparent reward effects can be dominated by elicitation.
AI.motivation: Practitioners misinterpret the naive estimator (acc(TRUE) - acc(RANDOM)) as a pure reward-design effect. The authors aim to show this is systematically biased because it conflates two distinct mechanisms: reward-design signal and self-consistency elicitation (sharpening toward the modal answer via majority pseudo-reward).
AI.method: Controlled tabular-GRPO simulator with an exact telescoping decomposition (total = null + elicit + rd). Pre-registered 2x2x2 factorial design to test non-additivity. Points-vs-bounds analysis for identification regimes. Re-audits of two published results.
AI.result: Reward-design fraction ranges from 0.139 (weak prior, ps=0.20) to 0.05 (strong prior, ps=0.80). Elicitation term flips sign at self-consistency crossover. Non-additivity confirmed (interaction ratio 0.385; AxC effect -0.089). Re-audits show one result is ELICITATION DOMINATED (share 0.98), the other REWARD DESIGN DOMINATED (share 1.18).
AI.conclusion: The naive estimator is not a valid measure of reward-design effect; the proposed decomposition is necessary for diagnosing whether improvements come from reward design or elicitation. Practitioners should audit their results using this partition. Release reusable one-command harness for this audit.

title: CaliDist: Calibrating Large Language Models via Behavioral Robustness to Distraction
summary: Existing calibration methods for Large Language Models (LLMs) often overlook a critical dimension of trustworthiness: a model's {\em behavioral robustness} to irrelevant or misleading information. In this paper, we argue that a model's true confidence should reflect its stability under cognitive pressure. We introduce \textsc{CaliDist}, a novel post-hoc calibration approach that directly measures and penalizes a model's susceptibility to distraction. \textsc{CaliDist} quantifies how an LLM's predictions and uncertainty change when its input prompt is perturbed with semantic \textit{distractors}. This stability (or lack thereof) signal is then used to adaptively scale the model's initial confidence score. Our extensive experiments on seven Natural Language Understanding classification benchmarks using six distinct LLMs show that \textsc{CaliDist} consistently achieves lower Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier Score compared with strong baselines. Remarkably, our method reduces the ECE from 23\% to 7\% on average--a relative improvement of 70\%--demonstrating that behavioral stability is a powerful signal for calibration. We make our code and datasets available at github.com/m-anas-j/CaliDist.

AI.tldr: CaliDist is a post-hoc calibration method that measures LLM behavioral robustness to semantic distractors, achieving 70% relative improvement in ECE (from 23% to 7%) across 7 NLU benchmarks and 6 LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing calibration methods overlook behavioral robustness to irrelevant/misleading information; a model's true confidence should reflect its stability under cognitive pressure.
AI.method: CaliDist perturbs input prompts with semantic distractors, measures changes in predictions and uncertainty, then adaptively scales the initial confidence score based on stability signals.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines on 7 NLU benchmarks with 6 LLMs; reduces ECE from 23% to 7% (70% relative improvement), consistently achieving lower ECE and Brier Score.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral stability is a powerful signal for LLM calibration; code and datasets are publicly available.

title: Deep Learning-based 3D Oral Cavity Reconstruction Using 2D Intraoral Images
comment: 4 pages, 5 figures. English version of a paper presented at the Korea Multimedia Society Conference, November 2025
summary: Oral 3D modelling is one of the most essential stages in dentistry, and many different approaches, such as impression taking and intraoral scanning, are commonly used for this phase, each with notable limitations. Impression taking, which involves placing alginate or silicone material in a tray and inserting it into the patient's oral cavity to form a negative mold, suffers from significant patient discomfort, material deformation errors, and difficulties in storage and transportation. Intraoral scanners, which directly scan oral structures in real time using structured light or laser technology, produce state-of-the-art results but are associated with substantially high equipment costs. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a software-based approach that reconstructs a 3D oral model using only ten 2D intraoral images captured from different angles, requiring no dedicated hardware devices. The proposed method reduces cost, eliminates the need for physical scanning equipment, minimises patient discomfort, and enables automated 3D reconstruction. The model is trained on the publicly available Dental3DS dataset, comprising 950 upper jaw samples, and employs MobileNetV2 as the image encoder combined with Multi-head Attention for multi-view feature fusion. The proposed model achieves an accuracy of 77.49%, measured by nearest-neighbor matching with a distance threshold of 0.035. However, predicted vertices tend to concentrate in high-density regions of the ground truth, resulting in uneven point distribution across the reconstructed model.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a software-based 3D oral reconstruction method using only ten 2D intraoral images, eliminating the need for expensive hardware like intraoral scanners or uncomfortable impression taking. Using MobileNetV2 and Multi-head Attention, it achieves 77.49% accuracy but suffers from uneven point distribution.
AI.motivation: Current oral 3D modelling methods (impression taking and intraoral scanning) suffer from patient discomfort, material errors, storage issues, and high equipment costs. The authors aim to address these limitations with a low-cost, hardware-free software approach.
AI.method: A deep learning model using MobileNetV2 as the image encoder with Multi-head Attention for multi-view feature fusion, trained on the Dental3DS dataset (950 upper jaw samples). It reconstructs 3D oral models from only ten 2D intraoral images captured from different angles.
AI.result: Achieves 77.49% accuracy measured by nearest-neighbor matching with a distance threshold of 0.035. However, predicted vertices tend to concentrate in high-density regions of the ground truth, leading to uneven point distribution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed software-based approach reduces cost, eliminates physical scanning equipment, minimizes patient discomfort, and enables automated 3D reconstruction, though point distribution unevenness remains a limitation to address.

title: Edit-R2: Context-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Turn Image Editing
summary: Text-guided image editing has advanced rapidly with diffusion models and unified multimodal foundation models. However, most existing methods remain confined to single-turn settings, overlooking the more realistic scenario of multi-turn in-context editing, where users iteratively refine an image through a sequence of instructions.
