In this setting, a model must follow each new instruction while preserving accumulated session-level constraints, challenged by two coupled failure modes: long-context dilution, where sparse textual constraints become difficult to recover from growing interleaved image-text histories, and state contamination, where earlier editing mistakes degrade subsequent generations. We introduce Edit-R2, a novel reinforcement learning post-training framework for unified multimodal models. Edit-R2 reconstructs the operative session intent, which effectively consolidates scattered historical constraints into an explicit reasoning trace before each editing turn. It further enables multi-turn RL over both reasoning and generation through a unified objective that jointly optimizes intent reconstruction generation in discrete text space and flow-matching image generation in continuous latent space, while a trajectory filtering mechanism suppresses corrupted rollouts to stabilize training under state contamination. To support systematic evaluation, we introduce MICE-Bench, a large-scale benchmark for multi-turn in-context editing with automated metrics for instruction following (IF), content consistency (CC), and global awareness (GA) over accumulated session constraints. Experiments show that Edit-R2 substantially improves multi-turn in-context editing and achieves competitive performance compared against strong baselines.

AI.tldr: Edit-R2 is a reinforcement learning post-training framework for unified multimodal models that addresses multi-turn in-context image editing by reconstructing session intent and using trajectory filtering to overcome long-context dilution and state contamination. A new benchmark MICE-Bench is introduced for evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing text-guided image editing methods focus on single-turn settings, overlooking the realistic multi-turn scenario where users iteratively refine images through sequences of instructions. Two key challenges in this setting are long-context dilution (sparse constraints lost in interleaved image-text history) and state contamination (earlier mistakes degrade later generations).
AI.method: Edit-R2 employs (1) operative session intent reconstruction to consolidate historical constraints into explicit reasoning traces before each editing turn, (2) multi-turn RL with a unified objective jointly optimizing discrete text-space intent reconstruction and continuous latent-space flow-matching image generation, and (3) a trajectory filtering mechanism to suppress corrupted rollouts and stabilize training under state contamination.
AI.result: Experiments on the introduced MICE-Bench benchmark (with automated metrics for instruction following, content consistency, and global awareness) show that Edit-R2 substantially improves multi-turn in-context editing and achieves competitive performance against strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Multi-turn in-context editing can be effectively addressed through reinforcement learning post-training with intent reconstruction and trajectory filtering, and MICE-Bench provides a systematic evaluation framework for this task.

title: SALT: When More Rollouts Don't Help in Group-Based Policy Optimization and How to Make Them Matter
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) often adopts GRPO-style group-relative updates, sampling multiple rollouts per prompt to construct normalized learning signals. However, merely increasing the number of rollouts does not reliably strengthen learning: under GRPO-style group normalization, per-rollout policy-gradient features can concentrate into a low-rank, signed geometry, causing substantial cancellation during aggregation and weakening the effective update. We address this failure mode with SALT, a Subspace-Adaptive geometry pLug-in componenT that uses sample-wise gradient geometry to reweight the coefficients of group-relative updates. SALT estimates a dominant shared subspace from the mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies the residual channel when signed cancellation is severe. Across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales, SALT improves effective update geometry and performance without modifying the reward model or the rollout sampling procedure

AI.tldr: SALT improves GRPO-style RLVR by detecting and compensating for signed gradient cancellation in group-relative updates through adaptive subspace reweighting, enhancing update geometry and reasoning performance without altering reward models or rollout sampling.
AI.motivation: Existing GRPO-style group-relative updates suffer from a failure mode where increasing rollouts causes per-rollout gradient features to concentrate into low-rank signed geometry, leading to substantial cancellation during aggregation and weakening effective updates.
AI.method: SALT (Subspace-Adaptive geometry pLug-in componenT) estimates a dominant shared subspace from mini-batch Gram geometry, decomposes group-relative coefficients into shared and residual channels, and adaptively amplifies the residual channel when signed cancellation is severe.
AI.result: SALT improves effective update geometry and performance across diverse reasoning-oriented RLVR benchmarks and model scales.
AI.conclusion: The proposed plug-in component effectively addresses a fundamental weakness in GRPO-style RLVR without requiring changes to the reward model or rollout sampling procedure.

title: ATT-CR: Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal
summary: Cloud removal aims to accurately reconstruct the ground objects obscured by clouds in remote sensing images. Existing Transformer-based methods utilizing self-attention have shown impressive results by effectively modeling long-range dependencies in cloudy images. However, they suffer from the following issues: 1) the high computational complexity of self-attention limits scalability; 2) treating both cloudy and clean pixels as valid within the attention computation brings disturbances in subsequent layers, leading to suboptimal performance. To address these challenges, we propose the Adaptive Triangular Transformer for Cloud Removal (ATT-CR), a model that effectively reduces computational costs and mitigates interference from cloudy pixels. Specifically, it consists of two core components: Triangular Attention (TAN) and Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM). TAN employs lower and upper triangular matrices to approximate Softmax attention with O(N) computational complexity, significantly reducing the computational costs. The FSGM, on the other hand, integrates with TAN to adaptively distinguish between cloudy and clean features, which minimizes the introduction of invalid information into subsequent layers. Extensive experiments on cloud removal benchmarks demonstrate that ATT-CR delivers superior performance compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: ATT-CR is an Adaptive Triangular Transformer for cloud removal that reduces computational complexity (O(N) vs O(N²)) and adaptively filters cloudy pixel interference using Triangular Attention and Feature Selected Gating Module.
AI.motivation: Existing Transformer-based cloud removal methods suffer from high computational complexity of self-attention and fail to distinguish between cloudy and clean pixels, causing information disturbance in subsequent layers.
AI.method: Proposes ATT-CR with two components: (1) Triangular Attention (TAN) using lower/upper triangular matrices to approximate Softmax attention with O(N) complexity; (2) Feature Selected Gating Module (FSGM) that adaptively distinguishes cloudy vs clean features to reduce invalid information propagation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on cloud removal benchmarks show ATT-CR delivers superior performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed triangular attention and feature gating effectively address both computational and interference issues in cloud removal, achieving better results with lower complexity.

title: Bidirectional Search for Longest Paths: Case for Front-to-Front Heuristics
summary: Bidirectional heuristic search can potentially reduce search effort for problems amenable to backward search. Therein, it is well-known that front-to-front heuristics can reduce the number of node expansions, but their overhead is so high that overall runtime almost always increases. We propose BiXDFBnB, a bidirectional depth-first branch-and-bound algorithm that adapts the Single-Frontier Bidirectional Search (SFBDS) framework - originally developed for shortest-path (MIN) problems - to the Generalized Longest Simple Path (GLSP) setting. Because SFBDS inherently operates on paired states, front-to-front (F2F) heuristic evaluation arises naturally and avoids the overhead typically associated with bidirectional frontier management. We show that this adaptation can be successfully applied to maximization (MAX) problems while efficiently handling overlapping constraints. BiXDFBnB is applied to several types of longest-path problems: Longest Simple Path (LSP), Snakes, and Coil-in-the-Box (CIB). Empirical evaluation shows that the new algorithm frequently reduces the number of node expansions and, in some cases, also improves overall runtime.

AI.tldr: BiXDFBnB is a new bidirectional depth-first branch-and-bound algorithm that adapts the Single-Frontier Bidirectional Search framework to longest-path problems, reducing node expansions through efficient front-to-front heuristics while avoiding typical overhead.
AI.motivation: Bidirectional heuristic search can reduce search effort but front-to-front heuristics, while reducing node expansions, typically introduce high overhead that increases overall runtime. The authors aim to overcome this limitation.
AI.method: The authors propose BiXDFBnB, which adapts the SFBDS framework (originally for shortest-path/MIN problems) to the Generalized Longest Simple Path (MAX) setting. It uses depth-first branch-and-bound with natural front-to-front heuristic evaluation that avoids the typical overhead of bidirectional frontier management.
AI.result: Applied to Longest Simple Path, Snakes, and Coil-in-the-Box problems. BiXDFBnB frequently reduces node expansions and in some cases improves overall runtime.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptation successfully extends SFBDS to maximization problems with overlapping constraints, offering efficiency gains in node expansions and sometimes runtime across multiple longest-path problem types.

title: Robust and sparse support vector machine via hybrid truncated loss for supervised classification
summary: The support vector machine (SVM) is a widely used classifier, but choosing an appropriate loss function remains difficult. Convex losses such as the hinge loss and least-squares loss are sensitive to outliers, while bounded non-convex losses often lead to high computational cost. To address this, we propose a hybrid truncated loss function ($L_{\mathrm{ht}}$) that is both sparse and bounded, and build the $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM model for single-view classification. We introduce the P-stationary point and use it to establish the first-order necessary and sufficient optimality conditions. Based on these conditions, we design an alternating direction method of multipliers with a working-set strategy that reduces computational cost and achieves global convergence. We further extend $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM to multi-view learning by adding structural information and view weights, resulting in Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM, which follows both the consensus and complementarity principles. Experiments on synthetic, real-world, and image datasets show that $L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM achieves higher accuracy with fewer support vectors and better noise robustness than five single-view methods, while Mv$L_{\mathrm{ht}}$-SVM outperforms six multi-view methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes hybrid truncated loss SVM (Lht-SVM) for robust single-view classification and its multi-view extension (MvLht-SVM), achieving higher accuracy with fewer support vectors and better noise robustness than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing SVM loss functions struggle with a trade-off: convex losses (hinge, least-squares) are sensitive to outliers, while bounded non-convex losses are computationally expensive. The goal is to design a loss that is both robust and computationally efficient.
AI.method: Proposes a hybrid truncated loss (Lht) that is sparse and bounded. Introduces P-stationary point for first-order optimality conditions. Designs an ADMM with working-set strategy for efficient global convergence. Extends to multi-view learning (MvLht-SVM) by incorporating structural information and view weights.
AI.result: Lht-SVM outperforms five single-view methods with higher accuracy, fewer support vectors, and better noise robustness. MvLht-SVM outperforms six multi-view methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on synthetic, real-world, and image datasets.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid truncated loss effectively balances robustness and computational efficiency for both single-view and multi-view SVM classification tasks.

title: Global-Local Monte Carlo Tree Search in Vision-Language Models for Text-to-3D Indoor Scene Generation
summary: Large Vision-Language Models have achieved significant reasoning performance in various tasks.However, there are few studies on text-to-3D indoor scene generation with LVLMs. The main challenge is that prevailing LVLM-based methods employ chain-of-thought sequential decision mechanisms that cannot revise earlier decisions, causing error propagation.In this paper, we consider the task as a planning problem constrained by spatial and layout commonsense.To solve this problem, we model it as a tree search problem with global and local trees, which differs from existing sequential decision-making approaches.In the global tree, we place each object iteratively and explore multiple attempts like humans furnishing a room, where the problem space is represented as a tree.To effectively search the tree, we propose a hierarchical scene representation and a PRM-guided MCTS method.The hierarchical representation abstracts a scene into room level, region level, floor object level, and supported object level.The PRM-guided MCTS method uses the PRM to prune unnecessary branches and the MCTS algorithm to balance exploration and exploitation to get an optimal solution with fewer attempts.In the local tree, it further decomposes the placement of each object into finer sub-steps, including the specific placement parameters.To make the whole appearance of the scene consistent, we leverage pre-trained diffusion image generative models to predict textures for all the objects in the scene.As existing benchmarks for text-to-3D indoor scene generation remain limited in scale and diversity, we collect a new large-scale diverse dataset that contains 65 scene types and 3,250 instructions with diverse sizes, layouts, and styles, named 3DTindo-bench, to better assess the capability of the state-of-the-art models. Our experiments show that our method generates more realistic 3D scenes than state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a tree-search-based framework (global and local trees) for text-to-3D indoor scene generation using LVLMs, overcoming the error propagation issue of chain-of-thought methods, and introduces a new large-scale benchmark (3DTindo-bench) with 65 scene types and 3,250 instructions.
AI.motivation: Existing LVLM-based text-to-3D scene generation methods suffer from error propagation due to chain-of-thought sequential decision mechanisms that cannot revise earlier decisions. There is also a lack of large-scale, diverse benchmarks for evaluating such tasks.
AI.method: Models text-to-3D generation as a tree search problem with global and local trees. Global tree iteratively places objects using a PRM-guided MCTS method (PRM prunes unnecessary branches, MCTS balances exploration/exploitation). Local tree decomposes each object placement into finer sub-steps. Hierarchical scene representation (room → region → floor object → supported object). Pre-trained diffusion models generate consistent textures.
AI.result: The proposed method generates more realistic 3D scenes than state-of-the-art approaches, validated on the newly collected 3DTindo-bench dataset (65 scene types, 3,250 instructions).
AI.conclusion: Modeling text-to-3D scene generation as a tree search with hierarchical representation and PRM-guided MCTS effectively addresses error propagation, and the new benchmark enables more comprehensive evaluation.

title: The Self-Correction Illusion: LLMs Correct Others but Not Themselves
summary: Recent work shows that LLM agents struggle to correct errors in their own reasoning traces yet show markedly higher correction rates when identical claims appear under external sources. We ask whether this asymmetry reflects a capability deficit or a role-label artifact: does an agent's willingness to correct a wrong claim depend causally on the chat-template role that carries it, rather than on the claim's content? Our setup keeps the erroneous claim byte-identical across all conditions (SHA-256 verified) and varies only its wrapping role: the agent's own \role{<thought>}, a \role{user} message, a \role{tool} response, or a \role{system <memory>} block. Across 13 model-domain cells covering seven model families and three domains ($n{=}30$ paired tasks per cell), relabeling the claim from \role{<thought>} to an external role lifts the explicit-correction rate by 23 to 93 percentage points, with 10 of 13 cells reaching $p{<}0.001$. Further experiments confirm that the effect is asymmetric, mechanistically decomposable, and robust across domains. The failure to self-correct is not a cognitive deficit; it is a chat-template artifact. We exploit this artifact by designing a prompt-structure-only intervention that requires no training and no model modification, with its strongest role label being domain-dependent: \role{<memory>} dominates on math, while a plain \role{user} message dominates on logical deduction.

AI.tldr: LLM agents' failure to self-correct is not a cognitive deficit but a chat-template artifact: the role label carrying an erroneous claim causally determines correction rates. A simple role-relabeling intervention boosts corrections by 23–93 percentage points without any training or model modification.
AI.motivation: To determine whether LLM agents' inability to correct their own reasoning errors stems from a genuine capability limitation or simply from the chat-template role (e.g., <thought>, user, tool, system) that carries the claim.
AI.method: Kept erroneous claims byte-identical (SHA-256 verified) across conditions, varying only the wrapping role: <thought>, user, tool, or system <memory>. Tested across 13 model-domain cells (7 model families, 3 domains, n=30 paired tasks per cell).
AI.result: Relabeling the claim from <thought> to an external role lifted explicit-correction rates by 23–93 percentage points; 10 of 13 cells reached p<0.001. The effect is asymmetric, mechanistically decomposable, and robust across domains.
AI.conclusion: The failure to self-correct is a chat-template artifact, not a cognitive deficit. A prompt-structure-only intervention (no training, no model modification) can exploit this artifact, with best role label being domain-dependent (system <memory> for math, user for logical deduction).

title: Consistency Training Along the Transformer Stack
summary: Consistency training encourages models to behave similarly across different contexts, and has shown promise for reducing misalignment. We broaden the scope of consistency training in two ways. First, we introduce two new internal consistency targets: MLP Consistency Training (MLPCT), which matches post-activation MLP states, and Attention Consistency Training (AttCT), which matches per-head attention distributions. Second, we apply consistency training to four additional safety threats: persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment. Across several models and threat settings, we find that consistency training reduces misalignment well beyond the sycophancy and jailbreak settings studied in prior work. We also find cases of cross-threat generalization, where training against one failure mode improves robustness to another, and identify a shared residual-stream mechanism underlying ACT, MLPCT, and AttCT, while distinguishing BCT as mechanistically distinct. Our results suggest that consistency training is a flexible and extensible framework for alignment, capable of unifying defenses against a broader class of model pathologies.

AI.tldr: This paper extends consistency training beyond sycophancy and jailbreaks by introducing two new targets (MLPCT and AttCT) and applying them to four additional safety threats, showing cross-threat generalization and identifying distinct mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Prior consistency training was limited to sycophancy and jailbreak settings; the authors aim to broaden its applicability to a wider range of safety threats and introduce new internal consistency targets.
AI.method: Two new consistency targets: MLPCT (matches post-activation MLP states) and AttCT (matches per-head attention distributions). Applied to four new safety threats: persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment.
AI.result: Consistency training reduces misalignment across diverse threat settings beyond prior work. Cross-threat generalization observed. Shared residual-stream mechanism found for ACT, MLPCT, AttCT; BCT is mechanistically distinct.
AI.conclusion: Consistency training is a flexible, extensible alignment framework capable of unifying defenses against a broad class of model pathologies.

title: ReSAGE-PAR: Representational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
comment: Under review at IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology (TCSVT)
summary: To address the limited diversity and data scarcity in Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR), we explore image synthesis using diffusion models guided by attribute-based prompts. While this enables the controlled generation of pedestrian images, it faces two critical challenges: (i) the domain gap between high-quality pre-training data and low-resolution, non-standard surveillance crops, and (ii) the need for reliable attribute verification to prevent generative hallucinations. In this paper, we introduce a robust generate-score-autolabel pipeline called ReSAGE-PAR (REpresentational Similarity Assessment for Generative Expansion in PAR) that bridges this domain gap and enables scalable, high-fidelity dataset expansion. First, we adapt pre-trained diffusion models to native PAR resolutions using a tailored LoRA-based Image-to-Image approach. Second, we extract vision-language alignment scores between the generated images and their conditioning prompts, utilizing a comprehensive prompting strategy that includes label-consistent and inconsistent complements. Finally, we formulate a Bayesian classifier that converts these continuous scores into reliable binary pseudo-labels. Extensive evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of ReSAGE-PAR in preserving spatial priors and verifying attributes. When integrated into PAR training, ReSAGE-PAR consistently yields significant improvements-achieving gains of up to 8.7% on standard backbones and pushing state-of-the-art frameworks to new performance levels. This proves its value as an architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement. The complete codebase for ReSAGE-PAR is publicly available at http://www-vpu.eps.uam.es/publications/ReSAGE-PAR.

AI.tldr: ReSAGE-PAR is a pipeline that uses diffusion models with attribute-based prompts to generate synthetic pedestrian images for Pedestrian Attribute Recognition (PAR). It addresses the domain gap and attribute verification issues via LoRA-based image-to-image adaptation, vision-language alignment scoring, and Bayesian pseudo-labeling, achieving up to 8.7% improvement on standard backbones.
AI.motivation: To overcome limited diversity and data scarcity in PAR by leveraging diffusion models for controlled pedestrian image synthesis, while tackling the domain gap between high-quality pre-training data and low-resolution surveillance images, as well as generative hallucinations in attribute verification.
AI.method: (1) LoRA-based Image-to-Image diffusion model adapted to PAR native resolutions; (2) Vision-language alignment scoring using label-consistent and inconsistent prompts; (3) Bayesian classifier converting continuous scores into reliable binary pseudo-labels.
AI.result: Up to 8.7% gain on standard backbones; pushes SOTA frameworks to new performance levels; architecture-agnostic solution for scalable PAR enhancement.
AI.conclusion: ReSAGE-PAR effectively bridges the domain gap and ensures attribute fidelity, proving valuable as a scalable, architecture-agnostic dataset expansion method for PAR.

title: Framing, Judging, Steering: An Assessable Competency Model for Teach-ing Students to Reason With Generative AI
summary: Generative AI makes answers easy and understanding hard, and uncritical use invites cognitive offloading. Schools still measure unaided performance, yet the real task is to produce good work with AI: framing an ill-defined task, judging the output, and steering the model toward a better result. This ability is rarely assessed in its own right; where measured, it collapses into one "prompting" score that cannot diagnose why AI use succeeds or fails. We propose CoRe-3 (Co-Reasoning), a competency model factoring productive AI use into three assessable skills we abbreviate FJS: Framing (specifying an ill-defined task before invoking AI), Judging (evaluating output for errors and unstated assumptions), and Steering (iteratively redirecting the model). Its distinguishing claim is the separation of pre-generation Framing from post-generation Steering, with Judging as the gate between. We ground the skills in theory, state five testable propositions, and instantiate them in CoReasoningLab, an open platform that presents flawed AI output and scores them independently. Over simulated learners (generated and graded by different models), the skills dissociate: each tracks its own manipulated competence while staying flat in the others, and grades become correlated when one competence is shared across all three (convergent and discriminant validity), across grader backends from two providers. Human-rater agreement and outcomes are next; we release the instrument, data, and protocol.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CoRe-3, a competency model that breaks down productive AI use into three distinct skills: Framing, Judging, and Steering (FJS), arguing these should be assessed separately rather than as a single "prompting" score. The authors present CoReasoningLab, an open platform that tests these skills independently, and demonstrate through simulated learners that the three skills dissociate and show convergent/discriminant validity.
AI.motivation: Current education measures unaided performance, but real-world tasks require effective AI collaboration. Existing assessments collapse AI interaction ability into one "prompting" score, which cannot diagnose why AI use succeeds or fails. The authors aim to create a more granular, diagnostic competency model.
AI.method: The authors propose CoRe-3 (Co-Reasoning), factoring AI use into Framing (specifying tasks before invoking AI), Judging (evaluating output), and Steering (iteratively refining). They instantiate this in CoReasoningLab, an open platform with flawed AI output and independent scoring. They test with simulated learners generated and graded by different models across two provider backends.
AI.result: The three skills dissociate: each tracks its own manipulated competence while remaining flat in others. Grades become correlated when one competence is shared across all three (convergent and discriminant validity), demonstrated across grader backends from two providers.
AI.conclusion: CoRe-3 offers a valid, diagnostic framework for assessing AI collaboration skills. The platform, data, and protocol are released for further research. Human-rater agreement and real-world outcomes remain as next steps.

title: GenAutoML: An Agentic Framework for Dynamic Architecture Generation and Optimization in Time-Series Analysis
summary: Designing neural architectures for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection remains a resource-intensive task that often requires substantial domain expertise. Traditional Automated Machine Learning (AutoML) systems typically rely on static, predefined search spaces, limiting their ability to adapt to diverse data characteristics. We present GenAutoML, an agentic framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) as neural architects to bridge natural-language requirements and executable PyTorch implementations. The framework incorporates a Sandboxed Reflection Loop for autonomous code refinement and a Signature-Aware Runtime that enforces architectural consistency and execution safety. To improve robustness under non-stationary conditions, we further introduce a Dynamic Reversible Instance Normalization (Dyn-RevIN) wrapper. Experiments on the ETTh1, ETTm1, and Weather benchmarks demonstrate that GenAutoML can dynamically generate task-specific neural architectures tailored to dataset characteristics. Among the generated models, WaveInterferenceNet achieves inference latency below 0.01 ms per sample while maintaining competitive predictive performance. By emphasizing computational efficiency, architectural adaptability, and stable optimization behavior, GenAutoML enables the creation of ultra-lightweight neural networks suitable for resource-constrained and latency-sensitive Edge AI deployments.

AI.tldr: GenAutoML is an LLM-driven framework that automatically designs lightweight neural architectures for time-series forecasting and anomaly detection, achieving sub-0.01ms inference latency via models like WaveInterferenceNet while outperforming traditional AutoML through dynamic search spaces and sandboxed self-correction.
AI.motivation: Traditional AutoML systems rely on static, predefined search spaces and require substantial domain expertise, limiting adaptability to diverse time-series data characteristics.
AI.method: GenAutoML uses LLMs as neural architects with a Sandboxed Reflection Loop for autonomous code refinement, a Signature-Aware Runtime for safety, and a Dynamic Reversible Instance Normalization (Dyn-RevIN) wrapper for robustness under non-stationary conditions.
AI.result: On ETTh1, ETTm1, and Weather benchmarks, GenAutoML dynamically generates task-specific architectures; the best model (WaveInterferenceNet) achieves <0.01ms inference latency per sample with competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: GenAutoML enables ultra-lightweight, adaptive neural networks suitable for resource-constrained and latency-sensitive Edge AI deployments through computational efficiency and stable optimization.

title: Texture-preserving implicit neural representation for Cone beam CT truncated reconstruction
summary: Cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) frequently suffers from data truncation, which introduces severe artifacts and limits the effective field of view (FOV). Existing deep learning methods for truncated cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) reconstruction suffer from serious limitations, including a strict reliance on supervised ground truth and a failure to account for continuous 3D spatial truncation variations. To address these challenges, we introduce a self-supervised 3D reconstruction framework based on neural scene representations. By directly mapping spatial coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, our approach inherently bypasses traditional filtering and backprojection operations, thereby fundamentally eliminating truncation-induced ring artifacts while enabling robust continuous 3D data extrapolation. However, coordinate networks are susceptible to an inherent spectral bias, which leads to a severe loss of clinically vital high-frequency textures. To resolve this bottleneck, we further incorporate a physics-based iterative refinement module into the neural scene representation architecture. Leveraging the artifact-free, extrapolated volume from the coordinate network as an optimal initialization, this module progressively re-extracts and injects high-frequency structural information from the original projections back into the volume. Extensive experiments on both simulated and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method successfully unifies the exceptional artifact suppression and extrapolation capabilities of neural networks with the high-fidelity detail preservation of iterative algorithms.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised 3D CBCT reconstruction framework using neural scene representations with a physics-based iterative refinement module that eliminates truncation artifacts and extrapolates continuous 3D data while preserving high-frequency textures.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning methods for truncated CBCT reconstruction require supervised ground truth and fail to handle continuous 3D spatial truncation variations, limiting their practical applicability.
AI.method: A self-supervised 3D reconstruction framework based on neural scene representations that maps spatial coordinates to radiodensity under projection supervision, bypassing filtering/backprojection, with a physics-based iterative refinement module to recover high-frequency textures lost due to spectral bias.
AI.result: The method unifies artifact suppression and extrapolation capabilities of neural networks with high-fidelity detail preservation of iterative algorithms, validated on both simulated and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses truncation artifacts and data extrapolation in CBCT while preserving clinically important high-frequency details through the combination of neural scene representation and iterative refinement.

title: Beyond Vector Similarity: A Structural Analysis of Graph-Augmented Retrieval for Industrial Knowledge Graphs
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) fails systematically on queries requiring structural reasoning over interconnected entities. We compare eight retrieval architectures for aerospace supply chain intelligence, progressing from text retrieval through graph traversal to graph computation. Using a 46-node knowledge graph with 64 typed edges, we evaluate 23 queries across 10 intent categories and demonstrate that five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. Our central finding is the operator vocabulary thesis: the barrier to LLM-based graph reasoning is not model intelligence but the computational operators available as tools. An LLM Query Planner with 9 typed traversal primitives outperforms bespoke handlers (F1 = 0.632 vs. 0.472) while generalizing to unseen queries. Adding 6 graph computation tools, the LLM selectively adopts them for exactly the query categories where traversal fails. We also identify a measurement gap: entity-level F1 systematically underscores structural queries where comprehensive answers are correct.

AI.tldr: RAG fails on structural reasoning over interconnected entities. Using an aerospace knowledge graph, the paper shows that vector retrieval cannot reach five query classes. The key insight is the "operator vocabulary thesis": LLM graph reasoning is limited by available computational operators, not model intelligence. An LLM Query Planner with traversal primitives outperforms bespoke handlers (F1=0.632 vs 0.472), and adding graph computation tools helps exactly where traversal fails.
AI.motivation: Current RAG systems cannot handle queries that require structural reasoning (e.g., traversing relationships or computing properties across interconnected entities), which is a critical gap for domains like supply chain intelligence.
AI.method: The authors compare eight retrieval architectures on a 46-node aerospace knowledge graph with 64 typed edges, evaluating 23 queries across 10 intent categories. They propose an LLM Query Planner with typed traversal primitives and graph computation tools.
AI.result: Five query classes are structurally unreachable for vector retrieval. The LLM Query Planner with 9 traversal primitives achieves F1=0.632 vs 0.472 for bespoke handlers, and generalizes to unseen queries. Adding 6 graph computation tools is selectively adopted only for categories where traversal fails.
AI.conclusion: The barrier to LLM-based graph reasoning is not model intelligence but the available operator vocabulary. Entity-level F1 metrics also systematically under-score structural queries.

title: Deciphering Two Training Clocks in Grokking via Deep Linear Network Theory with Conditional ReLU Reduction
summary: Grokking suggests that fitting the training data and learning a simple underlying rule may occur on different time scales. We formalize this phenomenon by separating the fast decay of the classification loss from the slower simplification of the learned representation, and we call the resulting pair of stopping times two training clocks. For deep linear networks, we show that a post-margin gap-growth or one-step tail-contraction condition reduces the cross-entropy loss to level epsilon on a logarithmic time scale. In contrast, when layerwise weight decay is present, the induced regularization on the end-to-end map can be expressed as a Schatten-type penalty; under a sharp late-time Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail, this structural energy closes on a polynomial time scale. The two clocks, therefore, separate fitting from representation simplification. We then explain how the same mechanism can appear in ReLU MLPs. In regions where the activation patterns on the training set remain fixed, the network reduces to a linear model in the active coordinates. In a two-layer ReLU embedding model, chain-rule estimates further show that the classifier head can receive larger effective gradients than the embedding block under controlled downstream norms. This supports a two-stage mechanism in which the classifier fits first, while the representation continues to simplify later. We use modular addition as the main experimental setting. The deep linear theory provides the rigorous core of the analysis. But the ReLU results are formulated as conditional reductions that account for empirical behavior without claiming a global proof for nonlinear training dynamics.

AI.tldr: This paper formalizes "grokking" by introducing two training clocks—one for fast classification loss decay and one for slower representation simplification—showing they separate in deep linear networks via gap-growth/contraction conditions and in ReLU MLPs under fixed activation patterns.
AI.motivation: To formalize and theoretically explain the phenomenon of grokking, where models fit training data rapidly but simplify representations slowly, by separating these two processes into distinct temporal scales.
AI.method: Defines two training clocks (classification loss decay vs. representation simplification). For deep linear networks, proves logarithmic time fitting under gap-growth/contraction and polynomial time simplification under Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz tail with weight decay. Extends to ReLU MLPs via reduction to linear models under fixed activation patterns, and two-layer ReLU embedding analysis.
AI.result: Proves that classification loss decays logarithmically while representation simplification occurs on polynomial time scales under weight decay. Shows similar two-stage dynamics in ReLU networks: classifier fits first, representation simplifies later.
AI.conclusion: The two-clock framework provides rigorous theoretical grounding for grokking in deep linear networks, and conditional results suggest similar mechanisms operate in ReLU networks, though a global proof for nonlinear dynamics remains open.

title: LoomVideo: Unifying Multimodal Inputs into Video Generation and Editing
summary: Developing unified video generation and editing models capable of interpreting interleaved multimodal inputs is a promising yet challenging frontier field. Existing unified frameworks predominantly rely on massive models (typically 13B parameters or more) and incorporate source video conditions for editing by concatenating sequence tokens. This concatenation inevitably doubles the sequence length, quadrupling the computational complexity of the self-attention mechanism and introducing prohibitive overhead. To address these bottlenecks, we present LoomVideo, a highly efficient 5B-parameter unified architecture for both video generation and editing. LoomVideo replaces the standard text encoder with a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) and employs Deepstack injection mechanism to align multi-layer MLLM features with the Diffusion Transformer (DiT). Crucially, we introduce a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning approach for video editing. By scaling and directly adding the clean source video latent to the noised target latent, this elegant design eliminates the need for token concatenation, drastically reducing computational cost while maintaining robust capabilities for complex, non-rigid edits. Furthermore, a Negative Temporal RoPE strategy is seamlessly integrated to handle multiple reference images. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our compact 5B model achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across comprehensive benchmarks, exhibiting exceptional superiority in e-commerce and fashion generation scenarios. Benefiting from the zero-overhead conditioning mechanism, LoomVideo achieves at least a 5.41x acceleration in inference speed compared to models of similar capabilities, paving the way for highly practical and efficient video foundation models.

AI.tldr: LoomVideo is a 5B-parameter unified model for video generation and editing that uses a Multimodal LLM encoder, Deepstack injection, and a zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning mechanism (replacing costly token concatenation) to achieve state-of-the-art performance with 5.41x faster inference.
AI.motivation: Existing unified video generation/editing models rely on massive models (13B+ parameters) and concatenate source video tokens, which doubles sequence length and quadruples self-attention complexity, creating prohibitive computational overhead.
AI.method: 1) Replaces standard text encoder with an MLLM; 2) Deepstack injection mechanism for multi-layer MLLM feature alignment with DiT; 3) Zero-overhead Scale-and-Add conditioning (adds clean source latent directly to noised target latent, no token concatenation); 4) Negative Temporal RoPE for handling multiple reference images.
AI.result: State-of-the-art or highly competitive performance across benchmarks, exceptional in e-commerce/fashion scenarios; achieves at least 5.41x inference speedup over similar-capability models.
AI.conclusion: LoomVideo demonstrates that a compact 5B model can outperform larger models by using efficient architectural designs, paving the way for practical and efficient video foundation models.

title: TS-ICL: A Flexible Time-Indexed Foundation Model for Time Series via In-Context Learning
summary: Foundation models mark a profound paradigm shift in time series modeling, with task-specific models being superseded by general-purpose zero-shot models. Yet, current approaches primarily focus on forecasting, while real-world time series are often irregularly and partially observed, requiring models that can jointly forecast, impute missing values, and handle degraded sampling conditions. To address these challenges, we introduce TS-ICL, a novel probabilistic In-Context Learning encoder--regressor Transformer that unifies forecasting and imputation. TS-ICL formulates time series tasks as timestamp-aligned regression and naturally incorporates covariates by training on synthetic dependency structures generated from a novel causal data prior. Empirically, TS-ICL achieves a new state-of-the-art in imputation, while remaining competitive with leading forecasting foundation models across both univariate and covariate-aware benchmarks. It shows particularly strong performance in forecasting with partially observed look-back windows.

AI.tldr: TS-ICL is a probabilistic in-context learning Transformer that unifies time series forecasting and imputation, achieving state-of-the-art imputation while remaining competitive with forecasting foundation models, especially under partially observed conditions.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series are often irregularly and partially observed, yet existing foundation models focus primarily on forecasting and cannot jointly handle imputation and degraded sampling conditions.
AI.method: A probabilistic In-Context Learning encoder-regressor Transformer that formulates time series as timestamp-aligned regression, trained on synthetic dependency structures from a novel causal data prior.
AI.result: State-of-the-art in imputation, competitive with leading forecasting foundation models on univariate and covariate-aware benchmarks, strong performance under partially observed look-back windows.
AI.conclusion: TS-ICL effectively unifies forecasting and imputation for irregularly sampled time series, advancing beyond task-specific models toward general-purpose zero-shot time series modeling.

title: LLM-Conditioned Synthesis of Pathological Gaits via Structured Gait-Language Representations
comment: Accepted at CVPR MOMA Workshop 2026 and selected for spotlight presentation at the workshop
summary: Pathological gait datasets remain scarce due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and movement variability. Our work presents a multimodal LLM-guided framework for pathology-aware 3D gait data synthesis from structured textual descriptions. The proposed method generates fixed-length synthetic skeleton-based gait sequences for pathological gait classification tasks. The framework combines motion tokenisation, pathology-aware language conditioning, LLM-based semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation. A key contribution is the proposed pathological tokeniser, which is designed to preserve pathology-specific motion characteristics during discrete representation learning. Experiments suggest that the proposed synthetic sequences improve downstream classification for recurrent classifiers when combined with real data. The best result is obtained using a GRU classifier trained with real and synthetic samples, achieving 92.77\% accuracy under a leave-one-subject-out protocol.

AI.tldr: A multimodal LLM-guided framework synthesizes pathology-aware 3D gait sequences from text to augment scarce pathological gait datasets, achieving 92.77% accuracy with a GRU classifier trained on combined real and synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Pathological gait datasets are scarce due to privacy, recruitment, cost, and movement variability, hindering downstream classification tasks.
AI.method: A framework combining motion tokenisation, a pathology-specific tokeniser for preserving pathology characteristics, LLM-based semantic augmentation, and language-to-gait generation to produce fixed-length synthetic skeleton-based gait sequences from structured text descriptions.
AI.result: Synthetic sequences improve downstream classification when combined with real data; best result is 92.77% accuracy using a GRU classifier under leave-one-subject-out protocol.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pathology-aware synthetic data generation effectively augments limited pathological gait datasets and enhances classifier performance.

title: RedditPersona: A Modular Framework for Community-Conditioned LLM Adaptation from Reddit
summary: Community-conditioned language model adaptation requires choices about data collection, community definition, and evaluation that are currently made independently in each study, making it hard to compare assumptions or reuse artifacts. We present RedditPersona, a modular framework that standardizes these choices: it collects Reddit posts and comments, profiles active users, partitions them under five grouping strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, and interaction-based), trains a parameter-efficient adapter per strategy via QLoRA, and evaluates them under a shared metric suite spanning fluency, fidelity, distributional alignment, and community identifiability. Applied to 112 subreddits in the urban well-being domain (301,429 user profiles, 16M+ comments), we find that adapters' behavioral identifiability tracks each strategy's intrinsic agreement with the subreddit baseline, and that a consistent trade-off between identifiability and distributional similarity to real text holds across all five strategies. The code and configuration files are available at: https://github.com/Ahghaffari/redditpersona.

AI.tldr: RedditPersona is a modular framework for standardizing community-conditioned language model adaptation, tested on 112 subreddits, revealing trade-offs between identifiability and distributional similarity across five grouping strategies.
AI.motivation: Current community-conditioned LM adaptation lacks standardization in data collection, community definition, and evaluation, hindering comparability and reuse across studies.
AI.method: The framework collects Reddit posts/comments, profiles active users, partitions them via five strategies (subreddit-based, graph-structural, semantic, hybrid, interaction-based), trains QLoRA adapters per strategy, and evaluates with a shared metric suite (fluency, fidelity, distributional alignment, community identifiability).
AI.result: On 112 subreddits (301K profiles, 16M+ comments): adapters' behavioral identifiability aligns with each strategy's agreement with the subreddit baseline; a consistent trade-off exists between identifiability and distributional similarity across all strategies.
AI.conclusion: RedditPersona provides a reproducible, standardized pipeline for community-level LM adaptation, revealing systematic trade-offs that inform future design choices.

title: When Denser Credit Is Not Enough: Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon LLM Agent Training
summary: Long-horizon LLM agents require reinforcement learning methods that can assign credit to intermediate decisions under sparse and delayed rewards. Recent group-based methods such as GiGPO improve over GRPO by constructing step-level advantages at repeated anchor states. However, we show that such dense credit can be statistically unreliable: under limited rollouts, rare but lucky actions may receive overly large advantages, producing divergent anchor bias and late-stage training oscillation. We propose Evidence-Calibrated Policy Optimization (ECPO), a critic-free policy optimization algorithm that calibrates step-level credit before policy updates. ECPO combines Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage, which groups rollouts by canonical actions and shrinks low-count estimates, with Variance-Gated Credit Weighting, which suppresses anchor states dominated by within-action noise. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B show that ECPO consistently outperforms strong baselines, improving GiGPO by +5.2/+7.3 success points on ALFWorld/WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B while adding only 0.1% additional advantage-computation overhead.

AI.tldr: ECPO is a critic-free RL algorithm for LLM agents that calibrates step-level credit by grouping rollouts by canonical actions and suppressing noisy anchor states, outperforming GiGPO by +5.2/+7.3 success points with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing group-based methods like GiGPO construct dense step-level advantages but suffer from statistical unreliability—rare lucky actions get inflated advantages under limited rollouts, causing divergent anchor bias and training oscillation.
AI.method: ECPO combines two components: (1) Evidence-Calibrated Action Advantage, which groups rollouts by canonical actions and shrinks low-count estimates, and (2) Variance-Gated Credit Weighting, which suppresses anchor states dominated by within-action noise.
AI.result: On ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B, ECPO consistently beats strong baselines, improving GiGPO by +5.2 points on ALFWorld and +7.3 points on WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B, adding only 0.1% extra advantage-computation overhead.
AI.conclusion: ECPO demonstrates that critic-free, statistically calibrated credit assignment can effectively address the instability of dense step-level advantages in long-horizon LLM agent RL, achieving clear gains with negligible computational cost.

title: ReCache: Learning Budget-Aware Caching Schedules for Diffusion Models via REINFORCE
summary: Modern diffusion models generate high-quality images and videos, but their iterative denoising process makes inference expensive. Feature caching accelerates sampling by reusing or predicting intermediate activations across neighboring denoising steps, exploiting the redundancy of computations along the reverse trajectory. In this work, we focus on the caching schedule: selecting which denoising steps should be fully recomputed. Existing schedules are either fixed (e.g. uniform) or chosen adaptively from per-step error heuristics; in both cases, the actual compute cost is a side-effect of hand-tuned thresholds rather than a quantity the user can specify. We propose ReCache, which inverts this: given a target budget k, it learns the recomputation schedule that maximizes generation quality, turning compute into a directly controllable input. ReCache trains via policy gradients, sidestepping backpropagation through full diffusion inference, and uses no labelled data. Generations from uncached inference serve as matching targets, paired with a reward for generation quality. ReCache is compatible with any caching mechanism, including feature reuse and feature forecasting; for each mechanism, a single trained policy adapts across computational budgets at inference time. ReCache consistently outperforms scheduling baselines: under a $\times5.04$ FLOPs reduction on FLUX, it reduces LPIPS by 31% (from 0.456 to 0.316) compared to DiCache; on Wan 2.1 at a $\sim \times2.6$ speedup, it drops LPIPS by 65% (from 0.480 to 0.169) and boosts the VBench score by 7% (5.6 points, from 70.4 to 76.0) over uniform HiCache. Code is available at https://github.com/thecrazymage/ReCache.

AI.tldr: ReCache learns a recomputation schedule for diffusion model feature caching that turns compute budget into a controllable input, outperforming fixed and adaptive baselines across multiple models and caching mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Existing caching schedules rely on fixed patterns or hand-tuned error thresholds, making compute cost an uncontrolled side-effect rather than a user-specifiable parameter.
AI.method: ReCache trains a policy via policy gradients (no backprop through full diffusion, no labeled data) to learn a recomputation schedule given a target compute budget k, using uncached inference generations as matching targets with a quality reward.
AI.result: Under ×5.04 FLOPs reduction on FLUX, ReCache reduces LPIPS by 31% vs DiCache; on Wan 2.1 at ~×2.6 speedup, reduces LPIPS by 65% and boosts VBench by 7% (5.6 points) over uniform HiCache.
AI.conclusion: ReCache provides a principled, controllable approach to caching scheduling that consistently outperforms existing baselines across different models, caching mechanisms, and computational budgets.

title: Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents
summary: Despite recent progress, LLM agents still struggle with reasoning over long interaction histories. While current memory-augmented agents rely on a static retrieve-then-reason paradigm, this rigid pipeline design prevents them from dynamically adapting memory access to intermediate evidence discovered during inference. To bridge this gap, we propose MRAgent, a framework that combines an associative memory graph with an active reconstruction mechanism. We represent memory as a Cue-Tag-Content graph, where associative tags serve as semantic bridges connecting fine-grained cues to memory contents. Operating on this structure, our active reconstruction mechanism integrates LLM reasoning directly into memory access, allowing the agent to iteratively explore and prune retrieval paths based on accumulated evidence. This ensures that memory retrieval is dynamically adapted to the reasoning context while avoiding combinatorial explosion caused by unconstrained expansion. Experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark and LongMemEval benchmark demonstrate significant improvements over strong baselines (up to 23%), while substantially reducing token and runtime cost, highlighting the effectiveness of active and associative reconstruction for long-horizon memory reasoning.

AI.tldr: MRAgent is a framework that uses an associative memory graph with an active reconstruction mechanism to improve LLM agents' reasoning over long interaction histories, achieving up to 23% improvement over baselines while reducing token and runtime costs.
AI.motivation: Current memory-augmented agents rely on a static retrieve-then-reason paradigm that cannot dynamically adapt memory access to intermediate evidence discovered during inference.
AI.method: MRAgent represents memory as a Cue-Tag-Content graph with associative tags as semantic bridges, and uses an active reconstruction mechanism that integrates LLM reasoning into memory access for iterative exploration and pruning of retrieval paths.
AI.result: Achieves up to 23% improvement over strong baselines on LoCoMo and LongMemEval benchmarks, while substantially reducing token and runtime costs.
AI.conclusion: Active and associative reconstruction is effective for long-horizon memory reasoning in LLM agents.

title: High-Dimensional Theory of LoRA Fine-Tuning in a Solvable Attention Model
summary: We develop a high-dimensional statistical theory of low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in attention models, capturing the interplay between pre-training and fine-tuning. We introduce a solvable framework in which a single-head attention layer is first pre-trained on a data-abundant task and subsequently adapted via a rank-one LoRA update on limited data. In the high-dimensional limit, both stages admit a sharp asymptotic characterization in terms of a finite set of order parameters, yielding explicit predictions for test errors and representation alignment. Our analysis shows that the impact of pre-training on LoRA is summarized by an effective noise term, from which we derive prescriptions for the optimal pre-training procedure. We also demonstrate a regime with a mismatch between the value of the test error and representation quality, and propose an application of our theory to active fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a high-dimensional statistical theory for low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in attention models, analyzing the pre-training and fine-tuning interplay using a solvable single-head attention framework with rank-one LoRA updates. It provides sharp asymptotic characterizations, derives optimal pre-training prescriptions, and identifies a mismatch regime between test error and representation quality.
AI.motivation: To understand the theoretical foundations of how pre-training benefits LoRA fine-tuning in attention models, and to provide optimal strategies for pre-training when subsequent LoRA adaptation is limited by data scarcity.
AI.method: A solvable high-dimensional statistical framework: a single-head attention layer is pre-trained on abundant data, then fine-tuned via rank-one LoRA on limited data. Sharp asymptotic characterization is derived using order parameters in the high-dimensional limit.
AI.result: (1) The impact of pre-training on LoRA reduces to an effective noise term. (2) Optimal pre-training procedures are derived. (3) A regime exists where test error and representation quality become mismatched. (4) An application to active fine-tuning is proposed.
AI.conclusion: Pre-training's effect on LoRA can be neatly summarized as an effective noise term, enabling principled optimization of pre-training procedures. The discovered mismatch between error and representation quality has practical implications for active fine-tuning strategies.

title: FontFusion: Enhancing Generative Text in Diffusion Models with Typographic Conditioning
summary: Typography generation in diffusion models faces a persistent trade-off: enabling precise font control typically degrades text legibility, while maintaining readability often sacrifices typographic fidelity. We present FontFusion, a plug-and-play conditioning framework for Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures that resolves this dilemma through three core innovations: (1) a hierarchical token representation establishing explicit text-font relationships at multiple granularities, (2) position-aware embeddings creating spatial bindings between typography and image content, and (3) a multi-level token dropping strategy improving both computational efficiency and generalization to unseen fonts. Our systematic evaluation of font embedding spaces reveals that a dual encoder combining DeepFont and DINOv2 outperforms any single encoder for typography tasks. FontFusion demonstrates 76% relative improvement on challenging decorative fonts over single-encoder baselines and font consistency gains exceeding approximately 68-76% over unconditioned models, while integrating into existing DiT architectures without retraining.

AI.tldr: FontFusion is a plug-and-play framework for Diffusion Transformer models that resolves the trade-off between font control and text legibility in typography generation, using hierarchical token representations, position-aware embeddings, and token dropping strategies, achieving 76% improvement on decorative fonts and ~68-76% font consistency gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: Typography generation in diffusion models faces a persistent trade-off: precise font control degrades text legibility, while maintaining readability sacrifices typographic fidelity.
AI.method: Three core innovations: (1) hierarchical token representation establishing explicit text-font relationships at multiple granularities, (2) position-aware embeddings creating spatial bindings between typography and image content, and (3) multi-level token dropping strategy for efficiency and generalization. Uses dual encoder combining DeepFont and DINOv2.
AI.result: 76% relative improvement on challenging decorative fonts over single-encoder baselines; font consistency gains exceeding ~68-76% over unconditioned models; integrates into existing DiT architectures without retraining.
AI.conclusion: FontFusion resolves the font control vs. legibility trade-off, demonstrating that dual-encoder (DeepFont + DINOv2) outperforms single encoders, and provides a plug-and-play solution for DiT-based typography generation.

title: Beyond Similarity: Trustworthy Memory Search for Personal AI Agents
summary:
Personal AI agents increasingly rely on long-term memory to provide persistent personalization across sessions. However, existing memory pipelines are largely driven by semantic similarity: memory data close to the current query is retrieved and injected into the model context. This creates a critical trustworthiness gap, since a semantically related memory may still be contextually inappropriate, leading to threats such as cross-domain leakage, sycophancy, tool-call drift, or memory-induced jailbreaks.
  In this paper, we study memory search as a trust boundary in personal AI agents. We evaluate representative agentic memory frameworks, including A-Mem, Mem0, and MemOS, together with OpenClaw, a real-world personal-agent environment with persistent state and tool-use capability. Our results show that long-term memory is not merely a utility layer, but a durable control channel that can reshape how agents interpret tasks and execute actions, leaving them highly susceptible to the aforementioned threats. To mitigate these vulnerabilities, we propose MemGate, a lightweight and deployable memory plug-in for trustworthy memory search, with only 9M parameters and a 35.1MB footprint. MemGate is inserted between the vector memory store and the backbone LLM, requiring no LLM modification, memory-database rewriting, or inference-time LLM judge. It applies a query-conditioned neural gate to candidate memory representations, turning raw similarity search into task-conditioned memory admission. Across multiple mainstream memory frameworks, real-world agent settings, and diverse LLM backbones, MemGate reduces memory-induced threats while preserving long-term memory utility.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies trust vulnerabilities in semantic similarity-based memory retrieval for personal AI agents (cross-domain leakage, sycophancy, tool-call drift, memory jailbreaks) and proposes MemGate, a lightweight 9M-parameter neural plug-in that applies query-conditioned gating to transform raw similarity search into task-conditioned memory admission.
AI.motivation: Existing memory pipelines rely on semantic similarity for retrieval, which creates a trustworthiness gap—semantically related memories may be contextually inappropriate, leading to security threats like cross-domain leakage, sycophancy, tool-call drift, and memory-induced jailbreaks.
AI.method: Proposes MemGate, a lightweight (9M parameters, 35.1MB) neural memory plug-in placed between vector memory store and backbone LLM. It applies query-conditioned neural gating to candidate memory representations, converting raw similarity search into task-conditioned memory admission without modifying the LLM, memory database, or using inference-time LLM judges.
AI.result: Across multiple memory frameworks (A-Mem, Mem0, MemOS), real-world agent settings (OpenClaw environment), and diverse LLM backbones, MemGate reduces memory-induced threats while preserving long-term memory utility.
AI.conclusion: Long-term memory is not merely a utility layer but a durable control channel that can reshape agent behavior, making agents susceptible to trust threats. MemGate offers a lightweight, deployable solution that establishes memory search as a critical trust boundary in personal AI agents.

title: Addressing Imbalance in Multi-Label Data via Label-Specific Distance-based Oversampling
summary: The complex imbalanced label distribution poses a crucial challenge to multi-label classification, as most classifiers are biased towards the majority class and high-frequent labels. Oversampling is an efficient and flexible solution that augments instances to provide a more balanced training dataset for multi-label classifiers. Most existing oversampling methods create synthetic instances in a heuristic way that essentially relies on neighborhood information retrieved using Euclidean distance within the entire feature space. However, they fail to consider the varying semantic relevance of features to different labels, leading to label inconsistency among proximate neighbors and further introducing label confusion and overfitting to synthetic instances. To overcome the above issue, we propose a novel sampling approach called Label-Specific Distance-based Multi-Label Oversampling (LSDMLO) that creates more useful and well-labeled synthetic instances to address the imbalance in multi-label datasets. LSDMLO derives the label-specific distance to identify label-consistent neighbors based on the weighted pertinent feature space, which facilitates selecting seed instances that express more label correlations in boundary areas and generating synthetic instances aligned with the label distribution of original data. The comprehensive experiments verify that the proposed LSDMLO outperforms the state-of-the-art multi-label sampling approaches under various base classifiers.

AI.tldr: LSDMLO is a new multi-label oversampling method that uses label-specific distances based on weighted relevant features to generate better synthetic instances, outperforming existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-label oversampling methods use Euclidean distance in the full feature space, ignoring varying semantic relevance of features to different labels. This causes label inconsistency among neighbors and introduces label confusion and overfitting.
AI.method: LSDMLO derives label-specific distance based on weighted pertinent feature space to identify label-consistent neighbors, selects seed instances expressing more label correlations in boundary areas, and generates synthetic instances aligned with original label distribution.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show LSDMLO outperforms state-of-the-art multi-label sampling approaches under various base classifiers.
AI.conclusion: Label-specific distance can effectively address label inconsistency issues in multi-label oversampling, producing more useful and well-labeled synthetic instances for imbalanced multi-label classification.

title: VZCrash: A Large-Scale IMU Dataset of Ego-Vehicle Crashes
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITSC 2026). VZCrash is publicly available at this URL: https://huggingface.co/datasets/vzc-research-chapter/VZCrash
summary: We introduce VZCrash, the largest publicly available dataset of real-world vehicle collision data featuring Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) telemetry. The dataset contains more than 31,000 validated crashes and 158,000 negative samples, including hard cases and distractors. Each sample includes acceleration and angular velocity at 100 Hz, and GPS speed at 1 Hz. Events in VZCrash were captured by devices installed on a fleet of 73,010 commercial vehicles of different sizes driving in the United States over the span of several years. We also present an extensive experimental study enabled by the volume of the dataset. We first benchmark several different approaches, from a simple threshold-based heuristic to state-of-the-art deep learning models. Then, we present an experiment demonstrating the importance of scaling data to train high-quality crash detection models, and we show that scale is especially important when these models need to be deployed into a real-world environment.

AI.tldr: VZCrash is the largest public real-world vehicle collision dataset with 31K+ crashes and 158K negative samples from 73K commercial vehicles, featuring IMU telemetry at 100Hz. The paper benchmarks crash detection methods and demonstrates that scaling data is critical for real-world deployment of high-quality models.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of large-scale, publicly available real-world vehicle collision datasets with IMU data, which limits the development and evaluation of robust crash detection models.
AI.method: The authors collected and curated a dataset from 73,010 commercial vehicles across the US over several years, capturing 31K+ validated crashes and 158K negative samples with 100Hz IMU and 1Hz GPS data. They benchmark multiple approaches from simple heuristics to deep learning models.
AI.result: VZCrash contains 31,000+ validated crashes and 158,000 negative samples. The experimental study shows that scaling data is crucial for training high-quality crash detection models, especially for real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale, real-world crash data is essential for developing deployable crash detection models, and VZCrash provides a valuable public resource to advance research in this area.

title: When Should Memory Stay Silent: Measuring Memory-Use Boundaries in Memory-Augmented Conversational Agents
summary: Long-term memory enables language model agents to support personalized interactions, but it remains unclear when available memories warrant integration into responses. Existing memory evaluations emphasize retrieval accuracy and downstream task utility, while overlooking whether retrieved sensitive memory content is warranted in the current turn. We introduce RBI-Eval, a controlled measurement study built around a probe set that compares model behavior with and without access to sensitive memory under identical benign prompts. We evaluate four base LLMs against a matched no-memory reference across four memory-access settings: full-context exposure and three retrieval systems. Our results reveal substantial behavioral divergence. With memory available, the separation score for sensitive-memory integration decreases by 8.9\%--26.6\% relative to the matched no-memory reference for GPT-5.4-mini, but by 51.1\%--82.9\% for Claude-Sonnet-4.6, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Qwen3.5-9B. Control experiments on DeepSeek and GPT-5.4-mini show this effect is specific to sensitive content, rather than general personalization. Retrieval systems reduce exposure but do not eliminate integration once sensitive memory reaches the generator. These findings suggest safe personalization requires memory-aware decisions at both retrieval and generation time.

AI.tldr: Introduces RBI-Eval to evaluate when language model agents appropriately integrate sensitive long-term memory into responses, finding significant behavioral divergence across models and that retrieval systems alone are insufficient for safe personalization.
AI.motivation: Existing memory evaluations focus on retrieval accuracy and task utility but overlook whether retrieved sensitive memory content is actually warranted in the current conversational turn, creating a gap in understanding safe personalization.
AI.method: RBI-Eval is a controlled measurement study with a probe set comparing model behavior with vs. without access to sensitive memory under identical benign prompts, testing four LLMs across full-context and three retrieval settings against a no-memory reference.
AI.result: Sensitive-memory integration separation score decreases by 8.9%-26.6% for GPT-5.4-mini but by 51.1%-82.9% for Claude-Sonnet-4.6, DeepSeek-V4-Flash, and Qwen3.5-9B relative to no-memory reference. Control experiments confirm this is specific to sensitive content, not general personalization. Retrieval reduces exposure but does not eliminate integration.
AI.conclusion: Safe personalization requires memory-aware decisions at both retrieval and generation time, as retrieval systems alone cannot prevent unwanted sensitive-memory integration.

title: Short paper: Models in the dark -- Rectification and erasure under GDPR in ML supply chains
comment: accepted for presentation at Annual Privacy Forum 2026
summary: The rights to rectification and erasure, as established under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), are central to protecting individuals' privacy. However, their effective enforcement in machine learning (ML) systems remains challenging. Existing work has largely addressed these rights from either a legal or a technical perspective in isolation and disregards the fact that models are produced in complex supply chains involving multiple actors across development, distribution, and deployment. This paper presents a holistic survey of challenges in implementing the rights to rectification and erasure in ML models. Drawing on academic literature and guidance from data protection authorities, we find that many GDPR requirements cannot yet be technically met in practice. Our findings further suggest that issues arising in ML supply chains are insufficiently addressed in research. To tackle this gap, we introduce the notion of models in the dark -- derived models created further downstream in an ML chain without sufficient transparency or traceability -- and analyse the urgent challenges posed by this phenomenon. By adopting an interdisciplinary perspective, this work contributes to bridging the gap between legal requirements and the technical implementation of data subject rights in ML, ultimately supporting the development of trustworthy artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: This paper surveys challenges in implementing GDPR rights to rectification and erasure in machine learning models, identifies gaps due to complex ML supply chains, introduces the concept of "models in the dark," and calls for interdisciplinary approaches to bridge legal requirements with technical implementation.
AI.motivation: GDPR rights to rectification and erasure are crucial for privacy but difficult to enforce in ML systems. Existing work addresses legal or technical aspects in isolation, ignoring the complexity of ML supply chains with multiple actors.
AI.method: A holistic survey drawing on academic literature and guidance from data protection authorities, with an interdisciplinary lens integrating legal and technical perspectives.
AI.result: Many GDPR requirements cannot yet be technically met; issues in ML supply chains are insufficiently addressed. The paper identifies "models in the dark" (derived models created downstream without transparency/traceability) as a key unresolved challenge.
AI.conclusion: The gap between legal requirements and technical implementation of data subject rights in ML remains significant, and the "models in the dark" phenomenon poses urgent challenges. Interdisciplinary work is needed to support trustworthy AI development.

title: Knowledge Distillation for Visual Autoregressive Models
summary: Autoregressive (AR) image generation models are highly expressive but computationally intensive, motivating effective model compression. Knowledge distillation (KD) is a natural approach for model compression and has been widely studied in language modeling, yet its behavior in visual AR generation remains underexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic study of distillation strategies for AR image models. Our analysis shows that while standard distillation can yield meaningful gains, recent methods developed for language do not directly transfer to images: long decoding horizons and visual token ambiguity make teacher supervision unreliable especially under student-conditioned contexts. To address this, we propose VarKD, a distillation framework for visual autoregressive models that distills on student samples while selectively applying teacher supervision and reducing token-level ambiguity. Experiments on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones show that VarKD consistently outperforms prior distillation baselines, narrowing the gap to large-scale models.

AI.tldr: VarKD is the first systematic distillation strategy for visual autoregressive models, addressing challenges like long decoding horizons and token ambiguity that prevent direct transfer of language-based KD methods.
AI.motivation: AR image models are computationally intensive and need compression; KD works for language models but its behavior in visual AR generation is underexplored.
AI.method: VarKD distills on student samples with selective teacher supervision and reduces token-level ambiguity, tackling issues caused by long decoding horizons and unreliable teacher signals.
AI.result: VarKD consistently outperforms prior distillation baselines on ImageNet across multiple AR backbones, narrowing the gap to large-scale models.
AI.conclusion: Standard KD gives limited gains for visual AR models; VarKD effectively addresses the unique challenges and provides strong compression results.

title: Learning Visual Spatial Planning from Symbolic State via Modality-Gap-Aware Self-Distillation
summary: While vision-language models excel at general multimodal understanding, they still struggle with visual spatial planning.
