We attribute this to a perception-reasoning modality gap: visual planning requires models to infer latent state structures from pixels and then reason over the recovered structure to produce valid actions, whereas symbolic planning directly leverages explicit objects and constraints. This creates dual bottlenecks in visual state recovery and multi-step planning. To address this, we propose MGSD, a two-stage modality-gap-aware self-distillation framework. First, a cold-start grounding stage equips the visual student with reliable state representations, minimizing early perception noise. Second, a privileged teacher transfers planning capabilities via on-policy distillation, using explicit symbolic states to supervise the student's own visual rollout prefixes. Crucially, symbolic data is used strictly during training, leaving inference purely visual. Experiments on visual planning benchmarks show that MGSD consistently improves visual planning across both 4B and 8B backbones, raising the macro average by 19.3% and 18.4%, respectively. The resulting models narrow the gap to symbolic-input upper bounds, while ablations and diagnostics confirm that the improvement comes from both visual state recovery and optimal-path reasoning. These results suggest that modality-gap-aware self-distillation improves not only how models perceive actionable states, but also how they plan over the inferred structure. Code is available at https://github.com/Oranger-l/MGSD.

AI.tldr: MGSD is a two-stage self-distillation framework that bridges the perception-reasoning gap in vision-language models for visual spatial planning, improving macro average by ~19% on 4B/8B backbones through cold-start grounding and privileged teacher distillation.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models struggle with visual spatial planning due to a perception-reasoning modality gap: they must infer latent states from pixels (perception) and then reason over them (planning), creating dual bottlenecks in visual state recovery and multi-step reasoning. Symbolic planning avoids this by using explicit objects/constraints.
AI.method: MGSD uses a two-stage modality-gap-aware self-distillation: (1) Cold-start grounding stage trains the visual student with reliable state representations to reduce early perception noise. (2) Privileged teacher stage transfers planning capabilities via on-policy distillation, where explicit symbolic states supervise the student's visual rollout prefixes. Symbolic data is used only during training.
AI.result: On visual planning benchmarks, MGSD improves macro average by 19.3% (4B backbone) and 18.4% (8B backbone), narrowing the gap to symbolic-input upper bounds. Ablations confirm improvements come from both visual state recovery and optimal-path reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Modality-gap-aware self-distillation improves both perception of actionable states and planning over inferred structure, effectively bridging the gap between visual and symbolic planning.

title: Dead Directions: Geometric Singular Learning
summary: Singular learning theory and information geometry have studied the same parameter spaces in mostly separate vocabularies: the former computes Bayesian invariants in resolved coordinates, the latter works in original coordinates under a non-degeneracy assumption that overparameterised models routinely violate. We bridge them through one primitive, the dead direction: a unit vector along which the Fisher metric degenerates, equivalently a tangent to the analytic singular set with a definite KL order, set by how fast the KL divergence vanishes. The two readings name the same vector; our central move shows its KL order is recoverable as the decay rate of the directional Fisher curvature approaching the singularity, in original parameter coordinates and without a Hironaka resolution. A selection rule on smooth fibres translates this rate into Watanabe's single-direction contribution to the real log canonical threshold, and we extend the recovery to multi-component crossings, multiplicity $m$, the singular fluctuation $ν$ (universal in the KL order for 1D directions), prior-RLCT shifts, and tempered posteriors. We then lift this rate to a deep network: a multi-layer K-FAC factorisation writes each Fisher block as a product of activation- and gradient-side rates with a duality between them, instantiated at modern-network primitives (residual streams, layer normalisation, attention). A quotient theorem carries the rate to the gauge quotient $Θ/G$ under gradient flow on a $G$-invariant metric; SGD qualifies, standard Adam does not, and we construct a $G$-equivariant Adam-family preconditioner (DDCAdam) that does. The bridge yields a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form per-architecture predictions, and a trajectory-rate readout of Watanabe's triple $(λ, m, ν)$ from one checkpoint's forward and backward passes, without posterior sampling.

AI.tldr: This paper bridges singular learning theory and information geometry by introducing the concept of "dead direction" — a unit vector along which the Fisher metric degenerates — and shows how its KL order can be recovered in original coordinates without Hironaka resolution. The approach extends to multi-component crossings, deep networks via K-FAC factorization, and yields a trajectory-based readout of Watanabe's invariants from a single checkpoint.
AI.motivation: Singular learning theory and information geometry study the same parameter spaces but use different vocabularies and coordinate systems. Singular learning theory uses resolved coordinates to compute Bayesian invariants, while information geometry works in original coordinates under a non-degeneracy assumption that overparameterized models routinely violate. The paper aims to bridge these two frameworks.
AI.method: The paper introduces the "dead direction" concept, a unit vector where the Fisher metric degenerates. It shows the KL order is recoverable as the decay rate of directional Fisher curvature approaching the singularity in original coordinates. It extends this to multi-component crossings, multiplicity m, singular fluctuation ν, and tempered posteriors. It lifts the rate to deep networks via K-FAC factorization and proposes a G-equivariant optimizer (DDCAdam).
AI.result: The bridge yields a parameter-coordinate handle on singular geometry, closed-form per-architecture predictions, and a trajectory-rate readout of Watanabe's triple (λ, m, ν) from one checkpoint's forward and backward passes without posterior sampling. SGD qualifies for the quotient theorem; standard Adam does not, but DDCAdam does.
AI.conclusion: The dead direction provides a unified framework connecting singular learning theory and information geometry in original coordinates, enabling practical computation of Bayesian singular invariants from single checkpoints in deep networks.

title: HyperVis: Continuous Latent Visual Relational Graphs on the Lorentz Hyperboloid for Compositional Reasoning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) struggle with compositional reasoning that requires understanding inter-object relationships. A natural remedy is to inject explicit scene graph triplets $\langle s, p, o \rangle$ from an off-the-shelf scene graph generator (SGG), but we show this backfires: discrete text labels collide with the continuous visual modality, degrading GQA accuracy from 60.38\% to 58.86\%. We propose \textbf{HyperVis}, which bypasses the SGG semantic bottleneck entirely. From $N$ class-agnostic region proposals, we compute a dense $O(N^2)$ visual relation tensor via spatially-biased cross-attention, project it onto a Lorentz hyperboloid, and enforce hierarchy through spatial physics, namely IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion. We discover that HyperVis contributes in two complementary ways: (1) as a \emph{training-time regularizer}, the hyperbolic relational losses shape LoRA representations that improve generative VQA (GQA 61.03\% vs.\ 57.21\% for LoRA fine-tuning without relational losses, recovering and surpassing the baseline); and (2) as an \emph{inference-time relational encoder}, hyperbolic prefix tokens boost discriminative compositional scoring (SugarCrepe 79.94\%, $+$6.25pp over baseline). The learned curvature stabilises at $κ{=}4.0$, an order of magnitude above prior hyperbolic VLMs where $κ$ typically collapses toward zero, indicating that continuous visual features genuinely require the exponential volume of strongly curved space. A controlled Euclidean ablation confirms this decomposition: the relational pipeline regularises LoRA comparably in flat space (GQA 60.81\%), but the compositionality gain is specifically hyperbolic (SugarCrepe $+$4.58pp over Euclidean), with entailment loss ${\sim}6{\times}$ higher in Euclidean training. Codes are available at TBA.

AI.tldr: HyperVis improves VLM compositional reasoning by replacing discrete scene graph triplets with a continuous hyperbolic visual relation tensor, serving both as a training-time regularizer (boosting GQA to 61.03%) and an inference-time relational encoder (boosting SugarCrepe to 79.94%).
AI.motivation: VLMs fail at compositional reasoning (inter-object relationships). Using discrete scene graph triplets from SGG backfires (GQA drops from 60.38% to 58.86%) due to modality collision between discrete labels and continuous visual features.
AI.method: HyperVis computes a dense O(N²) visual relation tensor via spatially-biased cross-attention from class-agnostic region proposals, projects it onto a Lorentz hyperboloid, and enforces hierarchy through IoA-driven entailment cones and exterior-angle repulsion. It serves dual roles: training-time regularizer (via hyperbolic losses shaping LoRA) and inference-time relational encoder (via hyperbolic prefix tokens).
AI.result: GQA: 61.03% (vs. 57.21% LoRA baseline). SugarCrepe: 79.94% (+6.25pp). Learned curvature κ=4.0 (vs. near-zero in prior hyperbolic VLMs). Euclidean ablation confirms compositionality gain is specifically hyperbolic (+4.58pp over Euclidean), with entailment loss ~6× higher in Euclidean training.
AI.conclusion: Continuous visual features require strongly curved hyperbolic space (κ=4.0) for compositional reasoning. Hyperbolic relational losses recover and surpass baseline VLM performance, with the compositionality benefit being specifically hyperbolic rather than general to relation modeling.

title: A Framework for Measuring Appropriate Reliance on Set-Valued AI Advice
summary: Appropriate reliance on AI advice has become a central research theme in human-AI collaboration. Existing frameworks have focused exclusively on point predictions as AI advice. However, set-valued AI advice (e.g., discrete sets or continuous intervals) is increasingly being used to communicate uncertainty and improve human decision making. In this paper, we develop the first formal framework for measuring appropriate reliance on set-valued AI advice within the sequential judge-advisor paradigm, spanning both classification and regression tasks. For classification, we first introduce the dimensions that are necessary for evaluating set-valued AI advice. We then define two metrics: correct reliance rate on AI and correct reliance rate on self, which jointly characterize appropriate reliance in this setting. For regression, we introduce quantity of AI reliance and quality of AI reliance, which respectively measure whether a decision maker utilized the AI advice and whether their reliance helped them get closer to the ground truth relative to their initial estimate. Through the application of our framework, we demonstrate how these metrics capture important nuances in human-AI collaboration that existing measures overlook.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the first formal framework for measuring appropriate reliance on set-valued AI advice (e.g., intervals or discrete sets) in human-AI collaboration, proposing new metrics for classification (correct reliance rate on AI/self) and regression (quantity and quality of AI reliance).
AI.motivation: Existing frameworks for measuring appropriate reliance on AI advice focus exclusively on point predictions, but set-valued AI advice (e.g., intervals or discrete sets) is increasingly used to communicate uncertainty. No formal framework existed to evaluate how humans appropriately rely on such uncertainty-aware advice.
AI.method: The authors develop a formal framework within the sequential judge-advisor paradigm. For classification, they define dimensions for evaluating set-valued advice and propose two metrics: correct reliance rate on AI and correct reliance rate on self. For regression, they introduce quantity of AI reliance (whether advice was used) and quality of AI reliance (whether reliance improved accuracy toward ground truth).
AI.result: The framework captures important nuances in human-AI collaboration that existing measures overlook, demonstrated through application of the proposed metrics.
AI.conclusion: Set-valued AI advice requires new, dedicated metrics for appropriate reliance, and the proposed framework fills this gap by providing formal definitions that differentiate between classification and regression tasks.

title: Steering Vectors are an Adversarial Attack Surface
summary: Activation steering has become a popular way to control Large Language Model (LLM) behavior without fine-tuning. Since the technique is plug-and-play, users share datasets and precomputed vectors to steer model activations. However, we show that a \emph{stealth data poisoning attack} silently compromises this pipeline. By substituting $4{-}6\%$ of tokens in the steering dataset, an attacker can silently align the resulting vector with an anti-refusal direction. This jailbreaks the target model while preserving the intended steering effect on benign prompts. Under this threat model, a malicious actor can distribute an apparently safe bundle containing texts, vectors, and weights, alongside an equivalence certificate that the end-user can verify. We test the attack on two open-weight model families and eight model-attribute combinations, observing that poisoned vectors reach an absolute attack success rate (ASR) of $20{-}55\%$, $+19\%$ to $+51\%$ over a clean reference. Finally, we find that a refusal-direction orthogonalization defense can recover ${\approx}82\%$ of the ASR gap without harming benign behavior.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that activation steering in LLMs is vulnerable to stealth data poisoning attacks, where swapping 4-6% of tokens can secretly create jailbreak vectors that bypass safety mechanisms while appearing benign.
AI.motivation: To expose a security vulnerability in the activation steering pipeline, where users share steering vectors and datasets, enabling attackers to distribute compromised but apparently safe model control bundles.
AI.method: The authors perform a data poisoning attack by substituting 4-6% of tokens in steering datasets to create vectors aligned with anti-refusal directions, tested across two open-weight model families and eight configurations.
AI.result: Poisoned vectors achieve 20-55% absolute attack success rate (ASR), with +19% to +51% improvement over clean baselines. A refusal-direction orthogonalization defense recovers ~82% of the ASR gap without degrading benign behavior.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering pipelines are susceptible to stealth poisoning attacks, but the proposed orthogonalization defense can largely mitigate this vulnerability while maintaining utility.

title: MS-DKC: A Dataset Knowledge Card Framework for Designing and Adapting Medical Image Segmentation Models
summary:
Medical image segmentation is often framed as a search for stronger architectures, but this can obscure a more fundamental question: what does the dataset require from the model? In medical imaging, this requirement is shaped by foreground occupancy, morphology, boundary ambiguity, topology sensitivity, annotation quality, acquisition variation, and operating point.
  This paper introduces the Medical Segmentation Dataset Knowledge Card (MS-DKC), a framework for making these factors explicit. MS-DKC records dataset evidence through image/acquisition, morphology, supervision, context-dependence, and deployment-risk descriptors. These descriptors are mapped to failure modes, design priors, and risk-aligned criteria, making segmentation design more traceable than architecture-first comparison.
  We evaluate MS-DKC on DRIVE, ISIC2018, and ACDC, representing distinct regimes. DRIVE contains sparse, thin, branching vessels, favoring detail-preserving models, sensitivity-aware optimization, threshold analysis, and topology-aware metrics. DKC-TNet-v2 achieved Dice 0.8044 and IoU 0.6730 with 35103 parameters, while SA-UNetv2-DKC-AmbRef reached Dice 0.8141, IoU 0.6865, sensitivity 0.8265, specificity 0.9804, and AUC 0.9853. ISIC2018 involves compact but appearance-variable lesions; validation-constrained score-function selection on Att-Next-Topo/ATTNext produced MS-DKC-AttNextTopo-VCSF-NoAug with Dice 0.8872, IoU 0.8214, precision 0.9173, Boundary F1 0.4878, and ASSD 4.13, while plausible additions failed to improve the risk-aligned profile. ACDC provides a multi-class cardiac case, where MS-DKC recommends four-class softmax segmentation, class-balanced Dice/CE supervision, and class-wise surface evaluation. Overall, the results support dataset-conditioned design: different datasets require different priors, operating points, and evidence before a model can be judged appropriate.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MS-DKC, a framework that makes dataset requirements explicit for medical segmentation, moving beyond architecture-first comparisons by mapping dataset evidence to failure modes, design priors, and risk-aligned criteria. Evaluated on DRIVE, ISIC2018, and ACDC, results show different datasets demand different model design choices.
AI.motivation: Medical image segmentation research overly focuses on finding stronger architectures, ignoring the more fundamental question of what the dataset actually requires from a model. Factors like foreground occupancy, morphology, boundary ambiguity, topology sensitivity, annotation quality, acquisition variation, and operating point shape this requirement but are rarely made explicit.
AI.method: The MS-DKC framework records dataset evidence through five descriptor categories: image/acquisition, morphology, supervision, context-dependence, and deployment-risk. These descriptors are systematically mapped to failure modes, design priors, and risk-aligned criteria, enabling dataset-conditioned model design rather than architecture-first comparison.
AI.result: On DRIVE (vessel segmentation), DKC-TNet-v2 achieved Dice 0.8044 and IoU 0.6730, while SA-UNetv2-DKC-AmbRef reached Dice 0.8141, IoU 0.6865. On ISIC2018 (skin lesions), MS-DKC-AttNextTopo-VCSF-NoAug achieved Dice 0.8872, IoU 0.8214, and Boundary F1 0.4878. On ACDC (cardiac), MS-DKC recommends four-class softmax, class-balanced Dice/CE, and class-wise surface evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Different datasets require different priors, operating points, and evidence before a model can be judged appropriate. Dataset-conditioned design is superior to one-size-fits-all architecture comparisons, making segmentation design more traceable.

title: Beyond Semantic Organization: Memory as Execution State Management for Long-Horizon Agents
summary: LLM-based agents increasingly tackle long-horizon tasks with interdependent decisions, where each action reshapes future constraints and intermediate errors can cascade. Existing RAG and agent memory systems organize histories by semantic similarity, retrieving content-relevant entries at decision time. We argue that this design mismatches execution-state dependencies: it fragments decision trajectories and mixes valid and erroneous traces, hindering coherent state reconstruction and error isolation. We propose MAGE (Memory as Agent-Guided Exploration), an active execution-state manager that stores interactions in a hierarchical state tree. The agent derives its state from the active root-to-current path, combining subgoal summaries, recent traces, and hints from prior branches. Four coupled operations maintain the tree: Grow records new traces, Compress summarizes completed subgoals, Maintain validates summaries, and Revise restores a target boundary and resumes on a new branch. This design bounds context growth while preserving state integrity and isolating flawed segments from the active path. Experiments on MemoryArena show that MAGE improves the average task success rate by 7.8--20.4 pp over baselines, while reducing token consumption by 55.1%.

AI.tldr: MAGE replaces semantic-similarity-based memory with a hierarchical state tree that actively manages execution-state dependencies, improving task success by 7.8-20.4 pp while cutting token use by 55.1%.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG and agent memory systems organize histories by semantic similarity, which mismatches execution-state dependencies—fragmenting decision trajectories and mixing valid/erroneous traces, hindering coherent state reconstruction and error isolation.
AI.method: MAGE stores interactions in a hierarchical state tree with four coupled operations: Grow (records new traces), Compress (summarizes completed subgoals), Maintain (validates summaries), and Revise (restores a target boundary and resumes on a new branch). The agent derives state from the active root-to-current path.
AI.result: On MemoryArena, MAGE improves average task success rate by 7.8-20.4 percentage points over baselines and reduces token consumption by 55.1%.
AI.conclusion: An active execution-state manager that preserves state integrity and isolates flawed segments outperforms semantic-similarity-based memory for long-horizon agent tasks, both in success rate and efficiency.

title: LLM Explainability with Counterfactual Chains and Causal Graphs
summary: Causal graphs provide a high-level language for making mechanisms transparent. Recent work uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to recover causal graphs of external-world processes. Instead, in this paper, we use causal graphs to model LLM inference itself, providing stakeholders with a transparent view of how the model perceives and organizes high-level concepts to produce a prediction. We propose a four-phase method for constructing such graphs. Given a target LLM and a set of textual examples, our method discovers class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts and maps each input to LLM-perceived concept states. We then introduce an MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation procedure that expands the sparse observational data through chains of counterfactuals. This enables stable causal discovery with $σ$-CG, yielding informative, interpretable graphs. We apply our method to three LLMs across disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge classification tasks. We evaluate the learned graphs for predictive fidelity and structural stability, and the MCMC-inspired augmentation for convergence and downstream utility. Our results show that the discovered causal graphs capture meaningful dependencies consistent with LLMs' reasoning. Together, this paper provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a four-phase method to construct causal graphs that model LLM inference itself, enabling transparent, concept-level explainability of how LLMs perceive and organize high-level concepts for prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing work uses LLMs to recover causal graphs of external-world processes, but none model LLM inference internally. The authors aim to provide stakeholders with a transparent view of how LLMs perceive and organize high-level concepts to produce predictions.
AI.method: A four-phase method: (1) discover class-discriminative, human-interpretable concepts, (2) map inputs to LLM-perceived concept states, (3) apply MCMC-inspired counterfactual augmentation to expand sparse observational data, and (4) perform causal discovery with σ-CG to yield informative, interpretable causal graphs.
AI.result: Applied to three LLMs across disease diagnosis, sentiment analysis, and LLM-as-a-judge tasks. Evaluated for predictive fidelity, structural stability, convergence, and downstream utility. The discovered causal graphs capture meaningful dependencies consistent with LLMs' reasoning.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundation for concept-level explainability of LLMs by modeling their internal inference processes as causal graphs.

title: Where, What, Why, and Importance: Structured Defect Grounding for Text-to-Image Feedback
summary: Despite generating increasingly photorealistic images, text-to-image (T2I) models still exhibit localized, subtle, and structurally complex failures. Diagnosing these failures requires instance-level feedback that answers where a defect occurs, what type it is, why it is defective, and its importance to overall image quality. While recent dense-feedback methods move beyond scalar supervision, their heatmap-centric representations still formulate diagnosis as pixel-field regression, making it difficult to localize variable-cardinality defects and bind semantic reasons to individual failures. To address this representation bottleneck, we propose Structured Defect Grounding (SDG), which casts T2I diagnosis as structured set prediction by modeling each defect as a (location, type, reason, importance) tuple. To make this formulation trainable and measurable, we introduce SDG-30K, a 30K-image dataset with box-grounded annotations across four modern T2I generators, together with a dedicated evaluation protocol, SDG-Eval. Building on this structured representation, we further present a diagnosis-to-alignment framework in which a Vision-Language Model (VLM) serves as the SDG detector, and BoxFlow-GRPO converts predicted defect sets into box-derived, importance-weighted spatial rewards for diffusion model alignment. Extensive experiments show that our SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding, while SDG-guided rewards consistently improve T2I alignment and support localized image refinement. These results establish SDG as a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and enhancing modern generative models.

AI.tldr: SDG proposes a structured set prediction approach (location, type, reason, importance) for diagnosing defects in text-to-image models, outperforming proprietary VLMs and improving T2I alignment via a diagnosis-to-alignment framework.
AI.motivation: Existing T2I diagnosis methods use heatmap-centric representations that formulate diagnosis as pixel-field regression, making it difficult to localize variable-cardinality defects and bind semantic reasons to individual failures.
AI.method: SDG casts T2I diagnosis as structured set prediction with each defect represented as (location, type, reason, importance) tuple; introduces SDG-30K dataset with box-grounded annotations; uses VLM as SDG detector; employs BoxFlow-GRPO for diffusion model alignment via importance-weighted spatial rewards.
AI.result: SDG detector outperforms leading proprietary VLMs on structured defect grounding; SDG-guided rewards consistently improve T2I alignment and support localized image refinement.
AI.conclusion: SDG establishes a unified, instance-level interface for diagnosing, evaluating, and enhancing modern generative models.

title: Integrating Mechanistic and Data-Driven Models for Neurological Disorders through Differentiable Programming
summary: Advances in computational modeling, neuroimaging, and artificial intelligence are revolutionizing the modeling of neurological disorders for improved diagnostics, prognosis, and treatment planning. Mechanistic models provide valuable scientific insight into the disorders, but in practice they are often simplified with assumptions or computationally expensive and slow to solve. However, while purely data driven approaches provide speed and scalability, they require large, high quality data to train and generally suffer from interpretability and generalization issues. This perspective paper presents a structured overview of hybrid modeling strategies, which combine deep learning models with physics based solvers, and are categorized into parallel, series, and parallel-series architectures. Three main approaches that have been emphasized are residual modeling for missing or incomplete physics, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs) for continuous time dynamics approximation, and solver in the loop that accelerates traditional solvers with neural approximations. These hybrid models integrate the governing differential equation based formulations and deep learning to characterize the evolution of neurological disorders, and promise advanced personalized neurological modeling. In addition, the study explores and proposes different hybrid configurations to improve diagnosis accuracy, predict disease progression, and inform treatment strategies across a range of neurological disorders. These capabilities outperform standalone mechanistic or purely data driven approaches, making hybrid modeling a powerful tool, especially in applications involving modeling the progression and treatment responses in neurological conditions such as brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke.

AI.tldr: This perspective paper reviews hybrid modeling strategies that combine deep learning with physics-based solvers for neurological disorder modeling, categorizing them into parallel, series, and parallel-series architectures. Key approaches include residual modeling, Neural ODEs, and solver-in-the-loop methods, demonstrating superiority over purely mechanistic or data-driven approaches for conditions like brain tumors, Alzheimer's, and stroke.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic models offer scientific insight but are computationally expensive and rely on simplifying assumptions, while purely data-driven approaches lack interpretability and generalization. The motivation is to bridge this gap by combining both approaches for improved neurological disorder modeling.
AI.method: The paper presents a structured overview of hybrid modeling strategies categorized into parallel, series, and parallel-series architectures, emphasizing three main approaches: residual modeling for missing/incomplete physics, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (NODEs) for continuous time dynamics, and solver-in-the-loop methods that accelerate traditional solvers with neural approximations.
AI.result: Hybrid models outperform standalone mechanistic or purely data-driven approaches, showing improved diagnostic accuracy, better prediction of disease progression, and more informed treatment strategies for neurological disorders such as brain tumors, Alzheimer's disease, and stroke.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid modeling integrating differential equation-based formulations with deep learning promises advanced personalized neurological modeling, particularly for modeling disease progression and treatment responses across various neurological conditions.

title: Compress-Distill: Reasoning Trace Compression for Efficient Knowledge Distillation
summary: Reasoning models produce long chain-of-thought traces that are costly to distill and encourage verbose student outputs. We study post-hoc compression of such traces before knowledge distillation. Two teachers, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B, generate about 283k correct traces each; two instruction-tuned models then compress them to 8.6-21.0% of their original character length. Across a 48-run main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, compressed traces reduce training tokens to 12-30% of raw, speed up training by 2.0-7.6x, and shorten inference outputs by 3-19x with smaller reductions under the shorter gpt-oss teacher. However, raw traces retain the highest downstream accuracy at every scale and for both teachers. A length-matched raw-trace truncation ablation shows that compression is not merely benefiting from a smaller token budget: model-compressed traces usually beat or match naive truncation, especially for smaller students, while maintaining shorter inference outputs. Overall, reasoning-trace compression offers an accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a free improvement: students retain up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy while gaining up to 18x higher per-token efficiency, and at the 0.8B scale under LoRA compressed traces narrow the raw-vs-compressed gap but do not exceed raw.

AI.tldr: This paper studies compressing long chain-of-thought traces from reasoning models before knowledge distillation. Using two large teachers (Qwen3.5-397B and gpt-oss-120B), they compress traces to 8.6-21.0% of original length, achieving 2.0-7.6x faster training and 3-19x shorter inference outputs. However, raw (uncompressed) traces maintain the highest accuracy. Compression offers a trade-off: students retain up to 96% accuracy while gaining up to 18x per-token efficiency, but it's not a free improvement.
AI.motivation: Reasoning models generate long chain-of-thought traces that are costly to distill and encourage verbose student outputs. The authors aim to study whether post-hoc compression of these traces before distillation can reduce costs while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: Two teachers (Qwen3.5-397B-A17B and gpt-oss-120B) generate ~283k correct traces each. Two instruction-tuned models compress them to 8.6-21.0% of original length. They run a 48-experiment main grid plus seven Qwen-teacher truncation ablations, comparing compressed vs raw traces and vs length-matched naive truncation.
AI.result: Compressed traces reduce training tokens to 12-30% of raw, speed training by 2.0-7.6x, and shorten inference outputs by 3-19x. Raw traces retain highest accuracy at all scales. Model-compressed traces usually beat or match naive truncation, especially for smaller students. Students retain up to 96% of raw-trace accuracy while gaining up to 18x per-token efficiency. At 0.8B scale under LoRA, compressed traces narrow the gap but don't exceed raw.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-trace compression offers an accuracy-efficiency trade-off rather than a free improvement. It can significantly reduce training and inference costs while retaining most of the accuracy benefits of raw traces, but raw traces remain superior for maximizing downstream accuracy.

title: Diff-CA: Separating Common and Salient Factors with Diffusion Models
summary: Contrastive Analysis aims to separate factors that are common between two data distributions from those that are salient to only one of them. Existing contrastive methods are based on generative models (e.g., VAEs or GANs) that often suffer from limited reconstruction and image quality, which hampers effective latent factor separation and limits their applicability to high-fidelity image generation and edition. We propose a novel conditioning framework for diffusion models that enables contrastive decomposition without compromising generation quality. We first train a prompt-free, image-conditioned diffusion model, and then learn to decompose the conditioning into a common and a salient factor, using weak supervision. We prove that the additive contrastive factorization, commonly assumed in prior work, is identifiable under mild conditions. This factorization enables targeted operations by swapping or interpolating only the salient factor.

AI.tldr: A conditioning framework for diffusion models that enables contrastive decomposition of common and salient factors between data distributions, with theoretically grounded identifiability of additive factorization.
AI.motivation: Existing contrastive methods rely on generative models (VAEs/GANs) that suffer from limited reconstruction and image quality, hampering effective latent factor separation and limiting high-fidelity image generation and edition.
AI.method: 1) Train a prompt-free, image-conditioned diffusion model; 2) Learn to decompose conditioning into common and salient factors using weak supervision; 3) Apply additive contrastive factorization with proven identifiability under mild conditions.
AI.result: Enables targeted operations like swapping or interpolating only the salient factor while preserving generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models can be effectively adapted for contrastive decomposition, overcoming the quality limitations of prior VAE/GAN-based approaches.

title: CogManip: Benchmarking Manipulative Behavior in Multi-Turn Interactions with Large Language Model
summary: Whether Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit covert psychological manipulation in complex human-AI interactions has garnered increasing safety concerns. However, existing AI safety benchmarks remain largely restricted to explicit rule compliance and static prompts, failing to capture the dynamic and covert nature of manipulative strategies in multi-turn dialogues. We introduce CogManip, a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates 15 manipulation strategy risks across 1,000 multi-turn interaction scenarios, validated by human experts. A systematic evaluation of 13 representative models, including frontier models like GPT-5.4 and DeepSeek-V3.2, reveals significant risk heterogeneities and illuminates the targeted direction for future defense. Further analysis of objective function perturbation reveals that DeepSeek-V3.2's manipulation tactics are highly sensitive to both negative and benign system prompts, demonstrating the critical necessity of prompt-based defense engineering and implicit goal auditing. CogManip offers a robust instrument and perspective for auditing the implicit psychological influence and dynamic strategy selection of modern LLMs.

AI.tldr: CogManip is a new benchmark for evaluating covert psychological manipulation risks in LLMs across 1,000 multi-turn scenarios, revealing significant risk differences across 13 models and showing that DeepSeek-V3.2's manipulation tactics are sensitive to system prompt perturbations.
AI.motivation: Existing AI safety benchmarks focus on explicit rule compliance and static prompts, failing to capture the dynamic and covert nature of manipulative strategies in multi-turn dialogues between humans and LLMs.
AI.method: Developed CogManip benchmark covering 15 manipulation strategies across 1,000 multi-turn interaction scenarios, validated by human experts. Evaluated 13 models including GPT-5.4 and DeepSeek-V3.2, and performed objective function perturbation analysis on DeepSeek-V3.2.
AI.result: Significant risk heterogeneities observed across 13 models; DeepSeek-V3.2's manipulation tactics highly sensitive to both negative and benign system prompts.
AI.conclusion: CogManip provides a robust instrument for auditing implicit psychological influence and dynamic strategy selection of modern LLMs, highlighting the critical need for prompt-based defense engineering and implicit goal auditing.

title: Computation-Aware Event-to-Frame Reconstruction via Selective Attention
summary: Event-to-frame (E2F) reconstruction bridges asynchronous event streams with frame-based vision pipelines, but existing methods often face a trade-off between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency. In this work, we propose an efficient E2F framework that emphasizes causal temporal modeling and computation-aware design. The architecture adopts a recurrent encoder-decoder to incrementally aggregate event information with compact hidden states. To improve robustness under fast motion and illumination variations, a selective context fusion strategy is introduced to integrate event-driven features with prior intensity cues. Within this fusion process, a lightweight hybrid attention mechanism enhances feature selectivity without relying on heavy attention operations. Experimental results on standard benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves competitive reconstruction performance while maintaining a favorable balance between accuracy and model complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an efficient event-to-frame reconstruction framework that uses a recurrent encoder-decoder with selective context fusion and lightweight hybrid attention to balance reconstruction quality with computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing event-to-frame reconstruction methods face a trade-off between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, motivating the need for a causally-aware, computation-efficient framework.
AI.method: The method adopts a recurrent encoder-decoder for incremental event aggregation with compact hidden states, a selective context fusion strategy integrating event-driven features with prior intensity cues, and a lightweight hybrid attention mechanism for enhanced feature selectivity.
AI.result: Competitive reconstruction performance on standard benchmarks while maintaining a favorable accuracy-complexity trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach achieves a good balance between reconstruction accuracy and model complexity, making it suitable for efficient event-to-frame reconstruction.

title: Step-adaptive multimodal fusion network with multi-scale cloud feature learning for ultra-short-term solar irradiance forecasting
summary: Ultra-short-term solar irradiance prediction is critical for photovoltaic system dispatch and power grid stability. Existing approaches suffer from three key shortcomings: single time-series models cannot capture the spatial dynamics of clouds under complex conditions, standard convolutions inadequately represent multi-scale cloud features, and fixed low-frequency compensation strategies fail to adapt to different prediction steps. To address these issues, this proposes a multi-source data fusion model for ultra-short-term irradiance prediction. The model first employs InceptionNeXt to extract multi-scale, multi-directional spatial features from ground-based cloud images. A step-adaptive low-frequency compensation unit is then introduced to dynamically modulate global low-frequency information based on the prediction step. Eventually, the enhanced image features are combined with meteorological time-series features, and a TempAttnLSTM network captures global temporal dependencies for multi-step prediction. Experiments on the public NREL dataset and practical photovoltaic stations in Shandong illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with several state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-source data fusion model for ultra-short-term solar irradiance prediction that uses InceptionNeXt for multi-scale cloud feature extraction, a step-adaptive low-frequency compensation unit, and TempAttnLSTM for temporal modeling, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on NREL and practical datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for ultra-short-term solar irradiance prediction have three key shortcomings: (1) single time-series models cannot capture spatial cloud dynamics, (2) standard convolutions inadequately represent multi-scale cloud features, and (3) fixed low-frequency compensation strategies fail to adapt to different prediction steps.
AI.method: The model has three main components: (1) InceptionNeXt to extract multi-scale, multi-directional spatial features from ground-based cloud images; (2) a step-adaptive low-frequency compensation unit that dynamically modulates global low-frequency information based on prediction step; (3) a TempAttnLSTM network that fuses enhanced image features with meteorological time-series data for global temporal dependency modeling and multi-step prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on the public NREL dataset and practical photovoltaic stations in Shandong demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method compared with several state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-source data fusion model successfully addresses spatial dynamics capture, multi-scale feature representation, and adaptive frequency compensation for ultra-short-term solar irradiance prediction, showing superior performance over existing methods.

title: Diffusion Models for Adaptive Sequential Data Generation
summary:
Generating realistic synthetic sequential data is critical in real-world applications across operations research, finance, healthcare, energy systems, and scientific computing, where time-indexed observations are used for prediction, simulation, risk assessment, and data-driven decision-making. While diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in generating static data, their direct extensions to sequential settings often fail to capture temporal dependence and information structure. Designing diffusion models that can simulate sequential data in an adapted manner, and hence without anticipation of future information, therefore remains an open challenge.
  In this work, we propose a sequential forward-backward diffusion framework for adapted time series generation. Our approach progressively injects and removes noise along the sequence, conditioning on the previously generated history to ensure adaptiveness. A novel score-matching objective is introduced for efficient parallel training. We derive rigorous statistical guarantees under a generic framework, then establish score approximation, score estimation, and distribution estimation results with ReLU networks serving as a concrete instance. Empirically, we validate our method on synthetic data, including ARMA models and Gaussian processes, and demonstrate its effectiveness in constructing mean-variance optimal portfolios.

AI.tldr: Proposes a sequential forward-backward diffusion framework for adapted time series generation that preserves temporal dependence without anticipation, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation on ARMA, GPs, and portfolio optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion models generate static data well but fail to capture temporal dependence and information structure in sequential settings; generating adapted time series without anticipation of future information remains an open challenge.
AI.method: A sequential forward-backward diffusion framework that progressively injects and removes noise along the sequence, conditioning on previously generated history. A novel score-matching objective enables efficient parallel training.
AI.result: Derives rigorous statistical guarantees (score approximation, score estimation, distribution estimation) with ReLU networks. Validated on synthetic ARMA models, Gaussian processes, and mean-variance optimal portfolio construction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully generates adapted sequential data while preserving temporal dependencies, with both theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.

title: Adaptive Tokenisation Via Temporal Redundancy Masking And Latent Inpainting
summary:
Adaptive video tokenisation seeks to dynamically allocate token budgets based on the underlying visual complexity of a sequence. Current continuous-regime approaches achieve this via iterative binarised searches or trained neural regressors, while discrete methods often require a full-rate decoder pass to estimate information content. We demonstrate that such computational overheads are not strictly necessary. We show that the latent space of a frozen continuous video tokeniser inherently encodes temporal redundancy that can be exploited directly: spatial positions whose latent representations change minimally between consecutive frames carry near-zero additional information.
  We introduce a parameter-free adaptive token allocation mechanism that applies a fixed threshold to per-position temporal-L1 differences, identifying and dropping redundant latent positions. Consequently, the compression rate emerges naturally from the input content rather than being enforced top-down: static scenes get compressed aggressively, while highly dynamic sequences retain more tokens. To reconstruct the dropped positions, we propose the Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT), a lightweight factorised spatial-temporal attention architecture. The resulting inference pipeline is highly efficient, requiring only a single encoder pass and one LIT forward pass, eliminating the need for auxiliary routing networks. Evaluations across TokenBench and DAVIS, which are the standard benchmarks used by recent tokenisers~\cite{infotok, agarwal2025cosmos}, indicate that our framework yields meaningful, content-driven token allocation while maintaining competitive reconstruction fidelity, and delivers a $31\times$ inference-time speedup over the continuous adaptive baseline (ElasticTok-CV) and an $\approx2\times$ speedup over the discrete information-theoretic baseline (InfoTok)

AI.tldr: A parameter-free adaptive video tokenisation method that exploits temporal redundancy in latent space, dropping redundant tokens based on L1 differences and reconstructing them with a lightweight Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT), achieving 31× speedup over ElasticTok-CV and 2× over InfoTok.
AI.motivation: Existing adaptive video tokenisation methods (iterative binarised searches, trained regressors, or full-rate decoder passes) introduce unnecessary computational overhead. The authors argue that the latent space of a frozen continuous video tokeniser already encodes temporal redundancy that can be exploited more directly and cheaply.
AI.method: A parameter-free mechanism: applies a fixed threshold to per-position temporal-L1 differences in the latent space of a frozen continuous video tokeniser to identify and drop redundant positions. Dropped positions are reconstructed by the Latent Inpainting Transformer (LIT), a lightweight factorised spatial-temporal attention architecture. Requires only one encoder pass and one LIT forward pass.
AI.result: Achieves content-driven token allocation (aggressive compression on static scenes, more tokens for dynamic sequences) with competitive reconstruction fidelity. Delivers 31× inference-time speedup over ElasticTok-CV and ≈2× speedup over InfoTok on TokenBench and DAVIS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Computational overheads of prior adaptive tokenisation methods are unnecessary. The proposed approach offers a simple, efficient, and content-aware alternative that eliminates auxiliary routing networks and multiple passes, while maintaining reconstruction quality.

title: Towards Healthy Evolution: Exploring the Role and Mechanisms of Human-Agent Interaction in Self-Evolving Systems
summary: Self-evolving agents improve through continual self-play and self-generated learning signals, but autonomous evolution can also cause capability degradation and safety drift. Although human feedback has proven effective for static and post-trained agents, its role in self-evolving systems remains underexplored. We introduce Agent Norm Correction through Human-like Oversight and Review (ANCHOR), an LLM-based framework that simulates human supervision and delivers feedback at various phases of self-evolution. With ANCHOR, we evaluate two representative open-source self-evolving agent systems across coding, mathematical reasoning, and safety. Our results show that even limited supervision substantially mitigates safety degradation while preserving stable performance on core evolutionary objectives. Further analysis shows that supervision over the output verification phase is the most effective for intervention, whereas increasing supervision frequency yields diminishing returns. These findings provide empirical evidence and practical guidance for designing more stable, controllable, and human-aligned self-evolving agent systems.

AI.tldr: ANCHOR is an LLM-based framework that simulates human oversight to mitigate safety degradation and capability drift in self-evolving agent systems, demonstrating that even limited supervision during output verification effectively preserves safety and performance.
AI.motivation: Self-evolving agents can suffer from capability degradation and safety drift during autonomous evolution, and the role of human feedback in such systems is underexplored.
AI.method: ANCHOR (Agent Norm Correction through Human-like Oversight and Review) is an LLM-based framework that simulates human supervision and delivers feedback at various phases of self-evolution. The authors evaluated it on two open-source self-evolving agent systems across coding, mathematical reasoning, and safety tasks.
AI.result: Limited supervision substantially mitigates safety degradation while preserving stable performance; supervision over output verification is the most effective intervention phase; increasing supervision frequency yields diminishing returns.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide empirical evidence and practical guidance for designing more stable, controllable, and human-aligned self-evolving agent systems.

title: Adaptive Oscillatory-State Alignment for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Long-term time series forecasting benefits from inductive biases that expose recurring temporal structure. Existing periodic forecasting methods typically model recurrence through predefined periods, global spectral components, or fixed learnable templates. However, real-world temporal dynamics are rarely rigidly periodic: oscillatory behavior often evolves through amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation. Under these conditions, fixed-template periodic modeling can become fundamentally mismatched to the underlying temporal states. We propose AOSNET, a Hilbert-guided forecasting framework that reformulates periodic forecasting from fixed template matching to adaptive oscillatory-state alignment. AOSNET extracts analytic-signal descriptors from both the observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior, then adaptively aligns local states through a descriptor-conditioned gate that selectively preserves reliable observations while softly correcting mismatched regions. The learned prior serves not as a rigid repeated template but as a flexible oscillatory reference interpreted through local state dynamics. Experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy with fast inference speed. Controlled synthetic studies isolating amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation confirm that the advantage of oscillatory-state alignment consistently increases as non-stationarity intensifies.

AI.tldr: AOSNET is a Hilbert-guided framework for long-term time series forecasting that replaces fixed-template periodic matching with adaptive oscillatory-state alignment, achieving SOTA results on 8 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Real-world temporal dynamics are rarely rigidly periodic — they exhibit amplitude modulation, phase drift, and local frequency variation. Existing fixed-template periodic methods become fundamentally mismatched to evolving oscillatory states.
AI.method: AOSNET extracts analytic-signal descriptors (via Hilbert transform) from both the observed sequence and a learnable global oscillatory prior, then adaptively aligns local states using a descriptor-conditioned gate that selectively preserves reliable observations while softly correcting mismatched regions.
AI.result: State-of-the-art or highly competitive accuracy on 8 benchmarks with fast inference speed. Synthetic studies confirm the advantage grows as non-stationarity (amplitude modulation, phase drift, frequency variation) intensifies.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive oscillatory-state alignment outperforms rigid periodic template matching for forecasting non-stationary real-world time series, with greater benefits under stronger non-stationarity.

title: RQUL-UIE: Revitalizing Quality-Unstable Labels for Underwater Image Enhancement via In-Dataset Self-Supervision
summary: Underwater Image Enhancement (UIE) is essential for mitigating degradations caused by water medium. Although learning-based methods have advanced significantly, most rely on paired datasets with unstable label quality, which bottlenecks model performance. This paper proposes a diffusion-based, in-dataset self-supervised learning strategy designed to exploit the quality distribution of training labels. Specifically, we evaluate label quality via semantic perception embeddings from a pre-trained diffusion model in a training-free manner. These quality scores are subsequently quantized into noise-level indices, guiding a multi-step denoising process for level-wise supervision. This mechanism prevents low-quality labels from degrading the model while maximizing their utility during training. Furthermore, a Fourier-based refinement network is incorporated to explicitly reconstruct high-frequency components. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms SOTA approaches in restoration quality. The code and pre-trained model will be available once accepted in link.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based self-supervised learning strategy for underwater image enhancement that evaluates label quality via semantic perception embeddings, uses quality-guided noise-level indexing for multi-step denoising, and incorporates Fourier-based refinement to reconstruct high-frequency components, outperforming SOTA methods.
AI.motivation: Existing learning-based UIE methods depend on paired datasets with unstable label quality, which bottlenecks model performance. The authors aim to exploit the quality distribution of training labels to prevent low-quality labels from degrading the model.
AI.method: 1) Evaluates label quality via semantic perception embeddings from a pre-trained diffusion model in a training-free manner. 2) Quantizes quality scores into noise-level indices for multi-step denoising with level-wise supervision. 3) Uses a Fourier-based refinement network to reconstruct high-frequency components.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in restoration quality based on extensive evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed in-dataset self-supervised learning strategy effectively handles label quality variability in UIE training, maximizing utility of low-quality labels while preventing model degradation, achieving superior performance.

title: WorldFly: A World-Model-Based Vision-Language-Action Model for UAV Navigation
summary: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown promise in UAV navigation. However, existing approaches typically rely on historical observations to directly predict actions, often struggling in dense urban environments where severe occlusions and sharp turns result in drastic viewpoint transitions. We argue that the ability to "imagine" future states -- inherent in World Models -- is critical for robust decision-making under such partial observability. To address this, we construct a challenging Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark, specifically designed to evaluate spatial understanding in scenarios characterized by severe occlusions and drastic viewpoint transitions. To this end, we propose WorldFly, a novel world-model-based VLA framework that employs a dual-branch coupled flow matching mechanism to jointly generate future video predictions and navigation actions, thereby explicitly guiding the agent's policy via spatial imagination. Extensive evaluations on our benchmark demonstrate that WorldFly outperforms other baselines, particularly in unseen environments, validating the effectiveness of integrating world models into embodied aerial agents.

AI.tldr: WorldFly is a VLA framework combining world models and flow matching to improve UAV navigation in dense urban environments by jointly predicting future videos and actions.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models struggle in dense urban environments with occlusions and sharp turns due to drastic viewpoint transitions; the authors argue that imagining future states (world models) is critical for robust decision-making under partial observability.
AI.method: WorldFly uses a dual-branch coupled flow matching mechanism to jointly generate future video predictions and navigation actions, guiding the agent's policy via spatial imagination.
AI.result: WorldFly outperforms other baselines on the proposed Urban Canyon Traversal Benchmark, especially in unseen environments.
AI.conclusion: Integrating world models into embodied aerial agents improves navigation performance in challenging urban environments with occlusions and viewpoint transitions.

title: OPRD: On-Policy Representation Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) supervises the student only in output space by matching next-token probabilities. This output-only paradigm has two limits: (1) sampling variance from Monte Carlo KL estimates over large vocabularies (e.g., Qwen's ~150k tokens) persists throughout training, and (2) it treats the teacher as a black-box, discarding all intermediate hidden states after the LM head. We propose On-Policy Representation Distillation (OPRD), which lifts distillation into hidden-state space by aligning student and teacher representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head entirely. Theoretically, OPRD eliminates sampling variance and provides richer per-layer structural information. Empirically, OPRD closes the student-teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO, while output-space OPD baselines plateau below the teacher. OPRD also trains 1.44x faster and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD. Code: https://github.com/ShenzhiYang2000/OPRD.

AI.tldr: OPRD improves knowledge distillation by aligning student and teacher hidden representations layer-by-layer during on-policy rollouts, eliminating sampling variance and outperforming standard output-space on-policy distillation.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) suffers from sampling variance due to Monte Carlo KL estimates over large vocabularies and treats the teacher as a black-box by discarding intermediate hidden states, limiting student learning.
AI.method: OPRD performs representation distillation in hidden-state space by aligning student and teacher representations across selected layers on the same rollouts, bypassing the LM head entirely.
AI.result: OPRD closes the student-teacher gap on AIME 2024/2025 and AIMO benchmarks, trains 1.44x faster, and uses 54% less memory than top-k OPD.
AI.conclusion: Moving distillation from output space to hidden-state space eliminates sampling variance, provides richer per-layer structural information, and yields better performance with improved efficiency.

title: Adversarial Attacks Already Tell the Answer: Directional Bias-Guided Test-time Defense for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), such as CLIP, have shown strong zero-shot generalization but remain highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, posing serious risks in real-world applications. Test-time defenses for VLMs have recently emerged as a promising and efficient approach to defend against adversarial attacks without requiring costly large-scale retraining. In this work, we uncover a surprising phenomenon: under diverse input transformations, adversarial images in CLIP's feature space consistently shift along a dominant direction, in contrast to the dispersed patterns of clean images. We hypothesize that this dominant shift, termed the Defense Direction, opposes the adversarial shift, pointing features back toward their correct class centers. Building on this insight, we propose Directional Bias-guided Defense (DBD), a test-time framework that estimates the Defense Direction and employs a DB-score-based two-stream reconstruction strategy to recover robust representations. Experiments on 15 datasets demonstrate that DBD not only achieves SOTA adversarial robustness while preserving clean accuracy, but also reveals the counterintuitive result that adversarial accuracy can even surpass clean accuracy. This demonstrates that adversarial perturbations inherently encode directional priors about the true decision boundary.

AI.tldr: VLMs like CLIP are vulnerable to adversarial attacks. The paper discovers adversarial images shift along a dominant "Defense Direction" in CLIP's feature space, opposite to clean images. The authors propose DBD, a test-time defense using this direction and a two-stream reconstruction, achieving SOTA robustness where adversarial accuracy can even exceed clean accuracy.
AI.motivation: VLMs (e.g., CLIP) show strong zero-shot generalization but are highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, posing real-world risks. Existing defenses often require costly retraining, motivating the need for efficient test-time defenses.
AI.method: The paper uncovers that adversarial images in CLIP's feature space shift along a dominant direction (Defense Direction), unlike clean images. It proposes DBD: a test-time framework that estimates this Defense Direction and uses a DB-score-based two-stream reconstruction strategy to recover robust representations.
AI.result: Experiments on 15 datasets show DBD achieves state-of-the-art adversarial robustness while preserving clean accuracy. A surprising result: adversarial accuracy can even surpass clean accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial perturbations encode directional priors about the true decision boundary, and leveraging the Defense Direction enables effective test-time defense without costly retraining.

title: Amortizing Federated Adaptation: Hypernetwork Driven LoRA for Personalized Foundation Models
comment: Accepted at International Workshop on Federated Learning in the Age of Foundation Models In Conjunction with IJCAI 2026 (FL@FM-IJCAI'26)
summary: Federated fine-tuning of foundation models using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) offers a communication efficient solution for distributed learning. However, existing federated LoRA methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: (1) structural aggregation bias, where independently averaging low rank factors fails to approximate the true combined update, and (2) client side initialization lag, as clients repeatedly reinitialize LoRA parameters across communication rounds, slowing convergence. We propose HyperLoRA, a unified framework that addresses both issues through amortized federated adaptation through hypernetwork-driven LoRA generation and product space aggregation. Instead of iterative per-client optimization, HyperLoRA employs a learned generator that maps client distribution signatures to LoRA initializations, effectively amortizing per client adaptation. On the server side, we introduce a learned aggregation module that directly synthesizes updates in the low-rank product space, eliminating the inconsistencies of factor-wise averaging. A lightweight residual correction module further improves stability under heterogenous (non-IID) client distributions.By replacing iterative optimization and heuristic averaging with learned operators, HyperLoRA jointly enables efficient personalization, unbiased aggregation, and faster convergence. Experiments on federated vision and vision-language benchmarks show that HyperLoRA achieves improved convergence speed, greater robustness to distribution shift, and stronger personalization performance compared to prior federated LoRA methods.

AI.tldr: HyperLoRA addresses two key limitations in federated LoRA (structural aggregation bias and client-side initialization lag) by using a hypernetwork to generate LoRA initializations and a learned aggregation module for product-space synthesis, achieving faster convergence and better personalization.
AI.motivation: Existing federated LoRA methods suffer from (1) structural aggregation bias—averaging independent low-rank factors fails to approximate true combined updates, and (2) client-side initialization lag—repeated reinitialization of LoRA parameters slows convergence.
AI.method: HyperLoRA uses a learned generator (hypernetwork) to map client distribution signatures to LoRA initializations (amortized adaptation), a learned server-side aggregation module that synthesizes updates directly in the low-rank product space, plus a lightweight residual correction module for non-IID robustness.
AI.result: On federated vision and vision-language benchmarks, HyperLoRA achieves improved convergence speed, greater robustness to distribution shift, and stronger personalization compared to prior federated LoRA methods.
AI.conclusion: Replacing iterative optimization and heuristic averaging with learned operators enables efficient personalization, unbiased aggregation, and faster convergence in federated fine-tuning.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting as Accessibility Collapse: A Three-Level Framework for Knowledge Persistence in Continual Learning
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Sequential continual-learning experiments on CIFAR-100 using ResNet-18
summary: Catastrophic forgetting is commonly interpreted as the irreversible erasure of previously acquired knowledge during sequential learning. In this work, we investigate an alternative perspective: that forgetting may arise not from complete destruction of task representations but from a loss of accessibility to preserved information. We introduce a three-level framework separating knowledge storage, representation, and accessibility, and evaluate each component through a series of continual-learning experiments on sequential CIFAR-100 classification using ResNet-18. Our analysis combines checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry, classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability experiments. We observe complete behavioral forgetting of earlier tasks, with task accuracy collapsing from 54.8% to 0%, while linear probe performance retains approximately 76% of the original representational information. Furthermore, retraining only the final classifier restores 75.7% of the original task performance without modifying the backbone network. Layer-wise analysis reveals that early and intermediate layers preserve highly recoverable task information despite severe degradation at later stages. Projection-energy and principal-angle analyses indicate that retained knowledge persists as distributed high-dimensional representations rather than through preservation of a small dominant subspace. These findings suggest that catastrophic forgetting is better characterized as an accessibility failure than complete representational erasure, and that substantial task-relevant information remains embedded within neural representations even after functional forgetting has occurred.

AI.tldr: Catastrophic forgetting is mainly an accessibility failure rather than representational erasure; task-relevant knowledge persists in neural representations even when behavioral performance drops to zero.
AI.motivation: To challenge the conventional view that catastrophic forgetting involves irreversible erasure of knowledge, and to investigate whether forgotten information is actually still preserved but inaccessible.
AI.method: Proposes a three-level framework (storage, representation, accessibility); conducts continual learning experiments on sequential CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18 using checkpoint persistence, linear probing, representation geometry, classifier-reset recovery, and layer-wise recoverability analyses.
AI.result: Behavioral accuracy drops from 54.8% to 0%, but linear probing retains ~76% representational information; retraining only the final classifier restores 75.7% performance; early/intermediate layers preserve recoverable information, while later layers degrade; retained knowledge is distributed across high-dimensional representations rather than a dominant subspace.
AI.conclusion: Catastrophic forgetting is better characterized as a failure of accessibility rather than complete erasure, with substantial task-relevant information remaining embedded in neural representations even after functional forgetting.

title: SC-MFJ: A Simple Haptic Quality Metric for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Standard segmentation metrics such as Dice and Hausdorff distance measure geometric overlap but say nothing about whether a segmented surface is suitable for haptic rendering in surgical simulation. We propose SC-MFJ (Surface-Constrained Mean Force Jerk), a simple, inexpensive metric that samples a segmented organ surface with many short virtual stylus walks and measures how jerky the resulting contact forces are. The metric is computed from existing segmentation outputs and uses roughly one minute of CPU time per case. We evaluate three pancreas CT segmentation approaches-binary nnU-Net output, Gaussian-smoothed output, and learned signed distance function (SDF) regression-across 80 cases in five-fold cross-validation. SC-MFJ reveals a 147x gap in haptic quality between the raw binary baseline and simple Gaussian post-processing, a difference entirely invisible to Dice and HD95. It also shows that learned SDF regression, despite requiring full model retraining, produces more variable haptic quality than Gaussian smoothing, with a case-level standard deviation of 168 N/s2 compared with 22 N/s2 for Gaussian. A second evaluation on the LiTS liver dataset (131 cases) confirms the generality of these findings: the binary-to-Gaussian gap widens to 189x, and Gaussian smoothing again produces consistently low force jerk across all folds. Our results suggest that for haptic simulation applications, a one-line post-processing step may be sufficient, and that a cheap metric like SC-MFJ can flag problems that geometric metrics miss.

AI.tldr: The authors propose SC-MFJ, a simple metric to evaluate segmentation quality for haptic rendering in surgical simulation, revealing that Gaussian smoothing of binary nnU-Net outputs dramatically improves haptic quality (147-189x) while geometric metrics like Dice/HD95 show no difference.
AI.motivation: Standard segmentation metrics (Dice, HD95) measure geometric overlap but fail to assess whether a segmented surface is suitable for haptic rendering in surgical simulation, creating a gap in evaluation for this important application domain.
AI.method: SC-MFJ (Surface-Constrained Mean Force Jerk) samples a segmented organ surface with many short virtual stylus walks and measures jerkiness of resulting contact forces. It is computed from existing segmentation outputs with ~1 minute CPU time per case. Evaluated on pancreas CT (80 cases, 3 approaches) and LiTS liver (131 cases).
AI.result: SC-MFJ reveals a 147x gap in haptic quality between binary nnU-Net and Gaussian post-processing (invisible to Dice/HD95). Learned SDF regression shows higher variability (168 N/s² std) vs Gaussian (22 N/s²). LiTS confirms generality with 189x binary-to-Gaussian gap.
AI.conclusion: For haptic simulation, a simple Gaussian post-processing step may suffice, and SC-MFJ can flag problems that geometric metrics miss, serving as a cheap, informative complement to standard evaluation.

title: Where does Absolute Position come from in decoder-only Transformers?
summary: RoPE-trained transformers distinguish absolute position in their attention patterns, even though RoPE encodes only relative offsets in the inner product. We trace this leakage to two architectural components, The causal mask is responsible for the first: its per-query softmax denominator depends on the absolute query position by construction. The residual stream supplies the second. Under causal attention the activation at position $0$ attends only to itself and runs as a closed dynamical system from the embedding of the token at that position; downstream attention reads this trajectory through sink-reading heads. Both components appear in all three architectures we study, in architecturally specific balance: NTK scaling suppresses the residual-stream component, sliding-window attention allows it to accumulate with depth, and standard RoPE sits between. Replacing the \texttt{BOS} embedding before the forward pass removes $40\%$ of the residual-stream component at early queries. Attention sinks are token-anchored stabilizers that pass forward a deterministic fingerprint of the token at position $0$, constant across inputs when that token is the auto-prepended \texttt{BOS} and varying with it otherwise.

AI.tldr: RoPE-trained transformers leak absolute position information through the causal mask and residual stream, despite RoPE encoding only relative offsets. This leakage manifests in two forms: causal mask's query-dependent softmax denominator and sink-reading heads that read position-0's trajectory from the residual stream.
AI.motivation: To understand why RoPE-trained transformers exhibit absolute position sensitivity in attention patterns, despite RoPE theoretically encoding only relative positional information.
AI.method: The authors trace the leakage to two architectural components: (1) the causal mask, where the per-query softmax denominator depends on absolute query position, and (2) the residual stream, where position-0 acts as a closed dynamical system that downstream sink-reading heads can read. They compare three architectures: NTK scaling, sliding-window attention, and standard RoPE.
AI.result: Both leakage components appear in all architectures but in different balances. Replacing the BOS embedding before the forward pass removes 40% of the residual-stream component. Attention sinks are token-anchored stabilizers that pass forward a deterministic fingerprint of position-0's token, constant across inputs when that token is BOS and varying otherwise.
AI.conclusion: RoPE-trained transformers inevitably encode absolute position information through fundamental architectural constraints (causal mask and residual stream), with attention sinks serving as token-anchored stabilizers rather than just attention artifacts.

title: When Good Enough Is Optimal: Multiplication-Only Matrix Inversion Approximation for Quantized Gated DeltaNet
summary: Matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention is a major bottleneck for long-context modeling, particularly on NPUs, where forward-substitution-based methods exhibit limited parallelism and poor hardware utilization. We propose a fast, Matrix Multiplication (MatMul)-based algorithm tailored for strictly lower-triangular matrices arising in chunk-wise linear attention. Motivated by the rapid growth of Neumann-series terms and the diagonal concentration of the inverse matrix, we employ a truncated Neumann expansion with structural masking and parallel residual correction to eliminate sequential dependencies. We further extend our method to low-bits INT by mitigating the dynamic range expansion arising from repeated matrix power operations, and adapt the approximation order and residual step to the chunk size to minimize computational cost while preserving the model's accuracy. Experiments on Qwen3.5-family models demonstrate up to 5$\times$ kernel-level speedup and a 20% reduction in decode-layer overhead, while preserving accuracy under both floating-point and low-precision inference. Our method offers an efficient and hardware-friendly solution for scalable linear attention.

AI.tldr: Proposes a MatMul-based truncated Neumann expansion method with parallel residual correction to replace forward-substitution for matrix inversion in chunk-wise linear attention, achieving up to 5× kernel speedup on NPUs with preserved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Matrix inversion in chunk-wise parallel linear attention is a major bottleneck for long-context modeling on NPUs because forward-substitution methods have limited parallelism and poor hardware utilization.
AI.method: Uses truncated Neumann expansion with structural masking and parallel residual correction; extends to low-bit INT by managing dynamic range expansion from repeated matrix powers; adapts approximation order and residual step to chunk size.
