Our approach effectively mitigates phase degradation, improving physical consistency by an average of 6.2 points across diverse models while largely maintaining visual fidelity, with negligible overhead ($1.06\times$ time, $1.02\times$ memory) and reduced reliance on expensive external guidance methods ($\sim5\times$ time).

AI.tldr: PhaseLock is a training-free framework that improves physical consistency in Image-to-Video diffusion models by preserving motion priors from 2-step inference throughout denoising, addressing phase erosion that degrades physical consistency during multi-step generation.
AI.motivation: Image-to-Video diffusion models generate visually appealing content but often produce motions violating physical laws. The authors discovered that 2-step generation yields better physical consistency than 50-step outputs, with phase degradation (≈18% drop) being the root cause during denoising.
AI.method: PhaseLock is a training-free framework that extracts valid motion priors from early (2-step) inference and enforces them onto high-fidelity generation using Latent Delta Guidance, preserving phase information throughout the denoising trajectory.
AI.result: Improves physical consistency by 6.2 points on average across diverse models while maintaining visual fidelity, with minimal overhead (1.06× time, 1.02× memory) and 5× reduction in reliance on expensive external guidance.
AI.conclusion: Phase degradation is the key factor behind poor physical consistency in multi-step I2V diffusion models, and preserving early-step motion priors via PhaseLock effectively mitigates this issue with negligible computational cost.

title: AIS-Based Vessel Trajectory Prediction Using Memory-Augmented Neural Networks
summary: Accurate vessel trajectory prediction is essential for safe and efficient maritime operations, enabling collision avoidance and supporting route optimization. Although memory-augmented neural networks have recently shown strong performance in pedestrian and road-vehicle trajectory prediction by selectively retrieving relevant information from an external memory, their potential for vessel trajectory prediction remains underexplored. This paper presents an empirical investigation of memory-based trajectory prediction using Automatic Identification System (AIS) data. Experiments on data from the Gulf of Mexico and the New York Bight demonstrate consistent and substantial performance gains over a range of deep learning baselines that do not incorporate an external memory.

AI.tldr: This paper empirically shows that memory-augmented neural networks (with external memory) significantly improve vessel trajectory prediction over standard deep learning baselines, using AIS data from two maritime regions.
AI.motivation: Memory-augmented neural networks have succeeded in pedestrian and road-vehicle trajectory prediction but their potential for vessel trajectory prediction is underexplored, despite the importance of accurate prediction for maritime safety and efficiency.
AI.method: Empirical investigation comparing memory-based trajectory prediction models against deep learning baselines without external memory, using AIS data from the Gulf of Mexico and the New York Bight.
AI.result: Memory-augmented models achieved consistent and substantial performance improvements over non-memory baselines across both datasets.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating external memory into vessel trajectory prediction models yields significant accuracy gains, validating the approach for maritime applications.

title: Learning to Route LLMs from Implicit Cost-Performance Preferences via Meta-Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) present a trade-off between performance and cost, where more powerful models incur greater expense. LLM routing aims to mitigate expenses while maintaining performance by sending queries to the most suitable model. However, existing methods cannot perform well for different user cost-performance preferences. To address this gap, we introduce a novel perceptive LLM routing paradigm for personalized and user-centric cost-performance optimization, which efficiently learns users' implicit preferences through little interaction. To handle the challenge of heterogeneous user needs, we formulate preference profiles as a set of distinct tasks in contextual bandit and propose MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework designed for preference-aware LLM routing. Experimental results show that MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks. Furthermore, it exhibits high efficiency in learning user preferences, robustness to changes in the routable LLMs, and scalability to multi-model routing.

AI.tldr: MetaRouter is a meta-learning framework for personalized LLM routing that learns user preferences through minimal interaction to optimize cost-performance trade-offs, outperforming baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM routing methods cannot adapt to diverse user cost-performance preferences. Users have varying needs but current approaches lack personalization.
AI.method: Formulates user preferences as distinct tasks in a contextual bandit setting and proposes MetaRouter, a meta-learning framework for preference-aware LLM routing that learns implicit preferences through little interaction.
AI.result: MetaRouter outperforms strong baselines on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution tasks, showing high efficiency in learning user preferences, robustness to changes in routable LLMs, and scalability to multi-model routing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed perceptive routing paradigm enables personalized cost-performance optimization for LLM routing, effectively handling heterogeneous user needs.

title: GMBFormer: An NDVI-Guided Global Memory Bank Transformer for Urban Green-Space Extraction from Ultra-High-Resolution Imagery
summary: Urban green-space extraction from ultra-high-resolution (UHR) imagery is commonly performed patch by patch, which limits semantic reuse among spatially separated but visually similar vegetation patterns. Directly injecting the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) into red-green-blue (RGB) backbones can also blur the roles of visual appearance learning and physical vegetation confidence. We propose GMBFormer, a SegFormer-based framework that replaces adjacency-driven feature propagation with selective, similarity-driven prototype retrieval. Only RGB channels enter the backbone and decoder, while NDVI is decoupled as a physics-informed gate that admits high-confidence vegetation descriptors into a compact global memory bank through momentum updates. During training and inference, the current patch queries stored prototypes through memory-mediated cross-attention, and the retrieved response is integrated with bounded overhead. Experiments use a self-constructed Chengdu UHR dataset with 7,700 labeled 512 x 512 patches and two reduced-label settings derived from the public International Society for Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing (ISPRS) Potsdam dataset. Under the same training and evaluation protocol, GMBFormer obtains mean intersection over union (mIoU)/mean Dice (mDice) scores of 89.25%/94.31%, 92.17%/95.92%, and 83.72%/90.86%, respectively, improving the controlled SegFormer-B4 baseline in each setting. Ablation studies indicate that decoupled NDVI admission, memory retrieval, capacity, and momentum jointly shape the final performance.

AI.tldr: GMBFormer is a SegFormer-based framework for urban green-space extraction that uses selective prototype retrieval instead of adjacency-driven propagation. NDVI is decoupled as a physics-informed gate to admit high-confidence vegetation features into a global memory bank via momentum updates. It achieves state-of-the-art results on a self-constructed Chengdu UHR dataset and the ISPRS Potsdam dataset, outperforming the SegFormer-B4 baseline.
AI.motivation: Existing patch-by-patch extraction limits semantic reuse among visually similar vegetation patterns across spatially separated areas. Directly injecting NDVI into RGB backbones blurs the distinction between visual appearance learning and physical vegetation confidence.
AI.method: GMBFormer builds on SegFormer with decoupled NDVI as a physics-informed gate, a global memory bank with momentum updates for prototype storage, and memory-mediated cross-attention to retrieve similar vegetation descriptors across patches.
AI.result: On the Chengdu UHR dataset (89.25% mIoU/94.31% mDice), ISPRS Potsdam (92.17%/95.92%), and a reduced-label setting (83.72%/90.86%), GMBFormer consistently outperforms the SegFormer-B4 baseline.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled NDVI admission, memory retrieval, capacity, and momentum jointly shape performance, validating the effectiveness of similarity-driven cross-patch prototype reuse for green-space extraction.

title: LLM Self-Recognition: Steering and Retrieving Activation Signatures
comment: To appear in Proceedings of the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Recent advances in interpretability suggest that large language models (LLMs) implicitly encode signals in their generated text that enable self-recognition of their outputs. We demonstrate that this capability is reliable, even in low-entropy scenarios, and that it can be amplified through targeted intervention. By steering the internal residual stream during generation with a random sparse vector, we create a detectable fingerprint that enables attribution of a given text to a specific LLM. This signal is recoverable from the activations of an LLM used as a detector, achieving over 98% accuracy across multiple detection settings while preserving the quality of generated text. As AI-generated content proliferates, this approach offers a practical alternative to traditional detectors by leveraging the model's natural representation structure for attribution rather than embedding a signal externally. Our contributions include: (i) establishing reliable self-recognition capabilities in LLMs, (ii) a simple steering mechanism enabling multi-LLM identification with no quality degradation, (iii) demonstrating that activation spaces contain exploitable structure for encoding signals without semantic interference.

AI.tldr: LLMs can self-recognize their own outputs via internal signals, and a random sparse vector steering mechanism creates detectable fingerprints enabling >98% accurate attribution across models without degrading text quality.
AI.motivation: To address the need for reliable AI-generated text attribution without relying on external watermarking or traditional detectors, by exploiting the model's own internal representation structure.
AI.method: Steering the internal residual stream during generation with a random sparse vector to embed a detectable fingerprint; using another LLM's activations as a detector to recover the signal.
AI.result: Achieves over 98% accuracy across multiple detection settings while preserving generated text quality; demonstrates reliable self-recognition even in low-entropy scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LLMs' natural activation spaces contain exploitable structure for attribution without semantic interference, offering a practical alternative to traditional external detectors for AI-generated content.

title: A Machine Learning-Based Framework for Discovering Huntington's Disease Stages: Integrating Graph Representation Learning and clustering to Uncover Progression Dynamics in Longitudinal Enroll-HD Dataset
comment: Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 10th International Conference on Medical and Health Informatics (ICMHI 2026), Association for Computing Machinery (ACM)
summary: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive brain disorder that gradually affects movement, cognitive function, and behavior. Identifying the stage of the disease accurately and consistently is important for understanding its course, grouping patients, personalized care, and discovering treatment. Existing clinical staging frameworks rely primarily on predefined clinical measurement thresholds and clinical expert decisions, yet these discrete cut-offs may obscure meaningful intra-stage variability and remain vulnerable to inter-rater differences, especially in motor and functional assessments. To address these limitations, we developed an unsupervised machine learning framework based on dynamic graph representation learning to capture temporal relationships within and across patients from longitudinal clinical measurements. Using the learned representations, we applied K-means++ clustering to identify well-separated groups. We then iteratively increased the number of clusters (k), using stability analysis to assess robustness and reveal additional meaningful clusters beyond the initial optimal solution. We applied the framework to 302 individuals from the Enroll-HD cohort (1,477 visits, 44 clinical variables per visit; 80% manifest participants), enabling data-driven discovery of HD stages reflecting natural clinical progression. Despite the limited cohort size, the proposed framework achieved robust clustering performance using a four-dimensional latent space, identifying four meaningful and statistically distinct disease stages through clustering stability analysis. Each stage corresponded to well-defined clinical measurement boundaries, with minimal overlap compared to previously established clinical staging methods.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an unsupervised machine learning framework using dynamic graph representation learning and K-means++ clustering to identify disease stages in Huntington's disease from longitudinal clinical data, discovering four robust and statistically distinct stages with minimal overlap compared to existing clinical staging methods.
AI.motivation: Existing clinical staging frameworks for HD rely on predefined thresholds and expert decisions, which obscure intra-stage variability and are vulnerable to inter-rater differences, especially in motor and functional assessments.
AI.method: The authors developed an unsupervised machine learning framework using dynamic graph representation learning to capture temporal relationships within and across patients from longitudinal clinical measurements, followed by K-means++ clustering with iterative stability analysis to identify meaningful disease stages.
AI.result: Applied to 302 individuals (1,477 visits, 44 clinical variables) from Enroll-HD, the framework achieved robust clustering with a four-dimensional latent space, identifying four meaningful and statistically distinct disease stages with well-defined clinical measurement boundaries and minimal overlap compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The data-driven approach can identify natural HD progression stages that are more refined and robust than traditional clinical staging frameworks, despite a limited cohort size.

title: Visual Commonsense Driven Knowledge Refinements for Scene Graph Generation
summary: Learning-driven Scene Graph Generation (SGG) models excel on frequent relation types but degrade sharply under annotation sparsity, failing to capture reliable visual commonsense knowledge. We propose a model-agnostic, semantically-guided knowledge refinement framework that systematically mines commonsense-grounded constraints from training data - capturing spatial, functional, and qualitative relational regularities - and uses general declarative commonsense reasoning to correct and refine ranked SGG predictions at inference time. The framework requires no manual rule authoring, no model retraining, and transfers across datasets and architectures. On three standard benchmarks, we obtain consistent improvements over strong baselines, demonstrating that structured visual commonsense reasoning over deep scene semantics is a practical and effective complement to purely learning-based scene graph generation.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic framework that uses commonsense reasoning to refine scene graph generation predictions, improving performance without retraining or manual rules.
AI.motivation: Existing SGG models perform well on frequent relations but degrade on sparse annotations, failing to capture reliable visual commonsense knowledge.
AI.method: A semantically-guided knowledge refinement framework that mines commonsense constraints (spatial, functional, qualitative) from training data and applies declarative commonsense reasoning to correct/refine SGG predictions at inference time.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over strong baselines across three standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Structured visual commonsense reasoning over deep scene semantics is a practical and effective complement to purely learning-based scene graph generation.

title: DragOn: A Benchmark and Dataset for Drag-Based GUI Interactions
summary: GUI agents - vision-based models that control desktops, web browsers, and mobile devices through graphical user interfaces - promise to automate a wide range of digital tasks. While million-scale datasets have enabled substantial progress on click-grounding, drag grounding (e.g. drag-and-drop, swipe, highlight) data remains an order of magnitude smaller and current models fall short on complex drag-based interactions. We introduce DragOn, a drag grounding benchmark and training dataset covering four domains: text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing and slider manipulation. The dataset comprises 286K training screenshots and 3.5M training tasks, plus a 2000-example held-out evaluation suite. We evaluate proprietary (GPT, Claude) and open-weight (Qwen, Kimi, Holo) models, as well as a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on our training data. Results suggest that our dataset could improve performance of state-of-the-art models on downstream computer-use tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces DragOn, a large-scale drag grounding dataset (286K screenshots, 3.5M tasks) covering four drag-based interaction domains, showing potential to improve state-of-the-art models on computer-use tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI agents excel at click-grounding due to million-scale datasets, but drag grounding (drag-and-drop, swipe, highlight) remains underdeveloped with much smaller datasets, limiting model performance on complex drag-based interactions.
AI.method: Created DragOn, a drag grounding benchmark and training dataset with 286K training screenshots and 3.5M training tasks across four domains (text highlighting, cell selection, element resizing, slider manipulation), plus a 2000-example evaluation suite.
AI.result: Evaluated proprietary (GPT, Claude) and open-weight (Qwen, Kimi, Holo) models, plus a Qwen VLM fine-tuned on DragOn data; results suggest the dataset can improve performance on downstream computer-use tasks.
AI.conclusion: The DragOn dataset helps bridge the gap between click and drag grounding capabilities for GUI agents, potentially advancing vision-based models' ability to handle complex drag-based digital interactions.

title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization for Event Data
summary: Continuous-time event data, in which entities emit instantaneous events over time, arises naturally across many domains such as neuroscience, seismology, and social networks. Non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) is a natural tool to uncover interpretable structure in such data, but it has so far only been applied after binning or smoothing the entity-level counting measures. This preprocessing step comes with the risk of erasing entity-level heterogeneities and fine-grained temporal features. In this paper, we introduce EventNMF, a continuous-time non-negative factorization model that operates directly on event times: each entity's events are modeled as a Poisson process whose intensity factorizes through a non-negative B-spline basis, and a simple estimation procedure recovers interpretable temporal templates shared across entities. The resulting method is mathematically principled, easy to implement, and computationally efficient. We further show that standard binned-count approaches arise as the special case of degree-zero splines, explore bias-variance tradeoffs and compare against existing methods on a synthetic latent factor model, and demonstrate the effectiveness of EventNMF on several real-world applications.

AI.tldr: EventNMF is a continuous-time non-negative matrix factorization model that operates directly on event times using Poisson processes and B-spline bases, avoiding information loss from binning or smoothing.
AI.motivation: Current NMF methods for event data require binning or smoothing, which risks erasing entity-level heterogeneities and fine-grained temporal features.
AI.method: Models each entity's events as a Poisson process whose intensity factorizes through a non-negative B-spline basis, with a simple estimation procedure recovering interpretable temporal templates shared across entities.
AI.result: Shows standard binned-count approaches are a special case (degree-zero splines), explores bias-variance tradeoffs, and demonstrates effectiveness on real-world applications.
AI.conclusion: EventNMF is mathematically principled, easy to implement, computationally efficient, and avoids the information loss of preprocessing steps.

title: EasyLens: A Training-Free Plug-and-Play Subtle-Lesion Representation Amplifier for Medical Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) have shown increasing potential for clinical image interpretation, including lesion detection and report generation. However, their practical utility remains limited by insufficient sensitivity to subtle lesions, whose visual evidence is often sparse, low-contrast, and embedded within complex anatomical context. As local visual tokens are aggregated, these weak lesion cues can become underrepresented in global image representations, making them difficult for medical VLMs to recognize. Existing efforts to improve lesion sensitivity mainly rely on medical-domain vision-encoder pre-training, clinical-term-guided alignment, or trainable pathological representation enhancement. Although effective, these approaches usually require additional training or model-specific adaptation and may overfit to particular disease morphologies, limiting their applicability to frozen medical VLMs. To address these limitations, we propose EasyLens, a training-free plug-and-play subtle-lesion representation amplifier for medical VLMs. EasyLens first constructs EasyBank, a pathology-anatomy prototype space that provides lesion-related prototypes and anatomy-aware normal references for comparing suspicious patches against both pathological and normal anatomical patterns. To avoid blindly amplifying normal tissues, EasyTag selects lesion-relevant patches through counterfactual prototype reasoning. To counteract the dilution of subtle lesion cues in global image representations, EasyAmplifier strengthens the selected lesion-relevant patch representations through morphology-guided residual enhancement, thereby increasing their contribution to the global image embedding. Experiments on multiple medical image datasets and frozen medical VLM backbones show that EasyLens improves subtle-lesion detection and outperforms existing encoder-enhancement baselines.

AI.tldr: EasyLens is a training-free plug-and-play module that amplifies subtle lesion representations in frozen medical vision-language models by constructing a pathology-anatomy prototype space, selecting lesion-relevant patches via counterfactual reasoning, and enhancing them through morphology-guided residual amplification.
AI.motivation: Medical VLMs struggle with subtle lesions due to their sparse, low-contrast visual cues being diluted during global representation aggregation. Existing solutions require additional training or model-specific adaptation and may overfit to specific disease morphologies.
AI.method: EasyLens consists of three components: (1) EasyBank - a pathology-anatomy prototype space with lesion-related and normal reference prototypes; (2) EasyTag - counterfactual prototype reasoning to select lesion-relevant patches; (3) EasyAmplifier - morphology-guided residual enhancement to strengthen selected patch representations in global embeddings.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple medical image datasets with frozen medical VLM backbones show that EasyLens improves subtle-lesion detection and outperforms existing encoder-enhancement baselines.
AI.conclusion: EasyLens provides an effective, training-free solution for enhancing subtle lesion sensitivity in frozen medical VLMs without requiring model-specific adaptation or additional training.

title: TokenMizer: Graph-Structured Session Memory for Long-Horizon LLM Context Management
comment: 12 pages, 10 figures. Code and benchmark available at https://github.com/Shweta-Mishra-ai/tokenmizer
summary: Large language model (LLM) deployments for long-horizon tasks face a fundamental constraint: context windows are finite while productive work sessions are not. When history exceeds the Maximum Effective Context Window (MECW), critical structured information - architectural decisions, task transitions, file histories - is silently discarded. Existing mitigations treat history as flat text, destroying the relational structure that makes sessions resumable. We present TokenMizer, an open-source proxy system that models LLM session history as a typed knowledge graph. The schema defines 14 node types and 7 edge types. A hybrid extraction pipeline populates the graph incrementally, while a three-tier checkpoint system serializes it into compact resume blocks. An 8-layer compression pipeline reduces context overhead, and a semantic cache reduces repeated-query latency. Evaluated on a controlled benchmark of 21 sessions spanning 5 domains, TokenMizer demonstrates significant token economy. It produces resume blocks averaging 78 tokens (range: 42-124) - 2x smaller than evaluated baselines (159-170 tokens) - while achieving higher decision recall (+9-17 percentage points). Crucially, baselines only preserve that a technology was mentioned; TokenMizer preserves the rationale. Across all sessions, TokenMizer achieves mean task recall 51.0%, decision recall 46.6%, and file recall 58.7%. Variance reflects domain heterogeneity: explicit imperative phrasing (software engineering) scores higher than implicit reasoning (research). Ablation studies show fuzzy label matching is the dominant improvement factor (+33 pp task recall). The heuristic compression achieves 47.3% token reduction with zero external dependencies. TokenMizer provides a queryable alternative to text-retention baselines at half the token cost.

AI.tldr: TokenMizer is an open-source proxy system that models LLM session history as a typed knowledge graph, using a hybrid extraction pipeline and 8-layer compression to produce compact resume blocks (~78 tokens) that outperform text-retention baselines in recall (task 51.0%, decision 46.6%, file 58.7%) at half the token cost.
AI.motivation: LLM context windows are finite, so when long-horizon sessions exceed the Maximum Effective Context Window (MECW), critical structured information (decisions, task transitions, file histories) is silently lost. Existing mitigations treat history as flat text, destroying relational structure needed for resumability.
AI.method: TokenMizer models session history as a typed knowledge graph (14 node types, 7 edge types). A hybrid extraction pipeline incrementally populates the graph, a three-tier checkpoint system serializes it into compact resume blocks, an 8-layer compression pipeline reduces context overhead, and a semantic cache reduces repeated-query latency.
AI.result: On 21 sessions across 5 domains, TokenMizer produces resume blocks averaging 78 tokens (range: 42-124) — 2x smaller than baselines (159-170 tokens) — with higher decision recall (+9-17 pp). Mean task recall 51.0%, decision recall 46.6%, file recall 58.7%. Ablation: fuzzy label matching yields +33 pp task recall. Heuristic compression achieves 47.3% token reduction with zero external dependencies.
AI.conclusion: TokenMizer provides a queryable, graph-structured alternative to text-retention baselines at half the token cost, preserving not just what was mentioned but the rationale behind decisions. However, performance varies by domain, with explicit imperative phrasing (software engineering) outperforming implicit reasoning (research).

title: Design a Reliable LLM-Integrated Interface for Mortality Forecasting
summary: Mortality forecasting plays an important role in actuarial and policy decision-making, but its implementation remains technically complex and inaccessible to non-expert users. This project proposes a reliable large language model (LLM)-integrated interface that improves usability while maintaining statistical power. The LLM is designed as a constrained orchestration layer that translates natural-language inputs into structured configurations for a deterministic forecasting pipeline. A three-phase methodology is employed to ensure accuracy, usability, and transparency. First, a baseline pipeline is implemented using the CoMoMo package, reproducing established mortality forecasting results. Second, the pipeline is extended to generate multi-step forecasts using rolling-origin evaluation and mean squared error (MSE). Third, a prototype interface uses a local LLM to handle users' forecasting requests in plain language. The system demonstrates that LLMs can enhance accessibility without compromising reproducibility, transparency, or actuarial validity in high-stakes analytical workflows.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LLM-integrated interface that translates natural language into structured configurations for mortality forecasting, improving accessibility while maintaining statistical rigor and reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Mortality forecasting is technically complex and inaccessible to non-expert users, creating a need for a more usable interface that doesn't sacrifice statistical power.
AI.method: A three-phase methodology: (1) baseline pipeline using CoMoMo package; (2) extended to multi-step forecasting with rolling-origin evaluation and MSE; (3) prototype interface using a local LLM to handle plain-language forecasting requests.
AI.result: The LLM-integrated system enhances accessibility for non-expert users while maintaining reproducibility, transparency, and actuarial validity.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can serve as effective constrained orchestration layers in high-stakes analytical workflows, bridging usability and statistical rigor in mortality forecasting.

title: HomeWorld: A Unified Floorplan-to-Furnished Framework for Generating Controllable, Densely Interactive Whole-Home Scenes
summary: Indoor scene generation is crucial for robot simulation and modern interior design. However, complex layouts together with scarce 3D scene data make learning-based generation challenging. Existing methods often rely on hand-crafted rules or focus on isolated sub-tasks (e.g., floorplan synthesis or single-room furnishing), producing whole-home scenes that lack global coherence, realism, and simulation readiness. To mitigate these limitations, we propose a unified hierarchical framework that decomposes indoor scene synthesis into controllable stages. First, we curate a large-scale dataset of 300K real residential floorplans to train a large language model for whole-home floorplan generation. With detailed descriptions and a K-D tree-based representation, our method enables fine-grained, controllable whole-home floorplan generation. Building upon the generated whole-home floorplan, we leverage image generation models to draft furniture layouts from multi-level roaming viewpoints, and then generate the layouts of small manipulable objects on different supporting surfaces (e.g., cabinets, desks, and dining tables) for embodied AI simulation. During furniture and object layout generation, a VLM-based refiner iteratively corrects furniture and object placement, and a 3D generative model enables flexible replacement of individual assets. We further attach basic physical attributes and simple surface texture and lighting setups to complete the pipeline for embodied AI use. Experiments and user studies demonstrate that our pipeline produces indoor spaces with greater layout diversity and stronger 3D design appeal, outperforming prior methods on both quantitative and qualitative metrics. Finally, alongside our generation pipeline, we will release the floorplan dataset and 5K fully furnished scenes to the community. Project Page: https://kairos-homeworld.github.io/

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified hierarchical framework for indoor scene generation that decomposes the task into controllable stages: whole-home floorplan generation using a large language model trained on 300K real floorplans, followed by furniture layout generation using image models and VLM-based refinement, and finally object placement for embodied AI simulation.
AI.motivation: Existing indoor scene generation methods rely on hand-crafted rules or focus on isolated sub-tasks, producing scenes that lack global coherence, realism, and simulation readiness for robotics and interior design.
AI.method: A hierarchical pipeline: (1) LLM trained on 300K floorplans with K-D tree representation for controllable whole-home floorplan generation; (2) Image generation models draft furniture layouts from multi-level viewpoints; (3) VLM-based refiner iteratively corrects placements; (4) 3D generative model enables asset replacement; (5) Physical attributes, textures, and lighting are added for embodied AI use.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods on both quantitative and qualitative metrics in experiments and user studies, producing indoor spaces with greater layout diversity and stronger 3D design appeal.
AI.conclusion: The unified hierarchical framework effectively addresses limitations of prior work, and the authors will release a floorplan dataset and 5K fully furnished scenes to the community.

title: Boosting Brain-to-Image Decoding with TRIBE v2 Data Augmentation
summary: Brain decoding is limited by the availability of labeled neural data, and remains challenging in low-data regimes. To address this issue, we investigate whether and when brain decoding can be boosted by augmenting small fMRI datasets with synthetic data generated by a pretrained model of fMRI responses to stimuli. We use TRIBE v2, a large encoding model pretrained on more than 1000 hours of fMRI responses to video, audio and language. For each dataset, we evaluate systematic grids that show how the performance of image decoders varies with the amount of synthetic data used for training. Our results, based on two datasets (the 7T fMRI Natural Scenes Dataset and 3T fMRI BOLD5000), show up to 68% improvement in Top-10 image-retrieval accuracy compared to decoders trained only on real data. Importantly, the proportion of augmented data required to reach a given image decoding performance needs to be adjusted depending on the data source. Surprisingly, image decoders trained exclusively on synthetic fMRI can perform above chance in some settings, suggesting that TRIBE v2 can support zero-shot brain-to-image decoding. Together, these results show how large-scale models of the fMRI responses to sight, sound and language may provide a foundation to improve the data efficiency for image decoding.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that augmenting small fMRI datasets with synthetic data from a large pretrained encoding model (TRIBE v2) can significantly improve brain-to-image decoding performance, achieving up to 68% improvement in Top-10 accuracy, with the optimal synthetic data ratio depending on the dataset source.
AI.motivation: Brain decoding is limited by the scarcity of labeled neural data, making it challenging to train effective decoders in low-data regimes. The authors aim to address this by investigating whether synthetic fMRI data can augment real datasets.
AI.method: The authors use TRIBE v2 (a large encoding model pretrained on 1000+ hours of fMRI responses) to generate synthetic fMRI data. They augment small real fMRI datasets (NSD and BOLD5000) with varying amounts of synthetic data and systematically evaluate image decoder performance across different data ratios.
AI.result: Up to 68% improvement in Top-10 image-retrieval accuracy compared to real-data-only training. The optimal proportion of synthetic data varies by dataset. Decoders trained solely on synthetic fMRI can perform above chance, enabling zero-shot decoding.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale encoding models like TRIBE v2 can serve as a foundation for improving data efficiency in brain decoding, suggesting that synthetic data augmentation is a viable strategy for overcoming the bottleneck of limited neural data availability.

title: Tracing the Oracle: Improving Diffusion Timestep Scheduling for 3D CT Reconstruction
summary: Pretrained diffusion models demonstrate impressive potential in solving highly ill-posed 3D computed tomography (CT) inverse problems, while the inference process suffers from significant computational overhead. Furthermore, existing uniform timestep schedules fail to capture the non-uniform evolution of the reverse conditional diffusion stochastic differential equation, thereby introducing substantial truncation errors. To overcome this limitation, we propose Tracing the Oracle (TrO), a plug-and-play framework for improved timestep scheduling. Specifically, we treat densely sampled numerical integration trajectories on a few samples as the reference oracle. The optimized schedule is extracted by leveraging dynamic programming to globally minimize the cumulative error between the few-step approximation and the oracle. This mechanism precisely allocates the limited sampling steps to critical evolution stages that are highly susceptible to truncation errors. Our extensive experiments on the AAPM dataset across multiple 3D CT reconstruction tasks demonstrate that, when combined with the state-of-the-art 3D CT reconstruction method DDS, our optimized timesteps significantly improve reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency compared to existing heuristic schedules, especially under a strict budget of no more than 10 sampling steps.

AI.tldr: TrO is a plug-and-play framework that uses dynamic programming to optimize timestep scheduling for diffusion-based 3D CT reconstruction, significantly improving fidelity and efficiency under limited sampling steps.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion models for 3D CT reconstruction suffer from high computational cost, and uniform timestep schedules fail to capture non-uniform evolution of the reverse diffusion process, leading to large truncation errors.
AI.method: TrO treats densely sampled trajectories on a few samples as a reference oracle, then uses dynamic programming to globally minimize cumulative error between few-step approximations and the oracle, allocating limited steps to critical stages.
AI.result: On the AAPM dataset with DDS method, TrO-optimized timesteps significantly improve reconstruction fidelity and computational efficiency over heuristic schedules, especially within ≤10 sampling steps.
AI.conclusion: TrO provides an effective, plug-and-play solution for timestep scheduling in diffusion-based CT reconstruction, balancing quality and speed under tight computational budgets.

title: Where Should Knowledge Enter? A Layered Framework for Knowledge Infusion in Multimodal Iterative Generative Mo
summary: Multimodal generative models produce fluent outputs but remain unreliable when generation must respect structured, domain-specific, or safety-critical knowledge. Existing methods incorporate knowledge through mechanisms such as prompt augmentation, guidance, latent editing, or fine-tuning, yet they are typically categorized by technique rather than by the component of the generative process they modify. We argue that knowledge infusion in iterative generative models is fundamentally anintervention-layer problem. Since thegenerative process unfolds as a trajectory of internal states, knowledge can act on four structurally distinct components of this process: the input/output boundary, the transition function, the intermediate state, and the model parameters. This maps to four intervention layers: surface, trajectory, latent, and parametric infusion. We instantiate the framework in diffusion models, map representative methods to all four layers, and derive design principles for multi-layer composition. In a controlled safety-alignment experiment using a multimodal knowledge graph with two diffusion backbones, we implement three of the four layers cumulatively, surface (input-side and output-side) and trajectory--latent (mid-generation). We show empirically that each additional layer addresses failure classes that prior layers cannot reach, reducing knowledge-violating outputs by 70.97% compared to vanilla generation and empirically confirming the framework's complementarity prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework categorizing knowledge infusion into four intervention layers (surface, trajectory, latent, parametric) for iterative generative models, demonstrating that combining multiple layers reduces knowledge-violating outputs by ~71% in safety-aligned diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge infusion methods are categorized by technique rather than by which component of the generative process they modify, lacking a unified structural understanding of how knowledge can intervene in iterative generation.
AI.method: The authors propose a four-layer intervention framework (surface, trajectory, latent, parametric) based on where knowledge acts in the generative trajectory, then instantiate it in diffusion models and test cumulative layer effects in a safety-alignment experiment.
AI.result: Adding multiple intervention layers cumulatively reduced knowledge-violating outputs by 70.97% compared to vanilla generation; each new layer addressed failure classes that prior layers could not reach.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge infusion is fundamentally an intervention-layer problem, and multi-layer composition is necessary to cover diverse failure modes in structured, safety-critical generative tasks.

title: Generative Criticality in Large Language Model Temperature Scaling
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, contributed to PAI 2026 Conference
summary: We propose a statistical-field framework for text generated by large language models (LLMs), treating token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a one-dimensional chain. Defining a susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field, we vary the \texttt{softmax} temperature $T$ and observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic $T_c$ with power-law-like scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and a collapse onto a single semantic direction below $T_c$. The intrinsic dimension estimated by the two nearest neighbor (TwoNN) method independently corroborates these findings, reaching a minimum near $T_c$. Results are robust across model scales (Qwen3: 0.6B--32B) and prompt categories. While the phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further investigation. Our framework provides quantitative tools for probing the collective statistical structure of LLM outputs and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a statistical-field framework for LLM text generation, treating token embeddings as spin variables on a 1D chain. By varying the softmax temperature, they observe a sharp susceptibility peak near a critical temperature Tc, with power-law scaling, order parameter changes, and semantic collapse—resembling a continuous phase transition, robust across model scales and prompts.
AI.motivation: To understand the collective statistical structure of LLM-generated text and to connect decoding strategies (like temperature tuning) to critical phenomena in statistical physics, providing a new theoretical lens for interpreting LLM outputs.
AI.method: Treat token embeddings as continuous spin variables on a 1D chain; define susceptibility from the connected two-point correlator and an order parameter from the ensemble-averaged embedding field. Vary softmax temperature T and analyze susceptibility peaks, order parameter changes, and intrinsic dimension (TwoNN method). Test across Qwen3 models (0.6B–32B) and prompt categories.
AI.result: A sharp susceptibility peak near a characteristic Tc with power-law scaling, a concurrent rapid change in the order parameter, and collapse onto a single semantic direction below Tc. Intrinsic dimension reaches a minimum near Tc. Results are robust across model sizes and prompt categories.
AI.conclusion: The phenomenology closely resembles a continuous phase transition, though the non-equilibrium nature of autoregressive generation warrants further study. The framework provides quantitative tools for probing LLM output structure and suggests connections between decoding strategies and critical phenomena.

title: Thinking with Imagination: Agentic Visual Spatial Reasoning with World Simulators
summary: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong visual reasoning capabilities, their spatial reasoning abilities remain largely constrained to the observed images and text-oriented chain-of-thought. They often struggle to infer unobserved layouts, maintain cross-view consistency, and reason from alternative viewpoints when only limited egocentric observations are available. In this work, we study this problem as thinking with imagination, where a VLM actively acquires imagined visual evidence by interacting with a world simulator during reasoning. We propose Astra, an agentic spatial reasoning framework that empowers VLMs with action-conditioned visual imagination. Specifically, Astra couples Astra-VL, an RL-trained VLM policy, with Astra-WM, a Bagel-based world simulator that generates novel-view observations from context images and natural-language camera motions. To provide reliable imagined evidence, Astra-WM is trained with view consistency tuning to improve pose and content consistency across views. In the RL stage, we propose a world-simulator-in-the-loop two-phase RL curriculum to stabilize tool-use exploration and advance the model's ability to invoke the simulator only when imagined observations improve over direct answering. Experiments demonstrate that both the world simulator and the agentic policy are necessary: Astra-WM improves simulator-augmented Gemini-3-Flash on MMSI-Bench from 45.1 to 49.5, while Astra-VL improves the Qwen3-VL backbone from 29.8 to 38.8 on MMSI-Bench and from 36.8 to 42.7 on MindCube. These results show that imagined observations can provide useful spatial evidence, but effective world-model-augmented reasoning requires learning when, where, and how to imagine.

AI.tldr: Astra is an agentic spatial reasoning framework that couples a VLM policy (Astra-VL) with a world simulator (Astra-WM) to enable VLMs to "think with imagination" — actively generating novel-view observations to improve spatial reasoning, achieving significant gains on MMSI-Bench and MindCube benchmarks.
AI.motivation: VLMs struggle with spatial reasoning tasks requiring inference of unobserved layouts, cross-view consistency, and reasoning from alternative viewpoints given only limited egocentric observations. The paper proposes enabling VLMs to actively acquire imagined visual evidence during reasoning.
AI.method: Astra consists of two components: (1) Astra-WM, a Bagel-based world simulator trained with view consistency tuning to generate novel-view observations from context images and natural-language camera motions; (2) Astra-VL, an RL-trained VLM policy that learns when to invoke the simulator using a world-simulator-in-the-loop two-phase RL curriculum.
AI.result: Astra-WM improves simulator-augmented Gemini-3-Flash on MMSI-Bench from 45.1 to 49.5. Astra-VL improves Qwen3-VL from 29.8 to 38.8 on MMSI-Bench and from 36.8 to 42.7 on MindCube.
AI.conclusion: Imagined observations provide useful spatial evidence, but effective world-model-augmented reasoning requires learning when, where, and how to imagine.

title: An Infectious Disease Spread Simulation Based on Large Language Model Decision Making
summary: Modelling individual decision-making during infectious disease outbreaks is crucial for understanding behavioural dynamics and informing effective public health interventions. Prior work has shown that large language models can simulate realistic human behaviour by generating agent decisions based on demographic prompts and situational context. We build on this foundation with a spatially grounded, agent-based simulation framework that integrates LLM-generated decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness into a census-based synthetic population of agents. Location is treated as a central feature: agents are assigned to spatial units within cities, capturing the spatial distributions of different demographic groups using real-world census data and enabling geographically diverse behavioural modelling. We implement and compare three decision scenarios, independent reasoning, household influence, and message framing, and simulate self-reporting outcomes in San Francisco and Atlanta. Results reveal that income and education are the dominant drivers of reporting rate variation, with smaller but consistent effects from geography, LLM model choice, and message framing. Our framework generates synthetic data that captures both social and geographic heterogeneity, supporting spatial epidemiological modelling and bias-aware behavioural analysis.

AI.tldr: A spatially grounded agent-based simulation using LLMs to model individual decision-making about self-reporting influenza-like illness, tested in San Francisco and Atlanta, showing income and education drive reporting variation.
AI.motivation: Modelling individual decision-making during outbreaks is crucial for understanding behavioural dynamics and informing public health interventions, and prior work shows LLMs can simulate human behaviour.
AI.method: Spatially grounded agent-based simulation framework integrating LLM-generated decisions about self-reported influenza-like illness into a census-based synthetic population, with three decision scenarios (independent reasoning, household influence, message framing) tested in San Francisco and Atlanta.
AI.result: Income and education are the dominant drivers of reporting rate variation, with smaller but consistent effects from geography, LLM model choice, and message framing.
AI.conclusion: The framework generates synthetic data capturing social and geographic heterogeneity, supporting spatial epidemiological modelling and bias-aware behavioural analysis.

title: Your GFlowNet Secretly Learns an Optimal Transport Plan
summary: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are a framework for sampling structured objects via stochastic trajectories in a directed graph. In this work, we establish a theoretical connection between non-acyclic GFlowNets and optimal transport (OT). We show that fixing the initial flow distribution in a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces its objective to a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest path costs. At the optimum, the learned GFlowNet policy therefore encodes an optimal transport plan from the source distribution to the target distribution: we show that sampling trajectories from the minimum-flow GFlowNet recovers the corresponding optimal coupling. Our formulation enables applying the GFlowNet learning framework to OT problems on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization. Experiments confirm agreement with exact OT solvers and demonstrate that GFlowNets can learn high-quality transport plans.

AI.tldr: GFlowNets are theoretically connected to optimal transport (OT), where a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces to a Kantorovich OT problem; its sampling policy learns optimal transport plans on graphs.
AI.motivation: To establish a theoretical connection between non-acyclic GFlowNets and optimal transport, enabling GFlowNet learning for OT problems on large graphs.
AI.method: Shows that fixing initial flow in a minimum-flow GFlowNet reduces its objective to a Kantorovich OT problem with graph-induced shortest path costs; the learned policy encodes an optimal transport plan.
AI.result: Experiments confirm agreement with exact OT solvers and demonstrate that GFlowNets learn high-quality transport plans.
AI.conclusion: GFlowNet learning framework can be applied to OT problems on large graphs via edge flows and neural parameterization, producing valid optimal couplings.

title: Complexity-Balanced Diffusion Splitting
summary: Standard continuous-time generative models rely on monolithic architectures that must navigate vastly different signal regimes, from isotropic noise to intricate data distributions. While scaling model capacity improves performance, deploying a massive network uniformly across the entire generative timeline is inherently inefficient. In this work, we propose Complexity-Balanced Splitting (CBS), a principled framework for temporal capacity allocation that distributes the generative workload across multiple specialized sub-networks. Grounded in function approximation theory and de Boor's equidistribution principle, CBS partitions the diffusion timeline into segments of equal approximation burden, allocating more representational capacity to regions where the generative dynamics are more difficult to model. To estimate this local complexity, we introduce two complementary and tractable monitor functions: a spatial measure based on the flow's Dirichlet energy, and a geometric measure based on the acceleration of the sampling trajectories. Using a lightweight auxiliary model to estimate these complexity profiles, our approach eliminates the need for heuristic temporal splits or computationally expensive search procedures. Extensive evaluation across multiple architectures (SiT, JiT, and UNet) and datasets demonstrates that CBS consistently improves synthesis quality without increasing per-step inference cost. In particular, CBS improves FID by ~35% on SiT-XL with CFG relative to naive temporal partitioning. Project page is available at https://noamissachar.github.io/CBS/.

AI.tldr: CBS is a framework that splits the diffusion timeline into segments of equal approximation complexity, allocating specialized sub-networks to each segment based on local difficulty, improving generation quality without extra per-step cost.
AI.motivation: Monolithic generative models waste capacity by applying the same large network uniformly across all noise levels, from simple isotropic noise to complex data distributions, which is inherently inefficient.
AI.method: CBS partitions the diffusion timeline into segments with equal approximation burden using de Boor's equidistribution principle, guided by two monitor functions: Dirichlet energy (spatial) and trajectory acceleration (geometric), estimated via a lightweight auxiliary model.
AI.result: CBS improves FID by ~35% on SiT-XL with CFG over naive temporal partitioning across multiple architectures (SiT, JiT, UNet) and datasets, without increasing per-step inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Temporal capacity allocation via complexity-balanced splitting is a principled and effective way to improve generative model efficiency and quality, eliminating heuristic splits and costly search.

title: Rethinking Infrastructure Inspection as Image Difference Classification: A Traffic Sign Case Study
comment: CVPR 2026 Computer Vision for the Built World Workshop (CV4AEC @ CVPR)
summary: Digital twins (DTs) allow the digitalization of road infrastructure inspection, though this is hindered by limited annotated data. This work exploits the relational nature of continuous asset condition monitoring to reformulate image-based defect detection as image difference classification (IDC) to reduce data reliance. This was evaluated in a case study on low-resource traffic sign inspection with different IDC classifiers using a newly-curated, high quality dataset. Results indicate that the instruction-based classifier outperforms encoder-based ones and gains from comparison with reference images. This shows that IDC can be an effective task modeling for tackling data constraints in infrastructure inspection and DT asset condition updating.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes reformulating defect detection as image difference classification (IDC) to address data scarcity in digital twin infrastructure inspection, showing that instruction-based classifiers outperform encoder-based methods in a traffic sign inspection case study.
AI.motivation: Digital twins enable digitalized road infrastructure inspection but are hindered by limited annotated data. The authors aim to reduce data reliance for defect detection.
AI.method: Reformulates image-based defect detection as image difference classification (IDC), leveraging the relational nature of continuous asset condition monitoring. Evaluated on low-resource traffic sign inspection using a newly-curated dataset with different IDC classifiers.
AI.result: Instruction-based classifier outperforms encoder-based classifiers, and benefits from comparison with reference images.
AI.conclusion: IDC is an effective task modeling approach for overcoming data constraints in infrastructure inspection and digital twin asset condition updating.

title: PAC-Bayesian Adversarially Robust Generalization for Message Passing Graph Neural Networks: A Sensitivity Analysis
summary: Whilst the vulnerability of graph neural networks (GNNs) to adversarial attacks poses a critical threat to graph representation learning, the understanding of the robust generalization behavior remains a fundamental challenge in the adversarial setting. Recently, PAC-Bayesian margin-based generalization analysis substantially advances this line of research by providing a flexible and data-dependent analytical framework. However, existing robust analyses often rely on isotropic Gaussian posteriors and control weight perturbations in the full parameter space, which limits the ability to capture heterogeneous parameter sensitivity yet hinges on hidden-width-dependent complexity terms, resulting in not-tight-enough generalization bounds. In this paper, we extend a recently proposed sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework from deep neural networks to message passing GNNs (MPGNNs) and derive a tighter robust generalization bound in the adversarial setting. Specifically, we first quantify how sensitive the perturbations across different parameter blocks are to the network outputs by deriving the output Jacobians with respect to the weight parameters. Exploiting the fact that these Jacobian matrices have rank at most $K$ in $K$-class graph classification, we then construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices and use anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to upper bound the KL divergence in a tight way. Notably, by refining the spectral-norm dependence on the learned weights and reducing the leading dimension factor from hidden-width-dependent terms to the number of classes $K$, our analysis yields much tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs, thereby guiding their designs to enhance adversarial robustness.

AI.tldr: This paper extends a sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework to MPGNNs, deriving tighter robust generalization bounds by using Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices and anisotropic Gaussian posteriors, reducing dimension dependence from hidden width to the number of classes K.
AI.motivation: Existing robust PAC-Bayesian analyses for GNNs rely on isotropic Gaussian posteriors and control weight perturbations in the full parameter space, limiting sensitivity capture and producing hidden-width-dependent complexity terms that lead to not-tight-enough generalization bounds.
AI.method: (1) Quantify parameter block sensitivity via output Jacobians w.r.t. weight parameters; (2) Exploit that Jacobian matrices have rank at most K in K-class classification; (3) Construct Jacobian-aligned sensitivity matrices; (4) Use anisotropic Gaussian posteriors with optimized covariances to tightly upper bound KL divergence.
AI.result: Tighter robust generalization bounds for MPGNNs, with spectral-norm dependence refined on learned weights and leading dimension factor reduced from hidden-width-dependent terms to the number of classes K.
AI.conclusion: The proposed sensitivity-aware PAC-Bayesian framework yields much tighter robust generalization guarantees for MPGNNs, guiding their design for enhanced adversarial robustness.

title: PAR3D: A Unified 3D-MLLM with Part-Aware Representation for Scene Understanding
summary: Recent advances in 3D multimodal large language models (3D-MLLMs) have enabled unified solutions for 3D scene understanding tasks, including visual question answering, captioning, and referring segmentation. However, existing 3D-MLLMs remain largely object-centric, limiting their ability to model fine-grained part structures that are essential for embodied interaction with 3D environments. In this work, we present PAR3D, a unified part-aware 3D-MLLM framework that enables models to understand, reason about, and ground both objects and their parts in 3D scenes. To enable training and evaluation of part-aware 3D scene understanding, we introduce ScenePart, a synthetic 3D scene dataset with part-level annotations and language instructions. We further develop Part-Aware 3D Representation Learning to enrich 3D visual representations with fine-grained part-level semantics, and propose Hierarchical Segmentation Query Generation to ground part targets via hierarchical object-part queries. Extensive experiments show that our method substantially improves part-level question answering and referring segmentation, while also achieving strong performance across object-level vision-language tasks.

AI.tldr: PAR3D is a unified part-aware 3D-MLLM framework that improves fine-grained part-level understanding and grounding in 3D scenes, supported by a new synthetic dataset ScenePart, achieving strong results on both part-level and object-level tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D-MLLMs are object-centric and fail to model fine-grained part structures needed for embodied interaction with 3D environments.
AI.method: (1) ScenePart dataset with part-level annotations; (2) Part-Aware 3D Representation Learning for fine-grained part semantics; (3) Hierarchical Segmentation Query Generation for object-part grounding.
AI.result: Substantial improvements on part-level QA and referring segmentation, while maintaining strong object-level performance.
AI.conclusion: Part-aware modeling is crucial for fine-grained 3D scene understanding, and PAR3D effectively bridges the gap between object-level and part-level reasoning.

title: Humans' ALMANAC: A Human Collaboration Dataset of Action-Level Mental Model Annotations for Agent Collaboration
summary: Recent advances in LLM agents have enabled complex cognitive capabilities, such as multi-step reasoning, planning, and tool use, that increasingly position these agents as human collaborators. Effective collaboration, however, requires collaborators to continuously maintain and align mental models of their own reasoning,partners' intentions, and shared goals during the collaborative process. Today's agents rarely develop such capabilities since they are primarily optimized for task completion, and the community lacks authentic human collaboration data with action-level mental model annotations that could guide agents toward process-level collaborative competence. To bridge this gap, we present ALMANAC, a dataset of Action-Level Mental model ANnotations for Agent Collaboration built from the Map Task, a classic dyadic routing task from social science. ALMANAC contains 2,987 collaboration actions, each paired with theory-informed mental model annotations that record the participants' self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, and perceived team goal. We benchmark six LLMs on predicting humans' next-turn behavior and mental models. Our results demonstrate ALMANAC's utility in evaluating models' ability to simulate human collaborative behaviors and infer their underlying mental models.

AI.tldr: ALMANAC is a dataset of 2,987 action-level mental model annotations for agent collaboration, built from the Map Task, enabling evaluation of LLMs' ability to simulate human collaborative behaviors and infer mental models (self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, perceived team goal).
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents are optimized for task completion but lack the ability to maintain and align mental models (self-reasoning, partner intentions, shared goals) during collaboration. The community lacks authentic human collaboration data with action-level mental model annotations to guide agents toward process-level collaborative competence.
AI.method: The authors built ALMANAC from the Map Task (a dyadic routing task), containing 2,987 collaboration actions each annotated with theory-informed mental model annotations covering self-reasoning, perceived partner intent, and perceived team goal. They benchmarked six LLMs on predicting humans' next-turn behavior and mental models.
AI.result: The benchmarking results demonstrate ALMANAC's utility in evaluating models' ability to simulate human collaborative behaviors and infer their underlying mental models.
AI.conclusion: ALMANAC provides a valuable resource for guiding LLM agents toward process-level collaborative competence by enabling evaluation of mental model inference and collaborative behavior simulation.

title: Reactive Flux Matching: Mechanism Discovery and Adaptive Sampling of Rare Events
summary: Path sampling methods generate ensembles of reactive trajectories connecting metastable states, but extracting mechanistic insight from these data remains nontrivial. We introduce Flux Matching, a framework that learns two complementary objects directly from reactive trajectory data: a current velocity $u(z)$, whose streamlines trace the dominant reaction pathways, and a scalar potential $h(z)$, obtained from a weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition of the reactive current, that serves as a data-driven reaction coordinate. Both minimize quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble, analogous to the flow matching loss in generative modeling, and require no knowledge of the underlying dynamics or stationary distribution. Unlike committor-based methods, $u$ and $h$ remain well-defined under projection onto non-Markovian collective variables, and their level sets in turn provide adaptive interfaces for improved sampling with enhanced sampling methods. Flux Matching is validated through the generation of current velocity trajectories and rate constant calculations on molecular systems.

AI.tldr: Flux Matching learns a current velocity (reaction pathways) and a scalar potential (reaction coordinate) from reactive trajectory data via quadratic loss minimization, enabling mechanistic analysis and improved sampling without requiring committor functions or knowledge of underlying dynamics.
AI.motivation: Path sampling generates reactive trajectories between metastable states, but extracting mechanistic insight (e.g., reaction pathways and coordinates) from such data remains challenging. Existing methods often require committor functions or knowledge of underlying dynamics.
AI.method: Flux Matching learns a current velocity u(z) (streamlines trace reaction pathways) and a scalar potential h(z) (data-driven reaction coordinate via weighted Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition of reactive current). Both minimize quadratic functionals over the reactive path ensemble, analogous to flow matching loss in generative modeling, requiring no knowledge of dynamics or stationary distribution.
AI.result: Validated on molecular systems by generating current velocity trajectories and calculating rate constants. The method remains well-defined under projection onto non-Markovian collective variables, unlike committor-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Flux Matching provides a principled, dynamics-agnostic framework for extracting mechanistic information from reactive trajectories, with level sets of h serving as adaptive interfaces for enhanced sampling.

title: Ask-to-Clarify: Resolving Instruction Ambiguity through Multi-turn Dialogue
summary: The ultimate goal of embodied agents is to create collaborators that can interact with humans, not mere executors that passively follow instructions. This requires agents to communicate, coordinate, and adapt their actions based on human feedback. Recently, advances in VLAs have offered a path toward this goal. However, most current VLA-based embodied agents operate in a one-way mode: they receive an instruction and execute it without feedback. This approach fails in real-world scenarios where instructions are often ambiguous. In this paper, we address this problem with the Ask-to-Clarify framework. Our framework first resolves ambiguous instructions by asking questions in a multi-turn dialogue. Then it generates low-level actions end-to-end. Specifically, the Ask-to-Clarify framework consists of two components, one VLM for collaboration and one diffusion for action. We also introduce a connection module that generates conditions for the diffusion based on the output of the VLM. This module adjusts the observation by instructions to create reliable conditions. We train our framework with a two-stage knowledge-insulation strategy. First, we fine-tune the collaboration component using ambiguity-solving dialogue data to handle ambiguity. Then, we integrate the action component while freezing the collaboration one. This preserves the interaction abilities while fine-tuning the diffusion to generate actions. The training strategy guarantees our framework can first ask questions, then generate actions. During inference, a signal detector functions as a router that helps our framework switch between asking questions and taking actions. We evaluate the Ask-to-Clarify framework in 8 real-world tasks, where it outperforms existing state-of-the-art VLAs. The results suggest that our proposed framework, along with the training strategy, provides a path toward collaborative embodied agents.

AI.tldr: The Ask-to-Clarify framework enables embodied agents to resolve ambiguous instructions through multi-turn dialogue before generating actions, using a VLM for collaboration and diffusion for action, trained with a two-stage knowledge-insulation strategy, outperforming prior VLAs on 8 real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Current VLA-based embodied agents operate in a one-way mode (receive instruction → execute) without feedback, failing in real-world scenarios where instructions are often ambiguous. The goal is to create collaborative agents that can interact with humans, not just passively follow instructions.
AI.method: The framework has two components: a VLM for multi-turn dialogue to resolve ambiguity (collaboration), and a diffusion model for low-level action generation. A connection module adjusts observations based on VLM outputs to create conditions for diffusion. Training uses a two-stage knowledge-insulation strategy: first fine-tune the collaboration component on ambiguity-solving dialogue data, then integrate the action component while freezing the collaboration one. A signal detector serves as a router to switch between asking questions and taking actions during inference.
AI.result: Evaluated on 8 real-world tasks, the Ask-to-Clarify framework outperforms existing state-of-the-art VLAs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework and training strategy provide a path toward collaborative embodied agents that can ask clarifying questions before generating actions, moving beyond passive instruction-following.

title: Risk Assessment of Autonomous Driving: Integrating Technical Failures, Ethical Dilemmas, and Policy Frameworks
summary: Autonomous driving technology has the potential to reduce the large number of road traffic accidents caused by human error each year, but it also brings new types of risks that need to be evaluated from the aspects of technology, ethics and regulations. Based on public crash data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA), disengagement reports from the California Department of Motor Vehicles (DMV), the MIT Moral Machines dataset, and a comparative regulatory analysis of five jurisdictions, we have found that the main types of technical failure modes are perception and classification errors. These account for a relatively large proportion of the reported accidents, and it can be concluded that there are different ethical frameworks for autonomous vehicle decision-making, and inconsistent regulations in different areas increase the uncertainty of widespread application. Generally speaking, the problems of technology, ethics and regulation are closely related and need to be solved together. Therefore, this paper recommends a more adaptive and cooperative governance approach that combines engineering standards, ethical discussion, and institutional supervision.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes autonomous driving risks from technical, ethical, and regulatory perspectives using crash data, disengagement reports, moral machine data, and cross-jurisdictional regulatory comparisons, finding perception/classification errors as dominant failure modes and inconsistent regulations as key barriers, recommending cooperative governance.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the multifaceted risks (technical, ethical, regulatory) introduced by autonomous driving technology, which promises to reduce human-error accidents but introduces new failure modes.
AI.method: Analysis of NHTSA crash data, California DMV disengagement reports, MIT Moral Machines dataset, and comparative regulatory analysis across five jurisdictions.
AI.result: Perception and classification errors are the primary technical failures; different ethical frameworks exist for decision-making; inconsistent regulations across regions increase uncertainty for widespread deployment.
AI.conclusion: Technology, ethics, and regulation are interconnected problems requiring a holistic, adaptive, and cooperative governance approach combining engineering standards, ethical discussion, and institutional supervision.

title: Tangram: Unlocking Non-Uniform KV Cache for Efficient Multi-turn LLM Serving
summary:
Multi-turn Large Language Model (LLM) serving is critical for consistent user experiences, yet the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache imposes significant pressure on GPU memory and bandwidth. Non-uniform KV compression effectively preserves more information by considering the individual importance of each KV cache. However, such KV cache heterogeneity introduces various systemic challenges - including memory fragmentation, scheduling complexities, and diminished kernel utilization - which collectively lead to significant inefficiencies in existing LLM serving systems.
  To overcome these challenges, we present Tangram, a novel serving system designed to make Non-uniform KV caches practical. Tangram addresses systemic inefficiencies through three core techniques: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation assigns a static memory footprint to each head based on its intrinsic pattern, entirely eliminating dynamic scheduling overhead and prefill stalls; (2) Head Group Page clusters attention heads with similar retention demands and manages them with independent, vectorized page tables, thereby maximizing physical memory reclamation; and (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing leverages static budget profiles to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead. Experimental results show that Tangram improves throughput by up to 2.6x compared to existing baselines, while fully preserving model accuracy. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/TANGRAM.

AI.tldr: Tangram is a serving system that makes non-uniform KV cache compression practical for multi-turn LLM serving, improving throughput by up to 2.6x without accuracy loss through deterministic budget allocation, head group page clustering, and ahead-of-time load balancing.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn LLM serving faces GPU memory/bandwidth pressure from linearly growing KV caches. Non-uniform KV compression preserves more information but introduces systemic challenges (memory fragmentation, scheduling complexity, low kernel utilization) that existing systems cannot handle efficiently.
AI.method: Tangram introduces three core techniques: (1) Deterministic Budget Allocation - assigns static memory per head based on intrinsic patterns, eliminating dynamic scheduling overhead; (2) Head Group Page - clusters attention heads with similar retention needs using independent vectorized page tables for efficient memory reclamation; (3) Ahead-of-Time (AOT) Load Balancing - uses static budget profiles to ensure uniform GPU utilization without runtime overhead.
AI.result: Tangram improves throughput by up to 2.6x compared to existing baselines while fully preserving model accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Non-uniform KV cache compression can be made practical for LLM serving systems by addressing systemic inefficiencies through static budgeting and clustering techniques, achieving significant throughput gains with no accuracy compromise.

title: Unsupervised Skill Discovery for Agentic Data Analysis
summary: Inference-time skill augmentation provides a lightweight way to improve data-analytic agents by injecting reusable procedural knowledge without updating model parameters. However, discovering effective skills for data analysis remains challenging, as reliable supervision is expensive and success criteria vary across analytical formats. This raises the key question of how to discover reusable data-analysis skills from unlabeled exploration alone. We propose DataCOPE, an unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery framework for data-analytic agents. DataCOPE derives verifier signals from the exploration trajectories and uses them to characterize relative quality or aggreement among trajectories. It iteratively coordinates a Data-Analytic Agent for trajectory generation, an Unsupervised Verifier for signal extraction, and a Skill Manager for contrastive skill distillation. For report-style analysis, we instantiate the verifier as an Adaptive Checklist Verifier that derives task-specific criteria, scores reports by verifiable coverage, and iteratively refines the checklist. For reasoning-style analysis, we instantiate it as an Answer Agreement Verifier that groups trajectories by answer agreement and uses self-consistency as an auxiliary signal. We evaluate DataCOPE on report-style analysis from Deep Data Research and reasoning-style analysis from DABStep. Across both settings, DataCOPE consistently improves held-out performance over baselines. Averaged across four model settings, DataCOPE improves the mean score by 9.71% and 32.30% on report-style and reasoning-style tasks respectively.

AI.tldr: DataCOPE is an unsupervised framework that discovers reusable data-analysis skills from unlabeled exploration trajectories using verifier-guided contrastive learning, improving downstream performance by 9.71% on report-style tasks and 32.30% on reasoning-style tasks without model parameter updates.
