AI.motivation: Inference-time skill augmentation can improve data-analytic agents without updating parameters, but discovering effective skills for data analysis is challenging due to expensive supervision and varying success criteria across different analytical formats.
AI.method: DataCOPE coordinates three components: a Data-Analytic Agent for trajectory generation, an Unsupervised Verifier (Adaptive Checklist Verifier for report-style or Answer Agreement Verifier for reasoning-style) for signal extraction, and a Skill Manager for contrastive skill distillation from unlabeled exploration.
AI.result: DataCOPE improves mean scores by 9.71% on report-style tasks (Deep Data Research) and 32.30% on reasoning-style tasks (DABStep) averaged across four model settings, consistently outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: Unsupervised verifier-guided skill discovery is effective for improving data-analytic agents across different analytical formats without requiring labeled data.

title: Plug-and-Play Guidance for Discrete Diffusion Models via Gradient-Informed Logit Correction
summary: Controllable generation with discrete diffusion models is often hindered by high computational overhead or the need for retraining. In this paper, we present \underline{\textbf{G}}radient-\underline{\textbf{I}}nformed \underline{\textbf{L}}ogit \underline{\textbf{C}}orrection (\textbf{GILC}), a plug-and-play framework that efficiently estimates guidance signals by repurposing the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy. To circumvent the gradient instability inherent in high-dimensional discrete spaces, we introduce a Jacobian-free mechanism that directly corrects the clean prediction logits, facilitating stable and effective guidance. Our method accommodates both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions. Extensive experiments across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks demonstrate that GILC achieves state-of-the-art performance without additional training, frequently outperforming fine-tuning approaches.

AI.tldr: GILC is a plug-and-play framework for controllable discrete diffusion generation that uses the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy with a Jacobian-free mechanism to correct logits, achieving SOTA results across DNA, protein, and molecular generation without retraining.
AI.motivation: Controllable generation with discrete diffusion models suffers from high computational overhead or requires retraining, motivating a more efficient plug-and-play approach.
AI.method: GILC repurposes the pretrained denoising network as a variational proxy and introduces a Jacobian-free mechanism to correct clean prediction logits, avoiding gradient instability in high-dimensional discrete spaces.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across DNA, protein sequence, and molecular generation tasks without additional training, often outperforming fine-tuning approaches.
AI.conclusion: GILC provides an efficient and effective plug-and-play solution for controllable discrete diffusion generation, eliminating the need for retraining while handling both differentiable and non-differentiable reward functions.

title: Agent Memory: Characterization and System Implications of Stateful Long-Horizon Workloads
summary: LLM agents are increasingly deployed on long-horizon tasks requiring sustained reasoning over extended interaction histories. Realizing this at scale requires agents to persistently store, retrieve, and update their own memory across sessions. A rich ecosystem of agent memory systems has emerged spanning flat retrieval, LLM-mediated extraction, consolidating fact stores, and agentic control flows. Yet, their system-level behavior remains uncharacterized. We present the first systems characterization of agent memory. First, we introduce a system-oriented taxonomy classifying agent memory systems along four axes. Second, we build a phase-aware profiling harness attributing cost to construction, retrieval, and generation. Third, we characterize ten representative systems across two benchmark suites, uncovering how design choices shift cost across the write and read paths. Finally, we derive 10 system recommendations covering construction scheduling, capability floors, amortization via query volume, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first systems-level characterization of agent memory systems for LLM agents, introducing a taxonomy, profiling harness, and empirical analysis of ten systems across two benchmarks, yielding ten actionable system design recommendations.
AI.motivation: LLM agents deployed on long-horizon tasks need persistent memory across sessions, but the system-level behavior of existing memory systems is uncharacterized, leading to unclear design tradeoffs.
AI.method: The authors introduce a four-axis taxonomy for agent memory systems, build a phase-aware profiling harness that attributes costs to construction/retrieval/generation, and characterize ten representative systems across two benchmark suites.
AI.result: The analysis uncovers how different design choices shift cost across write and read paths, and identifies key tradeoffs such as freshness-latency tradeoffs and the impact of query volume on amortization.
AI.conclusion: The paper derives ten system recommendations covering construction scheduling, capability floors, amortization via query volume, freshness-latency tradeoffs, and fleet-scale management.

title: Learning What to Forget: Improving LLM Unlearning via Learned Token-Level Importance
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model while preserving its general capabilities. For autoregressive language models, not all tokens in a forget sample are equally relevant to forgetting. Existing approaches either ignore this heterogeneity or rely on auxiliary models, heuristics, or external annotations to estimate each token's relevance for forgetting. We instead characterize it through the interaction with the retain objective: a token is forget-specific to the extent that minimizing the forget loss on that token does not conflict with retain optimality. We formalize this perspective as a joint optimization problem over the model parameters and the token weights and show that, under a natural separation condition, the resulting objective recovers the oracle forget-specific token support. Motivated by this formulation, we introduce Alternating Token-Weighted Unlearning (ATWU), a lightweight framework that jointly learns token forget-specificity and model parameters during unlearning using a simple linear scorer over the hidden states, without external token level supervision. Across TOFU and RWKU, ATWU achieves state of the art forget-retain trade-offs, outperforming sample-level methods, probability-based token weighting heuristics, and auxiliary-model-based approaches. Moreover, the learned scores align substantially better with ground truth forget-specific spans, indicating that ATWU identifies semantically meaningful token level forgetting signals. Overall, our results suggest that retain conflict provides an effective criterion for identifying what language models should forget, enabling unsupervised learning of token level forget-specificity directly from model representations with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes ATWU, a lightweight unsupervised framework that jointly learns token-level forget-specificity weights and model parameters during machine unlearning by leveraging retain conflict as a natural criterion, achieving state-of-the-art forget-retain trade-offs without external supervision.
AI.motivation: Existing machine unlearning methods for autoregressive language models treat all tokens in a forget sample equally or rely on auxiliary models, heuristics, or external annotations to estimate token relevance, ignoring the heterogeneity of token importance for forgetting.
AI.method: ATWU formulates unlearning as a joint optimization over model parameters and token weights, where token forget-specificity is measured by how much minimizing the forget loss on that token conflicts with retain optimality. It uses a simple linear scorer over hidden states to learn these weights without external supervision.
AI.result: On TOFU and RWKU benchmarks, ATWU achieves state-of-the-art forget-retain trade-offs, outperforming sample-level methods, probability-based heuristics, and auxiliary-model-based approaches. Learned scores align well with ground-truth forget-specific spans.
AI.conclusion: Retain conflict serves as an effective criterion for identifying what language models should forget, enabling unsupervised learning of token-level forget-specificity directly from model representations with minimal computational overhead.

title: Vortex: Efficient and Programmable Sparse Attention Serving for AI Agents
summary: Sparse attention is becoming increasingly important for serving large language models (LLMs) as generation lengths continue to grow. However, deploying and evaluating new sparse attention algorithms at scale remains highly engineering-intensive, slowing both human researchers and AI agents in exploring the sparse attention design. To address this challenge, we present Vortex, a system that combines a Python-embedded frontend language atop a page-centric tensor abstraction for expressing a broad range of sparse attention algorithms, with an efficient backend tightly integrated into modern LLM serving stacks. Vortex enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms, effectively translating their theoretical efficiency gains into real-world throughput improvements. As a result, Vortex substantially accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms. First, AI agents use Vortex to automatically generate and refine diverse algorithms, the best reaching up to $3.46\times$ higher throughput than full attention while preserving accuracy. Second, Vortex extends sparse attention to emerging architectures and very large models that are otherwise hard to experiment with, reaching up to $4.7\times$ higher throughput on the MLA-based GLM-4.7-Flash and $1.37\times$ on the 229B-parameter MiniMax-M2.7 on NVIDIA B200 GPUs.

AI.tldr: Vortex is a system with a Python-embedded frontend and page-centric tensor abstraction that enables rapid prototyping, deployment, and evaluation of sparse attention algorithms for LLMs, achieving up to 4.7× throughput improvements over full attention.
AI.motivation: Deploying and evaluating new sparse attention algorithms at scale is highly engineering-intensive, slowing both human researchers and AI agents in exploring sparse attention designs for LLMs with growing generation lengths.
AI.method: Vortex combines a Python-embedded frontend language with a page-centric tensor abstraction for expressing sparse attention algorithms, paired with an efficient backend integrated into modern LLM serving stacks.
AI.result: AI agents using Vortex achieved up to 3.46× higher throughput than full attention. Vortex reached up to 4.7× higher throughput on MLA-based GLM-4.7-Flash and 1.37× on the 229B-parameter MiniMax-M2.7 on NVIDIA B200 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: Vortex substantially accelerates the design and iteration of sparse attention algorithms, enabling both AI agents and human researchers to rapidly prototype and deploy efficient sparse attention that translates theoretical gains into practical throughput improvements.

title: PAMF: Prior-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Incomplete Time Series Data
summary: In healthcare, multimodal time series tasks often operate on incomplete observations in practice, for example when ECG segments are lost because electrodes detach or an entire respiratory channel is unavailable during overnight monitoring. Such missingness typically appears in two structurally distinct patterns: within-modality missing, where values are absent within an otherwise observed modality, and modality-level missing, where an entire modality is unavailable. Existing methods typically represent unobserved data implicitly through masks or missing embeddings, without learning instance-specific missing information, and most are designed for only one missingness pattern. A natural approach is to explicitly estimate the missing data; however, existing imputation methods treat missingness uniformly despite their different structural priors, and the imputation process is often isolated from downstream tasks, preventing downstream tasks from guiding imputation toward more informative representations. To address these limitations, we present PAMF, a multimodal time-series framework that explicitly handles different missingness patterns while coupling imputation with downstream prediction through prior-aware flow matching and weight sharing. Specifically, the method initializes the flow-matching source state with type-specific priors to distinguish two missing types. It further connects imputation and classification through architecturally matched encoders with weight sharing, transferring task-relevant representations into the imputation process. Experiments on multiple multimodal healthcare time-series benchmarks show that the proposed method achieves the strongest overall downstream performance across diverse datasets and missing settings compared with existing baselines.

AI.tldr: PAMF is a multimodal time-series framework that handles two distinct missingness patterns (within-modality and modality-level) by explicitly imputing missing data using prior-aware flow matching and weight sharing between imputation and classification, achieving superior performance on healthcare benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing methods treat missing data implicitly via masks or embeddings, fail to distinguish between within-modality missing vs. modality-level missing, and isolate imputation from downstream tasks, preventing task-guided informative representation learning.
AI.method: PAMF uses prior-aware flow matching to initialize source states differently for each missing type, and connects imputation and classification via weight-shared architecturally matched encoders, enabling downstream task guidance during imputation.
AI.result: The method achieves the strongest overall downstream performance across diverse multimodal healthcare time-series datasets and missingness settings compared to existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly handling different missingness patterns with task-coupled imputation improves downstream task performance in multimodal healthcare time-series analysis.

title: Benchmark Everything Everywhere All at Once
summary: Benchmarks are fundamental for evaluating and advancing LLMs and MLLMs by providing standardized and explicit measures of performance. However, their construction is labor-intensive and hard to reuse, raising concerns about sustainability and scalability. Moreover, existing benchmarks often quickly reach performance saturation after their release, resulting in insufficient discrimination among state-of-the-art models. To address these challenges, we introduce Benchmark Agent, a fully autonomous agentic system designed for benchmark building. Our framework orchestrates the complete benchmark construction pipeline, from user query analysis and subtask design to data annotation and quality control. To assess Benchmark Agent, we implement it to produce 15 representative benchmarks, spanning diverse evaluation scenarios, including text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning. Extensive experiments, including human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge assessment, and consistency checks, demonstrate Benchmark Agent can generate high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement. More importantly, through continual evaluation, we observe several insightful findings, including that current models struggle with certain domain-specific reasoning tasks. We believe that rapidly evolving benchmarks can contribute significantly to the research community. The preview and code will be publicly available at the demo page and code repository.

AI.tldr: Benchmark Agent is a fully autonomous system that builds high-quality benchmarks with minimal human involvement, addressing scalability and saturation issues in existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional benchmark construction is labor-intensive, hard to reuse, and quickly becomes saturated (models achieve near-perfect scores), making it difficult to distinguish between state-of-the-art models.
AI.method: A fully autonomous agentic system that orchestrates the complete benchmark construction pipeline: user query analysis, subtask design, data annotation, and quality control.
AI.result: Produced 15 representative benchmarks across text understanding, multimodal understanding, and domain-specific reasoning. Evaluated via human evaluation, LLM-as-a-judge, and consistency checks, showing high-quality benchmark samples with minimal human involvement.
AI.conclusion: Current models struggle with certain domain-specific reasoning tasks. Rapidly evolving benchmarks can significantly benefit the research community by avoiding saturation issues.

title: Efficient Mean Curvature Computation on High-Dimensional Data Manifolds
summary: Estimating local mean curvature at each point of a high-dimensional dataset is a key ingredient of geometry-aware machine learning algorithms, such as the Mean Curvature Boundary Points (MCBP) method. The naive implementation of this computation, based on a local shape operator approximated from k-nearest neighbor patches, involves an explicit construction of a matrix $H$ whose trace form yields an $O(m^4)$ cost per point, rendering the approach intractable for datasets with more than a few dozen features. This paper introduces two complementary contributions that together reduce this cost by several orders of magnitude. The first contribution is an exact algebraic identity. This identity, derived from the orthogonality of the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix and the cyclicity of the trace operator, eliminates $H$ entirely and reduces the per-point cost to $O(m^2)$ after the eigendecomposition. The second contribution addresses the remaining $O(m^3)$ bottleneck of the full eigendecomposition. Since the local covariance matrix has rank at most $k-1 \ll m$, we replace it with a truncated SVD of the $k \times m$ centered data matrix, an $O(k^2 m)$ operation, and derive an analytical approximation for the contribution of the null-space eigenvectors based on the expected value of their outer product under the Haar measure. The resulting estimator has total cost $O(k^2 m + k m p^2)$, where $p = k-1$. Experiments on real-world datasets confirm speedups of 50 to 300 times relative to the original implementation, with negligible loss when the fast estimator is used to replace the original version. By providing a scalable and data-driven estimate of local curvature, the proposed method establishes curvature as a practical geometric feature for a broad range of machine learning tasks, from classical to modern deep learning pipelines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces two contributions to efficiently estimate local mean curvature in high-dimensional datasets: (1) an algebraic identity reducing per-point cost from O(m⁴) to O(m²), and (2) a truncated SVD-based approximation replacing full eigendecomposition, achieving total cost O(k²m + kmp²) with 50-300x speedups on real data.
AI.motivation: Estimating local mean curvature is crucial for geometry-aware ML algorithms like MCBP, but the naive O(m⁴) per-point cost makes it intractable for high-dimensional data (m > ~50 features).
AI.method: First, derives an exact algebraic identity using covariance eigenvector orthogonality and trace cyclicity to eliminate the H matrix (O(m⁴) → O(m²)). Second, replaces full eigendecomposition (O(m³)) with truncated SVD of the k×m centered data matrix (O(k²m)), approximating null-space eigenvector contributions via Haar measure expected values.
AI.result: Achieves total cost O(k²m + kmp²) where p=k-1. Experiments show 50-300x speedups over original implementation on real-world datasets with negligible accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: Makes local curvature estimation practical for high-dimensional data, enabling curvature as a scalable geometric feature for classical ML to deep learning pipelines.

title: Goedel-Architect: Streamlining Formal Theorem Proving with Blueprint Generation and Refinement
summary: We introduce Goedel-Architect, an agentic framework for formal theorem proving in Lean 4 centered on blueprint generation and refinement. A blueprint is a dependency graph of definitions and lemmas that builds up to the main theorem. First, Goedel-Architect generates a blueprint of formally stated definitions and lemmas, along with declared dependencies. This blueprint is optionally guided by a natural language proof. Then, a tool-equipped Lean prover component closes each open lemma node in parallel using relevant dependencies. Failed lemmas in turn drive refinement of the global blueprint. This strategy contrasts with other mainstream approaches which use recursive lemma decomposition, and can inefficiently loop on dead-end strategies. Using the open-weight DeepSeek-V4-Flash (284B-A13B) as the backbone, Goedel-Architect attains 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test and 75.6% pass@1 on PutnamBench. With an optional natural-language proof seeding the initial blueprint on the harder problems, we additionally close the remaining two MiniF2F-test problems (reaching 100%), lift PutnamBench to 88.8% (597/672), and solve 4/6 on IMO 2025, 11/12 on Putnam 2025, and 3/6 on USAMO 2026. This represents state-of-the-art performance for an open-source pipeline at a price point up to 500x less than comparable open-source pipelines.

AI.tldr: Goedel-Architect is a Lean 4 theorem-proving agentic framework that generates and refines blueprints (dependency graphs of lemmas), achieving SOTA results on MiniF2F (99.2%), PutnamBench (75.6%), and IMO/USAMO problems at 500x lower cost than comparable systems.
AI.motivation: Existing formal theorem proving approaches use recursive lemma decomposition, which can inefficiently loop on dead-end strategies. The authors aim to create a more efficient, parallelizable, and globally coherent approach using blueprint generation and refinement.
AI.method: 1) Generate a blueprint (dependency graph of formally stated lemmas/definitions) optionally guided by a natural language proof. 2) Use a tool-equipped Lean prover to close each open lemma node in parallel. 3) Failed lemmas drive iterative refinement of the global blueprint.
AI.result: 99.2% pass@1 on MiniF2F-test, 75.6% on PutnamBench with open-weight model. With NL proof seeding: 100% MiniF2F, 88.8% PutnamBench, 4/6 IMO 2025, 11/12 Putnam 2025, 3/6 USAMO 2026. Achieved at 500x lower cost than comparable pipelines.
AI.conclusion: The blueprint-based agentic framework with parallel lemma closure and iterative refinement is a highly efficient and effective approach for formal theorem proving, achieving SOTA results at a fraction of the computational cost.

title: Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders for Effective Mechanistic Interpretability
summary: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are widely used for mechanistic interpretability in large language models, yet their formulation assigns each latent feature a single decoder direction, implicitly assuming features to be one-dimensional. We show that this assumption mismatches with the multi-dimensional structure of model features, provably inducing feature splitting through two distinct mechanisms. Geometrically, reconstructing a feature of intrinsic dimension $d_i \ge 2$ to error $\varepsilon$ with single-direction decoders forces a number of atoms that is exponential in $d_i$. From an end-to-end optimization perspective, this splitting is not merely possible but actively preferred. We prove that there exists a continuous path from the true $d_i$-dimensional basis to a strictly lower risk of the $\ell_1$-regularized SAE objective, whose descent directions drive any trained dictionary into that exponential regime. A single coherent feature is therefore fragmented across many near-collinear latents, producing spurious multiplicity and obscuring the intrinsic geometry. Motivated by this, we introduce Subspace-Aware Sparse Autoencoders (SASA), which replace single-vector decoders with learned decoder subspaces, enforce block sparsity via Top-$s$ group gating, and adapt each group's effective rank with a nuclear-norm regularizer. We then show that once the block size satisfies $r \ge d_i$, a single group not only can represent the entire feature slice but is the global minimizer of the SASA objective. This consolidation yields a sample complexity polynomial in $d_i$ rather than exponential -- a decisive advantage given that every training activation costs an LLM forward pass. Empirically, on GPT-2 and Mistral-7B, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches or exceeds standard SAEs while training on roughly half the token budget.

AI.tldr: Standard Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) incorrectly assume features are one-dimensional, causing exponential feature splitting. The authors propose Subspace-Aware SAEs (SASA) using learned decoder subspaces and block sparsity, which consolidate multi-dimensional features, improve interpretability, and match SAE performance with half the training tokens.
AI.motivation: SAEs assign each latent feature a single decoder direction, assuming features are one-dimensional. This mismatches the multi-dimensional structure of model features, causing problematic feature splitting.
AI.method: The authors (1) theoretically prove that single-direction decoders force exponential splitting for multi-dimensional features, (2) show the SAE objective actively prefers this splitting, and (3) introduce SASA: replacing single-vector decoders with learned subspaces, enforcing block sparsity via Top-s group gating, and using nuclear-norm regularization to adapt group rank.
AI.result: On GPT-2 and Mistral-7B, SASA reduces feature splitting and absorption, improves monosemanticity and interpretability, and matches/exceeds standard SAEs while training on roughly half the token budget.
AI.conclusion: SASA demonstrates that recognizing and modeling the intrinsic multi-dimensional structure of features leads to more compact, interpretable, and sample-efficient sparse representations in LLMs.

title: MLEvolve: A Self-Evolving Framework for Automated Machine Learning Algorithm Discovery
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly applied to long-horizon tasks such as scientific discovery and machine learning engineering (MLE), where sustained self-evolution becomes a key capability. However, existing MLE agents suffer from inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, which together hinder long-horizon optimization. We present MLEvolve, an LLM-based self-evolving multi-agent framework for end-to-end machine learning algorithm discovery. By extending tree search to Progressive MCGS, MLEvolve enables cross-branch information flow through graph-based reference edges and gradually shifts the search from broad exploration to focused exploitation with an entropy-inspired progressive schedule. To allow the agent to evolve with accumulated experience, we introduce Retrospective Memory, which combines a cold-start domain knowledge base with a dynamic global memory for task-specific experience retrieval and reuse. For stable long-horizon iteration, we further decouple strategic planning from code generation with adaptive coding modes. Evaluation on MLE-Bench shows that MLEvolve achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple dimensions including average medal rate and valid submission rate under a 12-hour budget (half the standard runtime). Moreover, MLEvolve also outperforms specialized algorithm discovery methods including AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks, demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at https://github.com/InternScience/MLEvolve.

AI.tldr: MLEvolve is an LLM-based multi-agent framework for machine learning algorithm discovery that uses Progressive MCGS (graph-based tree search with cross-branch information flow), Retrospective Memory (cold-start knowledge base + dynamic global memory), and decoupled strategic planning from code generation. It achieves state-of-the-art on MLE-Bench under half the standard runtime and outperforms specialized methods like AlphaEvolve on mathematical optimization tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing MLE agents suffer from inter-branch information isolation, memoryless search, and lack of hierarchical control, which hinder long-horizon optimization in self-evolving LLM agents.
AI.method: 1) Progressive MCGS: extends tree search with graph-based cross-branch reference edges and entropy-inspired progressive schedule shifting from exploration to exploitation. 2) Retrospective Memory: combines cold-start domain knowledge base with dynamic global memory for experience retrieval/reuse. 3) Decoupled strategic planning from code generation with adaptive coding modes.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on MLE-Bench (average medal rate, valid submission rate) under 12-hour budget (half standard runtime). Also outperforms AlphaEvolve on mathematical algorithm optimization tasks.
AI.conclusion: MLEvolve effectively enables self-evolving LLM agents for long-horizon machine learning algorithm discovery with strong cross-domain generalization.

title: Quantifying the Privacy of Counterfactuals by Leveraging Membership Inference Attacks Against Synthetic Data
summary: Counterfactuals are typically used in high-stakes decision areas to explain a machine learning model by showing how changes to the user profiles result in the desired outcome. However, explaining the model's decisions through counterfactuals can also be exploited by an adversary to conduct privacy attacks against the model or its training data. Drawing on the analogy that counterfactuals provide realistic substitutes for real training data, similar to synthetic data, we demonstrate in this paper how it is possible to successfully perform privacy attacks on counterfactuals by drawing on the attacks developed against synthetic data. More precisely, we investigate the effectiveness of the membership inference attacks designed for synthetic data on various types of counterfactuals. Additionally, while existing membership inference attacks against counterfactuals usually require to be able to query the model, we show how it is possible to perform successful membership inference attacks using only a set of counterfactuals, with no access to the model from which they are generated. Our results demonstrate that model developers should be more cautious when releasing counterfactuals to various users, as it can lead to a privacy breach.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that counterfactual explanations, like synthetic data, can be exploited for membership inference attacks, even without access to the original model - only the counterfactuals themselves are needed.
AI.motivation: Counterfactuals are widely used for explaining model decisions in high-stakes areas, but their potential privacy risks (being exploited for attacks against the model or training data) are not well understood.
AI.method: The authors draw an analogy between counterfactuals and synthetic data, then apply membership inference attacks originally designed for synthetic data to counterfactuals. They also propose attacks that require only the counterfactuals, without querying the original model.
AI.result: Membership inference attacks designed for synthetic data are effective on counterfactuals. Successful attacks can be performed using only counterfactuals, with no model access needed.
AI.conclusion: Model developers should exercise greater caution when releasing counterfactuals, as they pose privacy breach risks similar to synthetic data.

title: Bridging Domain Expertise and Generalization for Performance Estimation
summary: Performance estimation under distribution shift aims to predict how a model behaves on an unlabeled test set whose distribution differs from the training data, a scenario that requires reliable indicators that can faithfully reflect model behavior without ground-truth labels. Existing approaches rely solely on the outputs of the given model whose biases are amplified once the distribution shifts, weakening the correlation with the true performance. Motivated by this limitation, we propose Fused Reference Alignment Prediction (FRAP), which leverages the complementary strengths of an external foundation model and the base model to construct a more reliable surrogate of the ground-truth labels. FRAP aligns the prediction distribution of the foundation model with that of the base model by applying temperature-scaled calibration that minimizes their divergence. The aligned predictions are fused through confidence-based weighting into a refined reference distribution that integrates robustness from the foundation model and domain-specific expertise from the base model, and performance estimation is obtained by measuring how closely the base model predictions agree with this reference. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and architectures show that FRAP provides consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: FRAP improves performance estimation under distribution shift by fusing an external foundation model with the base model via temperature-scaled calibration and confidence-based weighting, creating a reliable reference distribution for agreement measurement.
AI.motivation: Existing performance estimation methods under distribution shift rely solely on the base model's outputs, whose biases are amplified under shift, weakening correlation with true performance.
AI.method: FRAP aligns foundation model predictions with base model predictions via temperature-scaled calibration, then fuses them through confidence-based weighting into a refined reference distribution, and estimates performance by measuring agreement between base model predictions and this reference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and architectures show consistent and substantial improvements over representative performance-estimation methods under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging complementary strengths of a foundation model and base model provides a more reliable surrogate for ground-truth labels, improving performance estimation under distribution shift.

title: Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation
summary: Probabilistic inference over spatially embedded variables requires beliefs that respect $SE(3)$ symmetry, yet existing equivariant networks produce only scalars and vectors -- not the rank-2 precision tensors needed for anisotropic uncertainty, and single-component messages collapse multi-modal energy landscapes to physically meaningless averages. We introduce Equivariant Neural Belief Propagation (ENBP), a factor-graph framework whose messages are equivariant Gaussian mixture models with sufficient statistics that transform exactly under $SE(3)$. Rank-2 precision matrices are synthesised via equivariant outer products, ingested through differentiable spectral decomposition, and kept tractable by a greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with $SE(3)$. On GEOM-QM9 and GEOM-Drugs, ENBP achieves 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090 $\mathring{A}$ error with sub-second latency -- over $100\times$ faster than diffusion baselines at higher accuracy. On multi-body robotic inference, vanilla loopy BP diverges at 15+ agents while ENBP converges with near-zero collision rates and machine-precision equivariance error (${\sim}10^{-7}$ vs.\ $10^{-1}$ for augmented baselines).

AI.tldr: ENBP introduces a factor-graph framework with SE(3)-equivariant Gaussian mixture messages, achieving sub-second conformational coverage at 98.9% accuracy on molecular benchmarks and stable multi-agent robotic inference where standard methods fail.
AI.motivation: Existing equivariant networks cannot represent rank-2 precision tensors needed for anisotropic uncertainty, and single-component messages collapse multi-modal energy landscapes to physically meaningless averages, motivating a need for richer probabilistic representations that respect SE(3) symmetry.
AI.method: ENBP uses factor graphs with equivariant Gaussian mixture messages; rank-2 precision matrices are constructed via equivariant outer products, processed through differentiable spectral decomposition, and kept tractable via greedy KL-based mixture reduction that provably commutes with SE(3).
AI.result: On GEOM-QM9/GEOM-Drugs: 98.9% conformational coverage at 0.090Å error, sub-second latency, 100× faster than diffusion baselines. On multi-body robotic inference: converges where vanilla loopy BP diverges (15+ agents), near-zero collision rates, machine-precision equivariance (~10⁻⁷ vs 10⁻¹ for augmented baselines).
AI.conclusion: ENBP demonstrates that incorporating SE(3)-equivariant Gaussian mixture messages into factor-graph inference enables both high-accuracy molecular conformation generation and stable multi-agent probabilistic inference, vastly outperforming both diffusion models and standard loopy BP.

title: Performance Evaluation of GraphCast for Medium-Range Weather Forecasting over Brazil
summary: The paradigm of global weather forecasting is rapidly shifting with the emergence of Machine Learning Weather Prediction models (MLWP). While these data-driven architectures demonstrate remarkable global skill, regional benchmarks in the Global South remain scarce, leaving their efficacy in complex, highly convective environments largely unverified. This study evaluates the performance of GraphCast operational against the deterministic ECMWF IFS HRES as baseline across four distinct Brazilian climatic sub-regions. Utilizing a scalable, cloud-native pipeline and the WeatherBench-X framework for benchmarking weather models, we assess selected tropospheric variables ($T_{850}$, $Q_{850}$, $Z_{500}$) over four selected seasonal windows, employing the operational IFS analysis as the ground truth to calculate the statistical metrics for both models. Results reveal a regime-dependent skill profile. During the austral winter, GraphCast underperforms in the medium range (lead days 2-7) for $Z_{500}$ when resolving fast-propagating baroclinic systems over southern Brazil, but regains an advantage in the extended range, where its inherent smoothing of chaotic small-scale variability becomes beneficial under deterministic skill metrics. Conversely, during the austral summer wet season, GraphCast accurately captures large-scale moisture transport while intrinsically dampening the high-frequency convective variability that degrades deterministic NWP temperature forecasts. These findings establish a baseline for Brazil and define the specific physical boundaries that will guide future ``tropicalization'' efforts, aiming to optimize these foundational AI models for regional resilience.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates GraphCast against ECMWF IFS HRES across four Brazilian climatic regions, finding regime-dependent performance: GraphCast struggles with baroclinic systems in winter medium-range but excels in extended range, while it captures large-scale moisture transport well in summer wet season despite dampening convective variability.
AI.motivation: MLWP models show global skill, but their performance in complex, highly convective environments like the Global South (specifically Brazil) remains unverified due to scarce regional benchmarks.
AI.method: GraphCast operational is compared against ECMWF IFS HRES (baseline) across four Brazilian climatic sub-regions using a cloud-native pipeline and WeatherBench-X framework, evaluating T850, Q850, and Z500 over four seasonal windows with operational IFS analysis as ground truth.
AI.result: GraphCast underperforms in austral winter medium-range (lead days 2-7) for Z500 over southern Brazil baroclinic systems, but regains advantage in extended range. In austral summer wet season, GraphCast captures large-scale moisture transport while dampening high-frequency convective variability.
AI.conclusion: The findings establish a baseline for Brazil and define physical boundaries for future "tropicalization" efforts to optimize AI models for regional resilience.

title: Maximising the Set-Piece Return: Optimising Football Corner Tactics with Graph Reinforcement Learning
summary: Machine learning is increasingly employed for the evaluation of football tactics. However, existing approaches focus on characterising historical actions or analyst-specified counterfactual scenarios. In this work, we seek to go beyond the imitation of historically observed patterns towards discovering new generalisable player configurations and strategies. To tackle this, we focus on optimising corner kick routines, and formulate a decision-making problem in which a central policy makes adjustments to attacking player positions and velocities to maximise first contact shot probability. Unlike classic optimisation that solves for isolated setups, we contribute a reinforcement learning architecture operating on graph-structured data that yields a general policy for adjusting arbitrary starting player positions. Evaluated on over 3,000 Premier League corners, our approach strongly outperforms baseline optimisation techniques under matched inference budgets. Our results suggest that graph reinforcement learning can shift set-piece analysis from historical evaluation and imitation towards reward-driven tactical discovery.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a graph reinforcement learning approach for optimizing corner kick routines in football, moving beyond imitation of historical patterns to discover new, generalizable player configurations that maximize first contact shot probability.
AI.motivation: Existing football tactics evaluation methods focus on characterizing historical actions or analyst-specified counterfactual scenarios, lacking the ability to discover new generalizable player configurations and strategies beyond imitating observed patterns.
AI.method: The authors formulate a decision-making problem for corner kick routines where a central policy adjusts attacking player positions and velocities using a reinforcement learning architecture operating on graph-structured data, yielding a general policy for adjusting arbitrary starting player positions.
AI.result: Evaluated on over 3,000 Premier League corners, the proposed graph reinforcement learning approach strongly outperforms baseline optimization techniques under matched inference budgets.
AI.conclusion: Graph reinforcement learning can shift set-piece analysis from historical evaluation and imitation towards reward-driven tactical discovery.

title: End-to-End Subgraph Detection with GraphDETR
summary: Subgraph detection seeks to identify whether and where instances of query patterns occur within a larger graph. This problem is fundamental across scientific domains and is closely related to subgraph isomorphism, which is NP-complete, limiting combinatorial approaches to small patterns or moderately sized graphs. We introduce GraphDETR, a deep learning framework that formulates subgraph detection as a set prediction problem, analogous to DETR in object detection. GraphDETR encodes the target graph with a graph neural network, and employs a fixed set of learnable query vectors, decoded via a transformer decoder, to predict all pattern occurrences jointly in a single forward pass. This is enabled by training the model end-to-end with bipartite matching. Unlike traditional combinatorial methods that only solve exact structural matching, GraphDETR naturally extends to approximate matching, enabling detection beyond exact pattern correspondence. Empirically, we show that GraphDETR can detect diverse patterns, such as molecular structures, cycles, cliques, and fuzzy patterns of up to 50 nodes, in target graphs with up to 1000 nodes. We further evaluate on molecular functional group detection over the ChEMBL dataset, where GraphDETR predicts the complete set of functional groups per molecule, achieving a strong performance of $\text{AP}_{100} = 91.2$.

AI.tldr: GraphDETR is a deep learning framework that treats subgraph detection as a set prediction problem (similar to DETR for object detection), using GNN encoding and transformer decoding with learnable queries to detect pattern occurrences in graphs, supporting both exact and approximate matching, achieving strong results on patterns up to 50 nodes in graphs up to 1000 nodes and AP₁₀₀=91.2 on ChEMBL functional group detection.
AI.motivation: Subgraph detection is fundamental across scientific domains but is NP-complete (subgraph isomorphism), limiting traditional combinatorial approaches to small patterns or moderate-sized graphs. The authors aim to overcome these scalability and flexibility limitations.
AI.method: GraphDETR formulates subgraph detection as a set prediction problem: it encodes the target graph via a graph neural network, uses fixed learnable query vectors decoded by a transformer decoder, and trains end-to-end with bipartite matching to predict all pattern occurrences in a single forward pass.
AI.result: GraphDETR detects diverse patterns (molecular structures, cycles, cliques, fuzzy patterns) up to 50 nodes in graphs up to 1000 nodes. On ChEMBL molecular functional group detection, it achieves AP₁₀₀ = 91.2.
AI.conclusion: GraphDETR provides an efficient deep learning alternative to combinatorial subgraph detection, with natural support for approximate matching and strong empirical performance on large-scale graphs.

title: Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator for HEC-RAS 2D Water-Surface Elevation Prediction
comment: Preprint manuscript prepared using IEEEtran journal format
summary: This article presents a cross-dataset evaluation of learned native-cell surrogate models for solver-consistent water-surface elevation (WSE) prediction in HEC-RAS 2D. To avoid raster remapping error and information-access confounding, surrogates are evaluated directly on the original nonuniform computational cells under an explicit policy that separates static project inputs, current hydraulic state, project-input forcing, calibration-derived quantities, and future solver-output targets. We introduce the Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator (LRFIO), a no-forcing, increment-based learned surrogate that calibrates an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories and deploys the retained operator through closed-form native-cell rollout. LRFIO evaluates a base-case-first response hierarchy consisting of persistence, global calibrated inertia, and segmented response-field inertia. Segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia are treated as learnable modeling choices, with added complexity retained only when validation evidence justifies its cost. Evaluated across four diverse HEC-RAS 2D benchmarks, LRFIO retains different response structures for different domains, demonstrating adaptive learned complexity. The selector audit shows controlled complexity with a maximum validation regret of 4.30%. During deployment, retained rollout times range from 0.003 s to 0.242 s, and the Beaver Bayou measured-solve comparison gives an estimated 2.75 x 10^4 horizon-normalized speedup over HEC-RAS. These results indicate that the current native-cell increment is a strong solver-conditioned predictive scaffold and that added response-field, neural, or spatial complexity should be retained only when empirically justified.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LRFIO, a learned surrogate model for predicting water-surface elevation in HEC-RAS 2D directly on native nonuniform computational cells. It achieves adaptive complexity across benchmarks with up to 2.75×10⁴ speedup over the solver.
AI.motivation: To avoid raster remapping error and information-access confounding in cross-dataset evaluation of learned surrogates for HEC-RAS 2D WSE prediction, and to develop a method that adaptively selects model complexity only when empirically justified.
AI.method: The paper proposes LRFIO (Learned Response-Field Inertia Operator), a no-forcing, increment-based learned surrogate that calibrates an inertial response operator from solved HEC-RAS trajectories and deploys it via closed-form native-cell rollout. It uses a base-case-first response hierarchy (persistence → global calibrated inertia → segmented response-field inertia) where segmentation, residual correction, and neuralized inertia are retained only when validation evidence justifies their cost.
AI.result: Across four diverse HEC-RAS 2D benchmarks, LRFIO adaptively retains different response structures per domain with a maximum validation regret of 4.30%. Deployment rollout times range from 0.003s to 0.242s, and the Beaver Bayou comparison gives 2.75×10⁴ horizon-normalized speedup over HEC-RAS.
AI.conclusion: The native-cell increment is a strong solver-conditioned predictive scaffold, and added complexity (response-field, neural, or spatial) should only be retained when empirically justified.

title: Proper Scoring Rules for Right-Censored Survival Data
summary: Proper scoring rules provide a rigorous theoretical basis for the training and evaluation of probabilistic forecasts. However, in the presence of right censoring, the event time is only partially observed, rendering conventional scoring rules inapplicable in their standard form. We propose a framework for proper scoring of right-censored survival outcomes based on a simple idea: first, map the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism, then apply the underlying proper score on the induced observed-data law. This yields localized scores for fixed censoring times and marginalized scores when the censoring time is random or only partially observed. The resulting construction recovers familiar right-censored likelihood and IPCW-type criteria within a coherent framework, while also yielding right-censored versions of the CRPS, pinball loss, Brier score, and energy score. We show that the marginalized score is proper under conditional independent censoring and strictly proper on the identifiable region. The same principle also leads to censored engression, a sample-based learning objective for multivariate right-censored survival modeling. In experiments, our scores correctly rank the oracle forecast across several censoring regimes, whereas forecast-dependent plug-in weighted scores can exhibit ranking reversals. Censored engression likewise substantially improves over naive training on censored outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework for proper scoring of right-censored survival outcomes by mapping predictive distributions through the censoring mechanism, recovering familiar criteria and yielding censored versions of popular scores, with proven propriety under conditional independent censoring.
AI.motivation: Proper scoring rules are theoretically sound for probabilistic forecasts but cannot be directly applied when event times are right-censored (partially observed), creating a need for a principled framework to handle censoring in scoring and evaluation.
AI.method: The framework maps the predictive distribution through the censoring mechanism to induce an observed-data law, then applies the underlying proper score. This yields localized scores for fixed censoring times and marginalized scores for random/partially observed censoring times.
AI.result: The construction recovers right-censored likelihood and IPCW-type criteria, produces censored versions of CRPS, pinball loss, Brier score, and energy score. The marginalized score is proper under conditional independent censoring and strictly proper on the identifiable region. Scores correctly rank oracle forecasts across censoring regimes, unlike forecast-dependent plug-in weighted scores.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a principled and effective way to extend proper scoring rules to right-censored survival outcomes, with the censored engression method substantially improving over naive training on censored data.

title: In-Context Multiple Instance Learning
summary: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) addresses problems where supervision is available at the level of bags of instances and has been successfully applied in fields ranging from computational pathology to satellite imagery. Nevertheless, existing algorithms struggle in the low-label regime that characterizes many real-world applications. Flexible models overfit and rigid ones fail to adapt to the task at hand. We show that pretraining an in-context learner with a Perceiver-style architecture on synthetic data yields a model that can solve new tasks from a handful of labeled bags. At inference time, classification happens in a single forward pass and requires no gradient updates. We propose and investigate different synthetic data generators for bag-structured data and find that they capture complementary inductive biases. A model pretrained on a mixture of these generators inherits their per-task strengths and achieves the best average performance across twelve MIL benchmarks, outperforming supervised baselines that require task-specific training.

AI.tldr: A Perceiver-style in-context learner pretrained on synthetic bag data solves MIL tasks from few labeled bags in a single forward pass, outperforming supervised baselines across 12 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing MIL algorithms struggle in low-label regimes—flexible models overfit and rigid ones fail to adapt—yet many real-world applications have limited supervision.
AI.method: Pretrain an in-context learner with a Perceiver-style architecture on synthetic data (mixture of generators) so that at inference, classification requires only a single forward pass with a handful of labeled bags.
AI.result: The mixture-of-generators model achieves the best average performance across 12 MIL benchmarks, outperforming supervised baselines needing task-specific training.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic pretraining with complementary inductive biases enables strong few-shot MIL performance without gradient updates or task-specific retraining.

title: The Post-GCN Decade Revisited: Curvature-Stratified Evaluation of Relational Learning
summary: Current evaluation practices in relational learning rely heavily on flat leaderboards that average performance across heterogeneous datasets, implicitly assuming a uniform underlying structure. We show that this assumption introduces systematic bias: it obscures geometry-dependent performance variations and can lead to misleading conclusions about model generalization. In this work, we identify intrinsic geometry as a key latent factor governing model effectiveness. We demonstrate that conventional aggregated metrics mask critical performance trade-offs that only become visible when datasets are stratified by their geometric properties. To address this issue, we introduce a curvature-stratified evaluation framework that partitions datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. Our benchmark evaluates 18 representative models including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), Graph Foundation Models (GFMs), and tabular learning methods across 14 datasets. We find that model rankings are highly stable within each curvature regime but shift significantly across regimes, indicating that performance is fundamentally geometry-dependent rather than universally transferable. Notably, we identify regimes where GFMs offer diminishing returns compared to geometry-aligned GNNs. Based on these findings, we propose a geometry-aware evaluation protocol that yields more reliable and interpretable comparisons than standard aggregated benchmarks. We release all code, curvature-stratified dataset splits, and evaluation tools to support reproducible and rigorous assessment of future relational learning methods. Code and datasets are provided in our project homepage: https://sirbabbage.github.io/CurvBench_HOME/.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that traditional flat leaderboards in relational learning obscure geometry-dependent performance variations. The authors propose a curvature-stratified evaluation framework, showing model rankings are stable within curvature regimes but shift across them, demonstrating performance is geometry-dependent rather than universally transferable.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation practices in relational learning use flat leaderboards that average performance across heterogeneous datasets, which introduces systematic bias by obscuring geometry-dependent performance variations and leading to misleading conclusions about model generalization.
AI.method: The authors introduce a curvature-stratified evaluation framework that partitions datasets into positive, negative, and near-zero curvature regimes. They evaluate 18 models (GCNs, GFMs, tabular methods) across 14 datasets stratified by geometric properties.
AI.result: Model rankings are highly stable within each curvature regime but shift significantly across regimes. GFMs offer diminishing returns compared to geometry-aligned GNNs in certain regimes.
AI.conclusion: Performance is fundamentally geometry-dependent rather than universally transferable. A geometry-aware evaluation protocol yields more reliable and interpretable comparisons than standard aggregated benchmarks.

title: Double Preconditioning (DoPr): Optimization for Test-Time Performance, not Validation Loss
summary: Many modern applications of deep learning involve training a neural network via a one-step prediction loss (e.g., $L^2$ regression, cross-entropy), but deploy the network by rolling out along its own predictions. Key examples include autoregressive language modeling, flow-based generative modeling, and robot policy learning. It is well-documented that these settings induce a phenomenon we call test-time feedback (TTF): the mismatch between the training/validation loss and downstream metrics of interest, such as task success rate and generation quality, which grows with task length. While data curation, architecture, and objective design have been proposed to combat train-test shift in TTF settings, this paper proposes optimization as a new design axis to mitigate error accumulation. Specifically, we introduce a new optimization paradigm called double-preconditioning (DoPr) uniquely tailored to the challenges of TTF. DoPr combines gradient-wise preconditioning, as in Adam and Muon, with activation-wise preconditioning (AP), such as in KFAC. We show that the addition of AP yields a drop-in intervention for increasing downstream model performance across a range of TTF settings. Interestingly, these gains in test-time performance do not consistently accompany improvements in validation loss, opening new questions about how to properly evaluate models trained with one-step supervised objectives.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes double-preconditioning (DoPr), a new optimization paradigm combining gradient-wise and activation-wise preconditioning, to mitigate test-time feedback (TTF) issues in deep learning where training with one-step prediction loss leads to error accumulation during deployment rollout.
AI.motivation: To address the mismatch between training/validation loss and downstream metrics (test-time feedback) in settings like autoregressive language modeling, flow-based generative models, and robot policy learning, where networks are trained via one-step prediction but deployed via multi-step rollout.
AI.method: Double-preconditioning (DoPr) - a novel optimization paradigm combining gradient-wise preconditioning (as in Adam, Muon) with activation-wise preconditioning (as in KFAC) to mitigate error accumulation in TTF settings.
AI.result: DoPr improves downstream model performance across various TTF settings as a drop-in intervention, but these gains do not consistently correlate with improvements in validation loss.
AI.conclusion: Optimization is a viable new design axis for combating train-test shift in TTF settings; the decoupling of validation loss and downstream performance raises new questions about evaluation protocols for models trained with one-step supervised objectives.

title: Causal Atlases from Entropic Inference: Bayesian Networks beyond Optimal DAGs
summary: Data-driven causal relationship identification is pertinent to advancing understanding of complex systems both within and beyond science. Bayesian networks offer a probabilistic method for modelling generic causal relationships via directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). However, typical techniques for constructing Bayesian networks rely on optimization, which can be ill-suited for learning causal relationships because the underlying data may admit multiple chains of causation. More data-faithful representations of causal relationships would provide frameworks for constructing multiple causal maps that are consistent with the variability that is inherent in underlying data. Here, we show that entropy-based inference generates atlases of plausible causal relationships that are consistent with underlying data. On simulated noisy data of 2- and 20-node linear structural equation models, we sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of graphs that allow us to quantify the inherent structural ambiguity in underlying causal relationships. Our method shows that "optimized" DAGs can contain causal artifacts are not consistent across equivalently accurate topologies.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an entropy-based inference method to generate atlases of multiple plausible causal graphs (DAGs) from data, rather than a single optimized graph, revealing structural ambiguity in causal relationships that optimized DAGs often miss.
AI.motivation: Standard optimization-based Bayesian network methods produce a single "optimal" DAG, which is problematic because underlying data often supports multiple valid causal chains, leading to overconfident or artifact-prone causal conclusions.
AI.method: The authors use entropy-based inference to sample a maximum-entropy ensemble of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) consistent with the data, applied to simulated noisy 2-node and 20-node linear structural equation models.
AI.result: The entropy-based method generates atlases of plausible causal relationships and reveals that optimized DAGs can contain causal artifacts—edges that are not consistent across all equivalently accurate topologies.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-based inference offers a more data-faithful representation of causal relationships by capturing structural ambiguity, providing multiple causal maps instead of a single potentially misleading optimized DAG.

title: Event Detection for Parameter-to-KPI Dependency Learning for AI-RAN
summary: Next-generation wireless networks are expected to rely on multiple concurrent AI-driven control functions that optimize different network objectives simultaneously, particularly in AI-integrated and open radio access network architectures such as AI Radio Access Network (AI-RAN) and Open Radio Access Network (O-RAN). When these functions interact, they can interfere with one another in ways that are difficult to detect from raw network data alone. A key missing piece for managing such interactions is a reliable, interpretable dependency structure that captures which control parameters are actively influencing which network performance outcomes at any given time. This paper focuses on the event-detection step needed to support such dependency learning by converting noisy continuous telemetry into binary indicators of parameter activity and KPI response. The central difficulty is that not every fluctuation in the data reflects a genuine control interaction, so the method must distinguish real parameter-outcome relationships from background variation. Because real AI-RAN traffic traces with known parameter-KPI ground truth are difficult to obtain, we introduce a synthetic closed-loop traffic generator with planted latent dependencies. We use this controlled telemetry to evaluate a machine-learning-based dependency recovery pipeline that formulates the conversion of continuous traces into binary event indicators as a significance-detection problem. Experimental evaluation shows that the proposed pipeline reliably recovers the latent dependency structure from noisy continuous traces when the signal is sufficiently separated from background variation, while highlighting threshold calibration as the key factor controlling event-detection quality. These results constitute a foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to detect events from noisy continuous telemetry in AI-RAN/O-RAN networks, using a synthetic traffic generator with known dependencies to evaluate a machine-learning pipeline that converts continuous data into binary event indicators for interpretable dependency learning.
AI.motivation: Multiple concurrent AI-driven control functions in next-gen wireless networks (AI-RAN, O-RAN) can interfere with each other, but detecting these interactions from raw network data alone is difficult. A reliable, interpretable dependency structure is needed to capture which control parameters influence which performance outcomes.
AI.method: The authors introduce a synthetic closed-loop traffic generator with planted latent dependencies to overcome the lack of real traffic traces with known ground truth. They formulate the conversion of continuous telemetry into binary event indicators as a significance-detection problem and evaluate a machine-learning-based dependency recovery pipeline.
AI.result: The pipeline reliably recovers latent dependency structures from noisy continuous traces when signal is sufficiently separated from background variation. Threshold calibration is identified as the key factor controlling event-detection quality.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundational step toward interpretable dependency learning for adaptive AI-RAN control systems, enabling better management of interacting AI-driven control functions in next-generation wireless networks.

title: PC Layer: Polynomial Weight Preconditioning for Improving LLM Pre-Training
summary: We propose a preconditioning (PC) layer, a weight parameterization via polynomial preconditioner that ensures stable weight conditioning throughout LLM training. The PC module reshapes the singular-value spectrum of weight matrices via low-degree polynomial preconditioning. After training, the preconditioned weights can be merged back into the original architecture, incurring no inference overhead. We demonstrate the advantage of the proposed PC layer over standard transformers in Llama-1B pre-training, for both the AdamW and Muon optimizers. Theoretically, we justify this spectrum-control principle by proving that uniformly bounding each layer's singular values ensures geometric convergence of gradient descent to global minima, for certain deep linear networks. Our code is available at https://github.com/Empath-aln/PC-layer.

AI.tldr: A preconditioning (PC) layer that reparameterizes weight matrices via polynomial preconditioning to stabilize singular-value spectra during LLM training, improving performance without inference overhead.
AI.motivation: To address unstable weight conditioning in LLM training that hinders optimization convergence, by controlling the singular-value spectrum of weight matrices.
AI.method: Introduces a PC layer that uses low-degree polynomial preconditioning to reshape the singular-value spectrum of weight matrices. The preconditioned weights can be merged back post-training, adding no inference cost. Evaluated on Llama-1B with AdamW and Muon optimizers.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved performance over standard transformers in Llama-1B pre-training with both AdamW and Muon optimizers. Theoretically proves geometric convergence to global minima for deep linear networks under uniform singular-value bounds.
AI.conclusion: The PC layer effectively stabilizes training dynamics by controlling singular-value spectra, yields practical gains with no inference overhead, and is supported by theoretical convergence guarantees.

title: RREDCoT: Segment-Level Reward Redistribution for Reasoning Models
summary: Recent advancements in reasoning language models have been driven by Reinforcement Learning (RL) fine-tuning. Most often, these rely on the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm or modifications thereof to steer the models to produce Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces. The final answer can only be verified, and the reward assigned, after the CoT trace is complete, making it a delayed reward problem. GRPO and its modifications correspond to Monte Carlo methods in standard RL, which are known to suffer from high variance. A possible solution to this problem is the redistribution of rewards through credit assignment, where segments of the CoT trace that are important for arriving at the desirable solution are emphasized by assigning a higher reward. While Monte Carlo sampling can be used to provide an unbiased estimate of intermediate state values, its computational overhead makes it unsuitable for train-time credit assignment in long contexts at high granularity. We introduce RREDCoT (Reward REDistribution for Chain of Thoughts), which utilizes the model itself to approximate the optimal reward redistribution without additional generation. We investigate the advantages of our method compared to MC sampling and several attribution methods. We further analyze several aspects relevant to the construction of the redistribution such as segmentation of CoT traces and state value estimation.

AI.tldr: RREDCoT proposes a reward redistribution method for Chain-of-Thought reasoning in language models, using the model itself to approximate optimal credit assignment without costly Monte Carlo sampling, addressing the high-variance delayed reward problem in GRPO-based RL fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: GRPO-based RL fine-tuning for reasoning models suffers from high-variance Monte Carlo reward estimation due to delayed rewards (reward only available after full CoT), and existing credit assignment methods are computationally prohibitive for long contexts at high granularity.
AI.method: RREDCoT uses the model itself to approximate optimal reward redistribution without additional generation, avoiding expensive Monte Carlo sampling for intermediate state value estimation.
AI.result: The proposed method shows advantages over Monte Carlo sampling and several attribution methods, with analysis on CoT segmentation and state value estimation aspects.
AI.conclusion: RREDCoT provides an efficient alternative to Monte Carlo-based credit assignment for delayed rewards in reasoning model fine-tuning, leveraging the model's own approximations.

title: Pretraining Recurrent Networks without Recurrence
summary: Training recurrent neural networks (RNNs) requires assigning credit across long sequences of computations. Standard backpropagation through time (BPTT) addresses this problem poorly: it is sequential in time, limiting parallelism, and suffers from vanishing or exploding gradients, making long-range associations difficult to learn. We propose Supervised Memory Training (SMT), a method for training nonlinear RNNs that sidesteps recurrent credit propagation entirely by reducing RNN training to supervised learning on one-step memory transition labels $(m_t, x_{t+1}) \rightarrow m_{t+1}$. SMT acquires these memory labels by training a Transformer-based encoder on a predictive state objective--retaining only information from the past necessary to predict the future. By decoupling what to remember from how to update memory, SMT enables time-parallel RNN training with a stable $O(1)$ length gradient path between any two tokens--without ever unrolling the RNN. We find that SMT outperforms BPTT when pretraining various RNN architectures on tasks like language modeling and pixel sequence modeling. SMT enables nonlinear RNNs to better capture long-range dependencies and train in parallel, potentially unlocking the scaling of models that build temporal abstractions of past experience.

AI.tldr: SMT replaces backpropagation through time for RNNs by training a Transformer to generate memory transition labels, enabling parallel training and stable gradients across long sequences.
AI.motivation: BPTT suffers from sequential training (limited parallelism) and vanishing/exploding gradients, making it hard to learn long-range dependencies in RNNs.
AI.method: SMT decouples what to remember (via Transformer-based encoder trained on predictive state objective) from how to update memory, reducing RNN training to supervised learning on one-step memory transition labels (m_t, x_{t+1}) → m_{t+1}.
AI.result: SMT outperforms BPTT when pretraining various RNN architectures on language modeling and pixel sequence modeling tasks.
AI.conclusion: SMT enables nonlinear RNNs to capture long-range dependencies better and train in parallel (O(1) gradient path), potentially unlocking scaling of temporal abstraction models.

title: Regret Minimization with Adaptive Opponents in Repeated Games
summary: In this paper, we study regret minimization in repeated games with \emph{adaptive} opponents who can respond based on histories of play. The standard metric of \emph{external regret} in online learning is known to fail to capture such adaptivity. To account for players' counterfactual reasoning, we introduce {\tt Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret)}, a game-theoretic metric that measures the difference between the \emph{realized} and the \emph{best-in-hindsight} accumulated utility when all players can \emph{respond} to the history of play. Compared to existing regret notions in this setting, ours is native to repeated game playing, enabling stronger comparators and opponents with fewer constraints, while maintaining the possibility of finding better equilibria when all players minimize it. We first identify necessary conditions for obtaining {\tt RP-Regret} sublinear in time, on the variation of the player's comparator strategies in the regret definition and on the memories of both the comparator and opponents' strategies. We then study additional conditions and provable algorithms to minimize {\tt RP-Regret}, which is by definition \emph{non-convex} in the strategy space. To address this challenge, we propose three algorithms: (i) one based on an optimization oracle, as assumed in some prior work in online non-convex learning; (ii) one that minimizes a convex and \emph{linearized} surrogate of {\tt RP-Regret} at each iteration; (iii) one that directly minimizes {\tt RP-Regret} when opponents change strategies slowly. Furthermore, when all players can run algorithms to minimize the {\tt RP-Regret} (or its linearized variant), certain subgame perfect equilibria of the repeated game can be learned. We also provide experiments showing that minimizing our regret notions can lead to more cooperative solutions with higher utility in games such as Stag-Hunt.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Repeated Policy Regret (RP-Regret) for repeated games with adaptive opponents, proposes algorithms to minimize it under various conditions, and shows that minimizing RP-Regret can lead to better equilibria and cooperative outcomes.
AI.motivation: Standard external regret fails to capture adaptivity of opponents in repeated games. The authors aim to develop a regret metric that accounts for players' counterfactual reasoning and enables learning better equilibria when all players minimize it.
AI.method: Three algorithms are proposed: (i) an optimization oracle-based method; (ii) a convex linearized surrogate minimization approach; (iii) a direct RP-Regret minimization method for slowly-changing opponents. Necessary conditions for sublinear RP-Regret are identified, including constraints on strategy variation and memory.
AI.result: Necessary conditions for sublinear RP-Regret are identified. Algorithms are shown to minimize RP-Regret under certain conditions. When all players minimize RP-Regret (or its linearized variant), subgame perfect equilibria can be learned. Experiments show more cooperative outcomes in Stag-Hunt games.
AI.conclusion: RP-Regret is a native metric for repeated games that supports stronger comparators and fewer opponent constraints. Minimizing it can lead to better equilibria. The non-convex nature of RP-Regret is addressed via three algorithmic approaches with provable guarantees.

title: TailLoR: Protecting Principal Components in Parameter-Efficient Continual Learning
summary: Parameter-efficient finetuning methods based on spectral decomposition have enabled progress in Continual Learning. In this paper we introduce TailLoR, which utilizes the singular bases U and V of the pre-trained weights as a fixed reference frame to learn a low-rank update applied to the singular value matrix. A soft spectral penalty discourages updates aligned with dominant singular directions, reducing interference while routing fine-grained adaptation into the highly flexible, long-tail spectral coordinates.

AI.tldr: TailLoR is a parameter-efficient continual learning method that applies low-rank updates to singular values in a fixed spectral reference frame, using a soft penalty to reduce interference by shifting adaptation to long-tail spectral coordinates.
AI.motivation: To address catastrophic forgetting in continual learning by leveraging spectral decomposition of pre-trained weights for interference reduction.
AI.method: TailLoR fixes the singular bases U and V from pre-trained weights as a reference frame, learns low-rank updates applied to the singular value matrix, and imposes a soft spectral penalty to discourage updates aligned with dominant singular directions, routing adaptation to long-tail spectral coordinates.
AI.result: Enables fine-grained adaptation with reduced interference in continual learning settings.
AI.conclusion: Spectral decomposition-based parameter-efficient finetuning can effectively mitigate forgetting by directing updates to less dominant spectral components.

title: Transformer-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning: Fundamentals and Applications in Communication Networks
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has long been a powerful solution to various problems in communication networks. However, traditional RL models still face with several limitations. Not only do they rely on large numbers of interactions with the environment, but they are also limited in terms of modeling long-term relationships and tackling partial observability. In recent years, the Transformer model has demonstrated the ability to enhance RL models, allowing them to overcome these issues. Particularly, the self-attention mechanism within the Transformer enables efficient modeling of long-range dependencies and global correlations, as well as accelerates training processes and handles heterogeneous data modalities. In this paper, we present a comprehensive survey of Transformer-based RL algorithms and their applications in communication networks. Specifically, the paper provides the mathematical background of RL and Transformer architectures, along with insights into key issues such as resource allocation, computation offloading, routing, and trajectory control, and network security.
